title: More delays in expected Return to Service (RTS) for both Callide C3 and C4
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "More delays in expected Return to Service (RTS) for both Callide C3 and C4" posted at 29-12-2022 03:16:00 PM is: The article discusses the delays in the expected return to service dates for Callide C3 and C4 units, both operated by a joint venture between CS Energy and OzGen. Callide C4 has been offline since an explosion on May 25, 2021, while Callide C3 has been offline since a partial cooling tower failure on October 31, 2022. CS Energy's latest media release adjusts the return to service date for Unit C4 to May 1, 2023, with a phased return to full capacity by October 2023. Unit C3's return has been shifted to May 8, 2023, also with a phased increase in capacity. These delays and the phased return are attributed to transportation delays of critical components and the ongoing assessment of damage by external experts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Potentially extending up to the new expected RTS dates (May to October 2023). Its root cause is: Delays in transporting key components and the need for further assessment and repairs. Its accident scale is: Limited to Callide C3 and C4 units. Its dates are: 29-12-2022 for the news and updated service dates outlined for C3 and C4 (May 1 and 8, 2023, respectively). Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Regional electricity consumers and businesses dependent on stable electricity supply. Its cause type is: Operational delay and structural failure. Its causes are: Explosion impact on Callide C4 and cooling tower structural issues for Callide C3 causing return to service delays.

title: Media buzz about compensation for Gladstone Power Station under the coal price cap
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "Media buzz about compensation for Gladstone Power Station under the coal price cap" posted at 28-12-2022 03:38:00 PM is: The articles discussed the Federal Government's negotiations over compensation related to the coal price cap, focusing on Gladstone Power Station. This complex intervention in the National Electricity Market, particularly during the 2022 energy crisis, involved significant compensation for Rio Tinto and partners. The spotlight on Gladstone highlights the financial dynamics faced by stakeholders amid regulatory changes and market volatility. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: June and July 2022. Its root_cause is: Federal Government's coal price cap negotiations and compensation considerations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: Same date. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and regulatory intervention. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Federal Government's coal price cap, compensation discussions involving Gladstone Power Station and implicated stakeholders.

title: Tis the season … for a ‘bomb cyclone’ in North America that stretches wholesale supplies of electricity (and leaves >1M customers off supply with localised network outages)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tis the season … for a ‘bomb cyclone’ in North America that stretches wholesale supplies of electricity (and leaves >1M customers off supply with localised network outages)" posted on 24-12-2022 10:39:00 PM is: A severe 'bomb cyclone' storm hit North America, causing significant disruptions in electricity supply across the United States and Canada. More than 1.5 million households were left without power due to localized network outages, primarily because of downed distribution lines and stretched electricity supplies. This extreme weather event also led to high demand for electricity, triggering alerts of tight supply conditions from regional transmission organizations like PJM, MISO, and SPP. The intensity of the storm, cited as a once-in-40-years event, prompted widespread power conservation calls, load shedding, and emergency alerts to manage demand-supply imbalances. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, with ongoing impacts as power is restored and systems recover. Its root cause is: Severe winter weather event, known as a 'bomb cyclone,' impacting power infrastructure. Its accident scale is: Over 1.5 million households affected. Its dates are: 24-12-2022. Its affected region is: United States and Canada, with a wide storm affecting extensive areas. Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Natural disaster. Its causes are: Downed power lines due to storm winds, increased electricity demand due to extreme cold, underperformance of wind generation, and subsequent strain on electricity markets and grid operators.

title: ‘NEM Local Temperature Alert’ for Port Augusta on Monday 26th December 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "'NEM Local Temperature Alert' for Port Augusta on Monday 26th December 2022" posted at 22-12-2022 04:05:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a "NEM Local Temperature Alert" for Port Augusta, South Australia, forecasting temperatures of 39°C or above on December 26, 2022. This alert is part of a general notice aimed at advising market participants to review and adjust their generator capacity in response to potential heat impacts. Previous alerts for elevated temperatures were issued for various locations including Port Augusta earlier in December. Despite the alert, demand is expected to remain subdued due to the holiday period, though local generation facilities could be affected. This development is in context with the influence of La Niña weather conditions, which have limited the number of alerts. It relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, pertaining to the specific forecast date. Its root_cause is: High temperature alert as per the weather forecast. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: December 26, 2022. Its affected region is: Port Augusta, South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity Market Participants in the area (e.g., generation units and MNSP converter stations). Its cause type is: Climatic. Its causes is a summarized list of cause events are: High temperatures forecasted to reach or exceed 39°C on December 26, prompting operational review and potential adjustments in generation capacity.

title: How closely monitoring households’ energy data can unleash their solar outputs and (possibly) make them more money
author: Richard Bean
classified_content: The summary of the news "How closely monitoring households’ energy data can unleash their solar outputs and (possibly) make them more money" posted at 22-12-2022 01:09:00 PM is: The article discusses a project in Australia aimed at enhancing the solar energy export capabilities of households by closely monitoring energy data. This initiative seeks to address challenges in energy distribution by enabling network operators to manage voltage fluctuations more precisely and allow dynamic energy export limits. The scheme could potentially increase household earnings from solar exports and expedite the transition to renewable energy sources. The data monitoring involves installing devices in households to collect energy metrics at frequent intervals, generating significant data for analysis by the Australian Renewable Energy Agency's Project SHIELD. This project is seen as beneficial for both consumers and network operators in managing distributed energy resources. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: The need for improved management of the energy distribution network due to increased household solar power generation and the two-way flow of electricity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-12-2022 01:09:00 PM. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Australian households with solar panels. Its cause type is: Operational challenge in energy distribution. Its causes are: Uncertainty and inefficiencies in managing network voltage fluctuations due to household solar energy exports and inadequate visibility into individual household energy data.

title: Discussion Paper released with respect to adding Emissions Reduction into the NEO
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Discussion Paper released with respect to adding Emissions Reduction into the NEO" posted at 20-12-2022 03:46:00 PM is: The article announces the release of a Consultation Paper aimed at incorporating an emissions reduction objective into the national energy objectives (NEO). This has been a long-discussed topic, and the paper invites submissions via email by the 7th of February 2023; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: An initiative to modernize energy policy by including emissions reduction as an objective. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-12-2022. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy reform. Its causes are: Discussion on including emissions reduction in energy objectives, release of a consultation paper, and call for public submissions.

title: Kogan Creek returned to service on Tuesday 20th December 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Kogan Creek returned to service on Tuesday 20th December 2022" posted at 20-12-2022 12:30:00 PM is: Kogan Creek Power Station, after an unexpected extended major overhaul due to stator and rotor issues, was returned to service slightly later than expected on the night of December 20, 2022. The return process included a false start on December 19 and a brief offline period on the evening of December 20; it successfully returned online early morning December 21. The complexity of managing the return after long outages often results in such operational glitches. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: Unexpected stator and rotor issues. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-12-2022 and 21-12-2022. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Extended major overhaul, unexpected stator and rotor issues, false start during return to service.

title: Return to service expectation for Callide C3 is (still) 11th February 2023
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Return to service expectation for Callide C3 is (still) 11th February 2023" posted at 20-12-2022 12:20:00 PM is: The expected return-to-service date for the Callide C3 unit remains set for 11th February 2023, following the cooling tower failure reported previously. This information is consistent with updates noted in November 2022 and reflects the latest data from the 'MT PASA DUID Availability' widget in ez2view; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Likely to affect pricing until the expected return date in February 2023. Its root_cause is: Cooling tower failure at Callide C3. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-12-2022; return to service expected 11th February 2023. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Equipment failure. Its causes are: Equipment failure leading to delayed return to service.

title: Callide C4 generator transformer delivered to Callide Power Station
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 - 25th May - Callide C4 Explosion, Fire and Outage
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C4 generator transformer delivered to Callide Power Station" posted at 20-12-2022 12:11:00 PM is: A significant milestone in the recovery from the Callide C4 Catastrophe, which occurred on 25th May 2021, has been achieved with the arrival of a replacement generator transformer at the Callide Power Station. The transformer was transported from the Port of Gladstone to the power station on the evening of 16th December. The expected return to service date for Unit C4 is projected for April 2023, specifically 7th April, marking a prolonged restoration period following the explosion. The cause of the explosion is still under investigation, with no engineer's report released yet. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Expected until April 2023. Its root_cause is: Generator transformer failure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-12-2022 for the news update and 16-12-2022 for the arrival of the transformer. Its affected region is: Callide Power Station, Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: Explosion, fire, and subsequent outage of the Callide C4 unit.

title: Victorian minimum demand drops lower on Sunday 18th December 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Victorian minimum demand drops lower on Sunday 18th December 2022" posted at 18-12-2022 08:42:00 PM is: Demand for electricity in Victoria hit a record low on Sunday, 18th December 2022, with 'Market Demand' reaching 1,977MW and 'Operational Demand' dropping to 2,195MW, marking a significant reduction compared to previous lows recorded in October. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) confirmed these figures on social media platforms subsequently.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Residential satisfaction. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Unspecified reduction in electricity demand, possibly due to cooler weather conditions or changes in consumption patterns. Accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-12-2022. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: General residential users and electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Demand fluctuation. Its causes are: Lower-than-expected electricity demand in the region.

title: How common are islandings and transmission tower failures in the NEM?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "How common are islandings and transmission tower failures in the NEM?" posted at 16-12-2022 02:47:00 AM is: The article discusses the recent challenges faced by the National Electricity Market (NEM) due to transmission tower failures, highlighting two key incidents in South Australia and Tasmania. In South Australia, thunderstorms and strong winds caused a transmission tower near Tailem Bend to collapse, leading to frequency islanding and significant market costs. In Tasmania, a landslide displaced a tower, affecting the transmission backbone. These incidents underscore the vulnerabilities in the aging transmission network and the impact of extreme weather on system security and electricity prices. Future risks could be mitigated through infrastructure upgrades and better maintenance practices.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (e.g., price spikes and market costs arose quickly after the events). Its root_cause is: Transmission tower failures due to natural events (thunderstorms and landslides). Its accident scale is: Regional (affecting specific areas within the NEM like South Australia and Tasmania). Its dates is: 16-12-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia and Tasmania. Its affected key users is: Regional electricity consumers and system operators in the affected areas. Its cause type is: Natural events. Its causes is: Thunderstorms, strong winds, and a landslide.

title: AER published Wholesale Market Performance Report for 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER published Wholesale Market Performance Report for 2022" posted at 15-12-2022 01:14:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) released its 'Wholesale electricity market performance report' for 2022, detailing methods, assumptions, and results of LCOE and LCOS modelling, along with approaches to economic withholding. The publication is extensive, with accompanying reports highlighting ongoing market reforms required for a transition to net zero emissions. It aims to provide a comprehensive analysis of the electricity market's performance in 2022. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: As part of its annual reporting on electricity market performance, the AER aims to provide a detailed analysis of electricity market dynamics and reforms. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15-12-2022 01:14:00 PM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Analytical report release. Its causes are: The report addresses methods of market performance analysis, economic withholding, and recommendations for market reforms.

title: AER releases ‘June 2022 market events report’
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER releases ‘June 2022 market events report’" posted at 15-12-2022 08:43:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) released a comprehensive report analyzing the events in June 2022, when electricity markets were suspended across Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia, and Victoria due to cumulative price caps being hit. The investigation highlighted the potential recklessness of some generators withdrawing capacity, possibly breaching market rules. Despite the regulatory challenges, it noted cooperation between AEMO and generators. The report identified fuel constraints as a reasonable cause for capacity withdrawals, mitigating accusations of market gaming. Compliance issues, particularly in PASA submissions and rebidding practices, were noted, with investigations ongoing for one generator. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: June 2022. Its root cause is: Generator behavior and market operational issues during extreme price conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12th June 2022 for QLD and 13th June 2022 for NSW, SA, and VIC. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia, and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants within the National Electricity Market. Its cause type is: Operational and market behavior. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Generator capacity withdrawal, potential recklessness in market participation, fuel constraints, and compliance issues in market operations.

title: Price Caps to be implemented on Gas and Coal (perhaps?) … with impacts to be felt in the NEM
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price Caps to be implemented on Gas and Coal (perhaps?) … with impacts to be felt in the NEM" posted at 13-12-2022 09:59:00 PM is: The article discusses the potential implementation of government-imposed price caps on domestic thermal coal and gas in Australia, with Federal legislation possibly being enacted as early as December 15, 2022. The initiative is part of an effort to intervene in the energy market and stabilize rising energy costs. A temporary one-year cap is proposed: $12/GJ for gas and $125/tonne for coal, alongside a mandatory Code of Conduct for the gas market. The intervention is a response to the ongoing 2022 Energy Crisis, aimed at providing relief for commercial and residential energy users suffering from increased costs. The article also reviews various media coverage and the reactions from industry organizations towards these proposed changes.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Fuel prices. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: One year, beginning potentially in late 2022 or early 2023. Its root_cause is: Rising energy costs due to the 2022 Energy Crisis and the need to stabilize the electricity market. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-12-2022, with consultation deadlines on 9th, 13th, and 15th December 2022, and February 7, 2023. Its affected region is: Australia, particularly in the National Electricity Market (NEM) regions. Its affected key users are: Commercial and residential energy users. Its cause type is: Government intervention in the energy market. Its causes are: High energy prices, government legislation, economic instability in the energy sector.

title: ACCC released its November 2022 update of the ‘Inquiry into the National Electricity Market’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "ACCC released its November 2022 update of the ‘Inquiry into the National Electricity Market’" posted at 13-12-2022 07:10:00 PM is: The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) has released its November 2022 report on the National Electricity Market, addressing the challenges posed by elevated wholesale electricity prices. The report highlights the exit of six electricity retailers since May 2022 and the pressures on smaller retailers due to increased hedging costs. The report contains recommendations to enhance market competition, including flexible default retail pricing and a mandatory code of conduct for energy price comparison sites. The ACCC stresses the importance of competitive markets for investments in a renewables-based grid; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing impact. Its root cause is: Elevated wholesale electricity prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-11-2022 (report date) and 08-12-2022 (release date). Its affected region is: Australia (specifically the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: Electricity retailers and consumers. Its cause type is: Market and regulatory issues. Its causes are: Increased wholesale electricity prices, exit of retailers, pressure on hedging costs, need for competition and regulatory adjustment.

title: AEMO reports on (and webinar about) System Strength, Inertia and NSCAS
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO reports on (and webinar about) System Strength, Inertia and NSCAS" posted at 13-12-2022 05:34:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) released three reports on December 1st, 2022, focusing on system security planning, particularly regarding system strength, inertia, and Network Support and Control Ancillary Services (NSCAS). These reports highlight the importance of maintaining a stable voltage waveform and steady electrical frequency, which are critical for a functioning grid amidst increasing reliance on renewable energy. The reports, forming part of the broader 'Engineering Roadmap to 100% Renewables', address the challenges in managing grid stability and security in a transitioning energy market.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, as it relates to strategic planning for future grid stability. Its root_cause is: The need to adapt the grid for increased renewable energy integration. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 1st December 2022 for the report release date; 13th December 2022 for the news post date. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Grid operators and energy policymakers. Its cause type is: Strategic planning requirement. Its causes are: Transition to renewable energy necessitating enhanced system strength and inertia to ensure grid stability.

title: The Capacity Market is dead … long live the ‘Capacity Investment Scheme’?!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Capacity Market is dead … long live the ‘Capacity Investment Scheme’?!" posted at 13-12-2022 12:20:00 PM is: The Australian Government, with support from state and territory Energy Ministers, is implementing a new 'Capacity Investment Scheme' to ensure a stable electricity supply as the energy generation mix shifts towards renewables. This policy aims to move away from coal and gas reliance, a continuation of discussions that have been ongoing since 2015. Recent media coverage highlights varied industry and public reactions to this shift, emphasizing the complexities and challenges in transitioning to green capacity mechanisms. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, as it focuses on structural changes in energy policy and infrastructure. Its root_cause is: Policy shift towards supporting renewable energy and moving away from coal and gas. Its accident scale is: Unknown (not an accident-related event). Its dates are: 13-12-2022. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers in Australia transitioning to renewable energies. Its cause type is: Policy decision. Its causes are: Endorsement by Energy Ministers for a renewable-focused capacity investment scheme, exclusion of coal and gas from capacity mechanisms, and ongoing efforts since 2015 to adapt to changes in the energy generation mix.

title: Spot volatility in the NSW region on Monday morning, 12th December 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot volatility in the NSW region on Monday morning, 12th December 2022" posted at 12-12-2022 09:20:00 AM is: The article discusses a spike in energy market prices in New South Wales (NSW) region due to operational constraints within the National Electricity Market (NEM). The price volatility was observed during the morning with prices surpassing $1,000/MWh for the third dispatch interval. Contributing factors included hydro generation output increases and counter-price flows over the VIC-NSW interconnector due to the ‘N-RVYS_2’ Constraint Set being invoked. An outage on the Ravine to Yass 330kV line and a service interruption in the negative residue management process were noted as crucial events. These operational issues highlight the complexity of the NEM dynamics. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, but expected to decrease post-afternoon once operational glitches are resolved. Its root_cause is: Operational constraints and a line outage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-12-2022. Its affected region is: New South Wales region. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the NSW region. Its cause type is: Operational and technical. Its causes are: Over $1,000/MWh price spikes, constraints on the interconnector, 'N-RVYS_2' Constraint Set invocation, Ravine to Yass line outage, and negative residue management process service interruption.

title: Who drove FCAS on the island
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Who drove FCAS on the island" posted at 08-12-2022 01:03:00 PM is: The article discusses the impact of November thunderstorms and strong winds in South Australia (SA), which caused a transmission tower to topple, leading to a line trip on the Heywood interconnector. This resulted in a seven-day separation of SA from the rest of the National Electricity Market (NEM), until interim maintenance restored the connection. Although spot energy prices in SA were stable, Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) markets experienced extreme price outcomes, affected by the Administered Price Cap (APC). Seven out of eight FCAS commodities reached the market price cap, with batteries and gas power stations as primary beneficiaries, earning significant revenue. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 7 days. Its root_cause is: transmission tower failure due to extreme weather. Its accident scale is: regional. Its dates are: 08-12-2022 (main date), from 14-11-2022 to 21-11-2022 (impact duration). Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: utility-scale batteries, gas power stations, and large-scale energy consumers. Its cause type is: natural causes (extreme weather). Its causes are: thunderstorms, strong winds, and resultant transmission tower failure.

title: Brief but sudden drop of ~1,740 MW of wind and solar generation across the NEM this afternoon Monday 5th December 2022
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Brief but sudden drop of ~1,740 MW of wind and solar generation across the NEM this afternoon Monday 5th December 2022" posted at 05-12-2022 05:25:00 PM is: A sudden and brief drop of approximately 1,740 MW in wind and solar generation occurred across the NEM, particularly in South Australia (SA) and Victoria (VIC), between 14:50 and 14:55 NEM Time. This led to a significant dip in the output of wind farms by 1,470 MW and large solar generators by 270 MW. Consequently, dispatch prices fell sharply to negative values in these regions before normalizing. Output levels returned to normal by 15:15. The event was influenced by the TUMUT3 generator rebidding negatively, leading to an increase in its output by 1,491 MW, and it was linked with N>>AVMA-17_39_11 constraint during the 14:55 interval. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (less than 1 hour with a rebound by 15:15). Its root_cause is: Sudden generation drop and rebidding action by TUMUT3. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-12-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA) and Victoria (VIC). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Generation and System Constraint Issues. Its causes are: Sudden drop in wind and solar output, TUMUT3 rebidding action, N>>AVMA-17_39_11 constraint.

title: Palmerston to Waddamana lines returned to service on December 2, 2022
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Palmerston to Waddamana lines returned to service on December 2, 2022" posted at 02-12-2022 01:45:00 PM is: The article discusses the successful restoration of the 220kV transmission lines between Palmerston and Waddamana in Tasmania, which were out of service due to a landslide on October 14, 2022. This landslide caused trips on both lines, significantly impacting the electricity network by necessitating the invocation of constraint set T-X_LIPM. The completion of repair works by TasNetworks has now allowed these critical lines to be reconnected, restoring the network link between southern and northern Tasmania; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: October 14, 2022, to December 2, 2022. Its root_cause is: Landslide impacting the footings of a strain tower on the transmission line. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates is: October 14, 2022, to December 2, 2022. Affected region is: Tasmania. Affected key users is: Unknown. Cause type is: Natural event. Causes is: Landslide causing trips on transmission lines.

title: AEMO release preliminary report into the trip of the South East – Tailem Bend 275 kV on 12 November 2022
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO release preliminary report into the trip of the South East – Tailem Bend 275 kV on 12 November 2022" posted on 02-12-2022 10:51:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has published a preliminary report on the incident that caused South Australia (SA) to be electrically isolated from the National Electricity Market (NEM) for a week in mid-November. The report reveals that the isolation was due to a failure of two circuits of the South East – Tailem Bend 275 kV line and subsequent incidents involving the Keith – Tailem Bend 132 kV line, triggered by automated safety protocols during severe weather conditions with wind gusts up to 115 km/h. AEMO's examination highlights a peak in SA's frequency at approximately 50.53 Hz and several voltage dips at the Para 275 kV substation shortly after the event. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate impact starting from 12th November 2022, with ongoing effects over the following week. Its root cause is: Double circuit transmission tower failure due to severe weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Region-wide impact, affecting South Australia's connection to the NEM. Its dates are: 12th November 2022 (same as the main date). Its affected region is: South Australia (SA). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Extreme weather event. Its causes are: Failure of South East – Tailem Bend 275 kV lines, tripping of Keith – Tailem Bend 132 kV line, concurrent thunderstorms and high winds causing system instability.

title: AEMO releases ‘Engineering Roadmap to 100% Renewables’ report
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases ‘Engineering Roadmap to 100% Renewables’ report" posted at 01-12-2022 04:23:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has published its comprehensive 'Engineering Roadmap to 100% Renewables' report. This document aims to outline the technical requisites for the National Electricity Market (NEM) to function reliably at 100% instantaneous renewable energy levels. The report is divided into three key themes: System Security, Operability, and Resource Adequacy and Capability, providing a detailed exploration of the challenges and steps involved in the transition to a fully renewable-powered grid. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Transition planning for renewable energy. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-12-2022. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy policymakers and stakeholders in the renewable energy sector. Its cause type is: Strategic planning for renewable energy transition. Its causes are: Development of technical frameworks for achieving 100% renewable penetration in the NEM.

title: The Administered Price Cap has increased to $600/MWh as of today 1st December 2022
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Administered Price Cap has increased to $600/MWh as of today 1st December 2022" posted at 01-12-2022 03:40:00 PM is: The Administered Price Cap (APC) has been increased from $300/MWh to $600/MWh effective December 1, 2022, following a proposal by Alinta Energy due to extreme pricing in June 2022 across all four mainland regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM). The rule change is temporary, lasting until June 30, 2025, during which the AEMC's Reliability Panel will consider long-term APC adjustments and mechanisms. title: Callide C3’s return to service pushed back to February 2023
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Callide C3’s return to service pushed back to February 2023" posted at 29-11-2022 02:46:00 PM is: The return to service of the Callide Power Station's C3 unit has been further delayed by five weeks, now expected by February 11th, 2023, due to ongoing cooling tower issues. The initial return date was postponed from November 21st, 2022, to January 3rd, 2023, and now again to February 11th, 2023. The C4 unit, which suffered a catastrophic explosion in May 2021, remains scheduled to be offline until April 7th, 2023; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: October 31st, 2022, for the initial cooling tower failure announcement, February 11th, 2023, for C3's expected return, and April 7th, 2023, for C4's expected return. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational failures. author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Administered Pricing ends for FCAS in SA on Saturday 26th November 2022" posted at 26-11-2022 12:16:00 PM is: Administered Pricing for Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) in South Australia has concluded after being in effect for approximately 12 days because the cumulative price dipped below the threshold of $1,398,100. title: A quick (interim) review of the ~7 days living on the SA island + some broader questions to explore later
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick (interim) review of the ~7 days living on the SA island + some broader questions to explore later" posted at 24-11-2022 06:18:00 PM is: The news article discusses the restoration efforts following the isolation of South Australia from the National Electricity Market due to storm damage to a transmission tower on November 12, 2022. ElectraNet repaired the damage by installing temporary transmission towers, restoring power flows between South Australia and Victoria by November 19, thus improving power security and reducing generation constraints. Key activities during the islanding included the curtailment of rooftop PV and fluctuations in frequency, with a return to normal operations noted by November 23. Future focus will be on implementing a permanent repair and further analysis of the event. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: Extreme weather; Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short term (approximately 12 days of direct impact with potential longer-term implications due to ongoing repairs and policy responses); Its root_cause is: Severe weather caused the collapse of a transmission tower, leading to the islanding of South Australia; Its accident scale is: Regional, affecting the South Australia region; Its dates are: 12-11-2022 to 23-11-2022; Its affected region is: South Australia; Its affected key users are: ElectraNet customers and the South Australian electricity market participants; Its cause type is: Natural disaster (severe weather event); Its causes are: A storm causing a transmission tower to collapse, resulting in disconnection from the National Electricity Market.

title: Enhancement of ASEFS and AWEFS forecasts provided by AEMO – from Wednesday 23th November 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Enhancement of ASEFS and AWEFS forecasts provided by AEMO – from Wednesday 23th November 2022" posted at 23-11-2022 07:00:00 AM is: The article discusses the enhancement of the ASEFS and AWEFS forecasts by AEMO, which went live on 23rd November 2022. The change aims to improve the ‘Available Generation’ forecasts for Semi-Scheduled units in the NEMDE dispatch process by implementing a better model developed by AEMO itself rather than outsourcing. The implications of this enhancement are anticipated to be explored in future updates and reports, with a focus on reducing Aggregate Raw Off-Target for Semi-Scheduled plants. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term. title: SA Islanding: Never mind the ‘Big Solar Button’, here’s Flexible Exports
author: James Martin
classified_content: The summary of the news "SA Islanding: Never mind the ‘Big Solar Button’, here’s Flexible Exports" posted at 22-11-2022 11:12:00 AM is: The article discusses the impact of a storm on 12 November which severed South Australia’s connection to Victoria, nearly islanding the state from the National Electricity Market. While immediate local outages occurred due to fallen trees and poles, the disconnection reduced AEMO's tools for grid management. Emergency measures like the Remote Disconnection and Reconnection (RD&R), known as the 'Big Solar Button', were implemented to manage rooftop solar exports and maintain grid stability. This approach is temporary until the planned interconnector with NSW completes by 2026. Meanwhile, new technologies such as Flexible/Dynamic Exports are being trialed to better integrate distributed energy resources such as rooftop solar into the grid operation. This move is fundamental as South Australia leads globally in renewable adoption, with rooftop solar sometimes meeting all or more of the state’s energy demand. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term (until the connection is restored and longer-term until the completion of new interconnectors). Its root_cause is: Storm damage to a transmission tower causing disconnection. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12 November 2022 to the expected restoration by the following Sunday. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Regional residential and commercial electricity users in South Australia. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Storm knocked down transmission tower, leading to partial isolation of the SA grid; subsequent need for emergency solar export management.

title: SA islanding ends, on Saturday evening 19th November 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "SA islanding ends, on Saturday evening 19th November 2022" posted at 19-11-2022 07:39:00 PM is: The Tailem Bend – South East No 1 275kV line in South Australia was returned to service on 19th November 2022 at 18:03, ending a seven-day islanding event. This re-synchronization with the Eastern National Electricity Market (NEM) was announced by AEMO. Despite the reconnection, power flows remained constrained due to a planned outage on another 275kV line, scheduled to return on 21st November. The event followed constraints and negative pricing conditions caused by strong winds in South Australia. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (7 days). Its root_cause is: Line outage and subsequent islanding of South Australia from the NEM. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 12-11-2022 to 19-11-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Technical failure and planned maintenance. Its causes are: Islanding due to the Tailem Bend – South East 275kV line outage, strong winds contributing to network constraints, planned maintenance affecting other lines.

title: Curtailment of rooftop PV occurring in South Australia on Saturday 19th November 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Curtailment of rooftop PV occurring in South Australia on Saturday 19th November 2022" posted at 19-11-2022 10:00:00 AM is: AEMO has implemented curtailments on distributed rooftop photovoltaic (PV) systems in South Australia on November 19, 2022, due to a Distributed PV Contingency condition to maintain system security. The curtailment, effective from 0830 hrs, involved reducing PV generation below a secure threshold to manage power system stability, with interventions expected to conclude by 1600 hrs the same day. This follows several similar interventions earlier in the week, highlighting ongoing challenges in balancing distributed generation with system security. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/System Instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, until 1600 hrs on 19/11/2022. title: Exploring aspects of Wind Farm performance in South Australia on Saturday 12th November – through the storm and into the islanding
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Exploring aspects of Wind Farm performance in South Australia on Saturday 12th November – through the storm and into the islanding" posted at 18-11-2022 02:42:00 PM is: Severe thunderstorms struck South Australia, causing power disruptions and impacting wind farm outputs. The storm led to the islanding of the region due to the tripping of key transmission lines, affecting more than 80,000 people with power loss. Despite the extreme weather, wind power output remained relatively stable overall, although individual sites experienced fluctuations due to local wind variability, network conditions, and high wind cut-out conditions. The event highlighted the challenges of maintaining consistent wind power output amid adverse conditions and significant grid constraints.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. title: (belatedly) one year on from Five Minute Settlement
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "(belatedly) one year on from Five Minute Settlement" posted at 17-11-2022 05:15:00 PM is: The article reflects on the first anniversary of the Five Minute Settlement (5MS) initiative, which started on 1st October 2021 in the Australian electricity market. This initiative was expected to bring changes in price outcomes and bidding patterns. The analysis shows increased price volatility in the year following 5MS, attributed partially to the ongoing 2022 Energy Crisis and possibly the 5MS implementation. The article also discusses the complexities in estimating the cost of implementing 5MS, with estimates ranging from $500 million to $1 billion, and notes the absence of any formal process to accurately collate these costs; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 1st October 2021 to 1st October 2022. Its root_cause is: Implementation of Five Minute Settlement (5MS). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 1st October 2021 to 1st October 2022. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market policy implementation. Its causes are: Shift in electricity bidding patterns and increased price volatility post-5MS.

title: Volatility in South Australia on Thursday afternoon 17th November 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Volatility in South Australia on Thursday afternoon 17th November 2022" posted at 17-11-2022 04:44:00 PM is: Price volatility occurred in South Australia due to a combination of very low wind generation, an abundance of solar generation with rooftop PV being curtailed for security reasons, and the region being islanded with no support from the east. The cumulative price threshold was reached earlier in the week, leading to intervention with an Administered Price Cap for certain FCAS commodities. Despite the presence of many thermal generators, the grid experienced high inertia levels. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. title: Curtailment of distributed PV occurring in South Australia on Thursday 17th November 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Curtailment of distributed PV occurring in South Australia on Thursday 17th November 2022" posted at 17-11-2022 10:00:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) announced that distributed photovoltaic (PV) curtailment has been initiated in South Australia on the morning of November 17, 2022, due to a Distributed PV Contingency condition. This measure is being taken to maintain system security, with the expected end time for the curtailment set for 6:00 PM the same day. The curtailment was triggered by a forecasted elevated risk of disconnection of distributed PVs which would cause a loss of scheduled generation beyond secure thresholds. As part of the intervention, actions may include directing scheduled generation or maintaining distributed PV generation below a secure threshold. The forecast DPV contingency was predicted to peak at 255 MW at 12:30 PM, with the contingency direction trigger set at 78 MW. Its relevance level is: Level 1. The sequence of market notices indicated potential risks of disconnection of distributed PV, requiring AEMO to keep the generated power below secure thresholds. The curtailment is part of established processes to manage power system stability amid increasing renewable energy generation. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). title: ElectraNet chief executive warns risks still to come as sunny weather forecast for Adelaide on Thursday
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "ElectraNet chief executive warns risks still to come as sunny weather forecast for Adelaide on Thursday" posted at 15-11-2022 05:38:00 PM is: The ongoing islanding event in South Australia, which began on Saturday due to a fallen transmission tower part of the Heywood interconnector between SA and VIC, poses ongoing risks. ElectraNet chief executive Simon Emms highlights concerns about rooftop PV production control as sunny weather is forecasted for Thursday, November 17th, predicting high rooftop PV generation could exacerbate security issues. The affected tower will remain out of service for a week, prompting worries about continued electricity market instability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: Why the Adelaide storm was so intense
author: Ant Sharwood
classified_content: The summary of the news "Why the Adelaide storm was so intense" posted at 15-11-2022 01:09:00 PM is: Adelaide and southern South Australia experienced a significant thunderstorm on Saturday, resulting in flash flooding, power outages, and widespread lightning. The storm was caused by an unstable atmosphere and a low-pressure surface trough, creating a squall line that produced severe weather conditions comparable to a low-grade tropical cyclone. A tower collapse at Tailem Bend led to the regional electricity grid's disconnection from the National Electricity Market, although it remained securely operational. The storm led to fallen trees and property damage, leaving many homes without power. South Australia rarely encounters storms of such intensity, prompting ongoing cleanup and recovery efforts; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate with ongoing recovery efforts. Its root_cause is: Extreme weather conditions caused by atmospheric instability and a surface trough. Its accident scale is: Regional, affecting much of southern South Australia. Its dates are: 15-11-2022. Its affected region is: Southern South Australia, including Adelaide and Fleurieu Peninsula. Its affected key users are: Residential and business users, schools, power grid operators. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Atmospheric instability, surface trough, squall line formation, high winds, and heavy rainfall.

title: A messy separation: a look at the frequency swings in SA during Saturday afternoon’s islanding event
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A messy separation: a look at the frequency swings in SA during Saturday afternoon’s islanding event" posted at 15-11-2022 11:30:00 AM is: The news discusses the impact of severe thunderstorms and strong winds on the 13th of November, which led to infrastructure damage and disconnection of the Heywood interconnector, causing South Australia to be electrically islanded from the National Electricity Market (NEM). This isolation resulted in significant frequency swings in South Australia, pushing the system outside the normal operating frequency band, requiring prompt intervention to stabilize it and impacting rooftop solar PV systems and frequency control ancillary services (FCAS) markets. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to several hours, with ongoing frequency deviations continuing into the evening. Its root_cause is: Severe weather leading to the loss of the Heywood interconnector. Its accident scale is: Unknown. The affected key users are: Rooftop solar PV systems and FCAS participants. The cause type is: Environmental. The causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Thunderstorms and strong winds causing transmission network trips and the downing of a transmission tower in South Australia.

title: Curtailment of distributed PV is occurring in South Australia on Tuesday 15th November 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Curtailment of distributed PV is occurring in South Australia on Tuesday 15th November 2022" posted at 15-11-2022 11:08:00 AM is: The article reports that on November 15, 2022, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued multiple market notices regarding potential contingencies related to Distributed Photovoltaic (PV) systems in South Australia. These notices were to maintain system security due to an elevated risk of disconnection of distributed PV systems as the power generated by these systems exceeded secure thresholds. The AEMO took actions such as curtailing PV generation and potentially scheduling generation to prevent system instability, with interventions potentially occurring from the morning until late afternoon.
