Its root_cause is: Strategic policy initiative by the Queensland Government. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-09-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy Change. Its causes are: Shift to 80% renewable energy by 2035, investment in $62 billion in clean energy, and phasing out coal reliance.

title: Analytical Challenge – choosing what measure to use, for ‘Installed Capacity’
author: Paul McArdle
topic: A collection of some core analytical challenges
classified_content: The summary of the news "Analytical Challenge – choosing what measure to use, for ‘Installed Capacity’" posted at 27-09-2022 03:29:00 PM is: The article discusses the complexities in determining the 'Installed Capacity' for units within the National Electricity Market (NEM). It highlights the challenges of choosing between 'Registered Capacity' and 'Maximum Capacity' as measures, how these metrics vary by plant type, and the implications for understanding project yields and performance metrics like Capacity Factor. The piece also examines the discrepancies between what is registered and the actual operational capacities, noting such variances at the beginning and end of an asset's life. The article concludes with a caution for analysts to consider these factors when evaluating capacity data. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing concern as it relates to analytical practices. Its root_cause is: The inherent complexities in representing installed capacity accurately. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-09-2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Energy analysts and policy makers. Its cause type is: Analytical challenge. Its causes are: Choice of capacity measures, variance in data for different units, and discrepancies between registration and actual capacity outcomes.

title: Minimum Demand drops lower, on Sunday 25th September 2022 – for the NSW region, and also NEMwide
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Minimum Demand drops lower, on Sunday 25th September 2022 – for the NSW region, and also NEMwide" posted at 25-09-2022 01:45:00 PM is: The news discusses the record low in electricity market demand for both the NSW region and the entire National Electricity Market (NEM) on September 25, 2022. At 13:30, NSW's market demand hit a low of 4,197MW, while the NEM-wide demand dropped to 12,113MW at 12:25. The article highlights the significant contribution of solar power, with small and large solar productions reaching approximately 11,800MW. Such reductions in demand are partly due to increasing solar power generation, as noted by AEMO through grid and operational demand metrics. The low demand aligns with trends observed during shoulder months and emphasizes ongoing shifts in the energy landscape due to renewable integration. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Temporary, specific to the day of the event, reflecting potential for similar future trends during shoulder months. Its root cause is: Increased solar power generation leading to reduced grid demand during daylight hours. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 25-09-2022. Its affected region is: NSW and the NEM-wide area. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes is: Record low demand coinciding with high solar generation output, reflective of seasonal energy use patterns and renewable energy influence.

title: What was the total cost of ‘keeping the lights on’ across the NEM in June?
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "What was the total cost of ‘keeping the lights on’ across the NEM in June?" posted at 16-09-2022 10:00:00 AM is: In June 2022, the National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced significant turbulence resulting in the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) suspending the market for nine days due to factors such as rising global commodity prices, La Niña weather patterns, and plant outages. Despite these challenges, load-shedding was avoided, but operational costs soared. The total estimated cost for June is approximated at $6.77 billion, primarily driven by energy spot market operations. This figure is potentially lower than initial speculation due to a reduced compensation claim than expected; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: June 2022. Its root cause is: A combination of rising global commodity prices, La Niña weather conditions, and plant outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 1st June 2022 to 30th June 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, and Tasmania. Its affected key users are: Electricity end-users across the NEM. Its cause type is: Systemic and environmental factors. Its causes are: Rising global commodity prices, La Niña weather conditions affecting supply and demand, plant outages, and market suspension by AEMO.

title: Two charts from ‘Smart Energy Queensland’ presentation, illustrating the electricity supply mix in Queensland
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two charts from ‘Smart Energy Queensland’ presentation, illustrating the electricity supply mix in Queensland" posted at 14-09-2022 03:53:00 PM is: The article discusses a presentation at the 'Smart Energy Queensland' conference, highlighting the electricity supply mix in Queensland with a focus on fuel mix, demand, exports, imports, and spot prices. The charts presented show a dominance of coal and gas in energy production, growing contributions from solar, and details on market and operational demand measures. The volatility in spot prices in Q2 2022 is emphasized, and the percentage supply mix shows renewables reaching 22% in February 2022. Furthermore, the article critiques the current metrics used and suggests improved metrics that focus on wind and solar contributions. It highlights the complexities of the National Electricity Market (NEM) and stresses the importance of NEM-wide performance measures.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 14-09-2022. title: The Case for Hourly Renewable Energy Certificates
author: James Allan
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Case for Hourly Renewable Energy Certificates" posted at 13-09-2022 01:10:00 PM is: The article discusses the need for hourly Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs) as a means to address challenges in decarbonizing Australia's power market. It underscores the importance of synchronized renewable energy generation and consumption to cater to the increasing commitment of major corporations like Google and Microsoft to 24/7 renewable energy use by 2030. The proposal aims to modify current REC policies to include hourly targets, supporting 24/7 green energy supplies, which could facilitate a faster transition to decarbonized, resilient power systems by incentivizing investment in renewable energy and storage solutions. This suggested shift could also serve as a stepping stone towards broader reforms in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (potentially over decades, as it targets full implementation by 2040 and beyond). Its root_cause is: The need for synchronization between renewable energy generation and consumption timelines to accelerate decarbonization and support continuous green energy supply. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-09-2022. Its affected region is: Australia, with potential global implications. Its affected key users are: Large corporations and government entities committed to 24/7 renewable energy use. Its cause type is: Policy and market-driven challenges. Its causes are: The conventional REC system's inability to align real-time energy supply with demand, major corporations' commitments to 24/7 renewable energy, and the need for greater storage and demand response investments.

title: AEMC published final determination for ‘Primary frequency response incentive arrangements’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC published final determination for ‘Primary frequency response incentive arrangements’" posted at 11-09-2022 03:21:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) has published its final determination regarding the Primary Frequency Response (PFR) Incentive Arrangements. This new framework introduces financial incentives for market participants to manage power system frequency effectively, ensuring grid stability and lowering costs for consumers. The determination includes the continuation of mandatory PFR requirements, a double-sided 'Causer Pays' approach, and new reporting obligations for AEMO and AER. These reforms aim to enhance the efficiency of frequency control services in the National Electricity Market (NEM) and provide more transparent cost allocation. The commencement of the frequency performance payments is set for June 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium to long-term impact starting June 2025. Its root_cause is: Regulatory changes in frequency control incentives. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 8th September 2022 (publication date), 8th May 2023 (AEMO consultation), and 8th June 2025 (commencement of new payments). Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: All scheduled and semi-scheduled electricity generators. Its cause type is: Regulatory policy change. Its causes are: Implementation of financial incentives for frequency control, continuation of mandatory PFR, and changes to reporting and 'Causer Pays' mechanisms.

title: ‘Minimum Demand’ records pushed lower in QLD on Sunday 11th September 2022 … re ‘Emergency backstop mechanism’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘Minimum Demand’ records pushed lower in QLD on Sunday 11th September 2022 … re ‘Emergency backstop mechanism’" posted at 11-09-2022 01:27:00 PM is: On Sunday 11th September, 2022, Queensland's "Market Demand" reached a new record low of 3,316MW according to NEMwatch data, indicating ongoing minimal energy demands, driven primarily by the rising contribution of rooftop solar to energy supply, and consequently stressing the grid's management capabilities. This has posed significant challenges, reflected in the possible implementation of an "Emergency Backstop Mechanism" by Energy Queensland, aimed at managing excess solar energy through a system to shut down solar exports as necessary. The "Operational Demand" during the same period also reached a record low of 3,469MW. In conjunction with low demand situations in other regions like NSW, it highlights the broader NEM-wide challenge in managing energy supply and demand balance. The consultations on this mechanism close on 7th October 2022. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to Short Term. Its root cause is: Rising rooftop solar generation reducing grid demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-09-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland/QLD. Its affected key users are: Residential solar customers and grid operators. Its cause type is: Renewable generation contribution exceeding demand. Its causes are: Record low energy demands in the QLD region due to high solar contribution; proposed policy interventions to manage solar excess.

title: A deeper (but still incomplete) look into the Overnight volatility in South Australia (i.e. part 2)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A deeper (but still incomplete) look into the Overnight volatility in South Australia (i.e. part 2)" posted at 08-09-2022 08:53:00 PM is: The article examines a 90-minute period of price volatility in South Australia's electricity market, occurring around midnight on September 8th. Despite the unexpected nature of these price spikes, analysis reveals that clues were visible in forecast models hours prior, though not with certainty. Contributing factors include an overestimation of wind farm output and higher-than-forecasted demand, coinciding with a large volume of unoffered capacity during a traditional outage season. This imbalance between supply and demand precipitated the volatility. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term impact due to the temporary nature of the price spikes. Its root_cause is: Underestimation of renewable generation and higher than expected demand juxtaposed with outage season constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: September 7th-8th, 2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and forecasting issues. Its causes are: Overestimated wind generation ability, unexpected increase in demand, mismatch between available and offered capacity, influence of outage season.

title: Overnight volatility in South Australia – part 1
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Overnight volatility in South Australia – part 1" posted at 08-09-2022 06:52:00 AM is: The article discusses early morning electricity market volatility in South Australia, where spot prices exceeded $1,000/MWh, reaching as high as $11,447.62/MWh between 23:05 and 00:25. Contributing factors included a decrease in the output of Barkers Inlet and wind farms, adjustments in the bidding strategy, and constraints on the Murraylink interconnector, while the Heywood interconnector imported at maximum capacity. This resulted in a Market Notice from AEMO declaring an Actual Lack Of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) condition from 00:30 to 01:30 due to insufficient reserve capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: System demand and supply variations alongside market bidding behavior. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 08-09-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and operational constraints. Its causes is: High electricity spot prices, constrained interconnectors, low wind power generation, change in bid strategies, and reserve capacity shortfall.

title: AER identifies (at least) 9 discrete factors contributing to the extreme price outcomes (and other concerns) through Q2 2022
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER identifies (at least) 9 discrete factors contributing to the extreme price outcomes (and other concerns) through Q2 2022" posted at 07-09-2022 01:30:00 PM is: The AER's Wholesale Markets Quarterly for Q2 2022 identifies multiple factors contributing to extreme electricity price outcomes, including outages and supply problems in coal-fired generation, unexpectedly low wind and solar generation, high gas prices due to international market influences, and high demand from cold winter conditions. Issues such as coal pricing, supply constraints, gas availability for power generation, environmental constraints on hydro, and increased demand were key contributors. The convergence of these issues led to record prices, protective price caps, and a rare market suspension. Future conditions remain challenging with anticipated generation closures and tight gas supply continuing. The report underscores increasing variability, uncertainty, and complexity in the energy market.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Fuel prices. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: The impact is ongoing and expected to persist into the foreseeable future, especially with the prediction of challenging conditions continuing into winter 2023. Root_cause: Root causes include fuel supply issues, generator outages, and unexpected weather patterns causing demand surges and lower renewable generation. Accident scale: Unknown. Dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date): Q2 2022. Affected region: Australia, specifically New South Wales, Queensland, South Australia, and Victoria. Affected key users: Electricity consumers and energy market participants in the National Electricity Market. Cause type: Combination of supply-side factors (fuel costs and generator outages) and demand-side factors (increased demand from colder weather). Causes (a summarized list of cause events): High coal and gas prices, coal generator outages, lower than expected renewable generation, high demand due to cold weather, strategic bidding behavior in the electricity market.

Despite the abundant wind supply, this volatility has been observed as per the NEMwatch snapshot, drawing attention to the market dynamics rather than supply deficiency; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). title: AER releases Wholesale Market Quarterly for Q2 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER releases Wholesale Market Quarterly for Q2 2022" posted at 07-09-2022 10:55:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) released its Wholesale Markets Quarterly report for Q2 2022, highlighting record-high spot prices in gas and electricity, largely due to market interconnections and the reliance on gas-powered electricity generation during this period. The report indicates the unanticipated nature of market volatility and suggests that higher prices may continue into 2023. Despite the report's significance, it has not received wide media coverage; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: fuel prices. title: Benchmarking large-scale solar PV performance
author: Lachlan McLeod
classified_content: The summary of the news "Benchmarking large-scale solar PV performance" posted at 06-09-2022 04:11:00 PM is: The article discusses a recent analysis, presented at the Clean Energy Council’s Large-scale Solar Forum and subsequently published by ARENA, which benchmarks the performance of 26 large-scale solar farms in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) using a performance ratio (PR) metric. The report provides an assessment of solar PV performance using satellite weather data, emphasizing the importance of PR over the capacity factor for evaluating solar plant performance. The analysis introduces a robust methodology for satellite-based PR calculations, allowing comparison of performance across different weather conditions and years. This information serves as a valuable benchmark for developers and investors to assess the relative performance of their solar assets in Australia; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, as it introduces a methodology that can influence future performance assessments. Its root_cause is: The need for better solar farm performance benchmarking. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-09-2022. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Solar PV developers and investors. Its cause type is: Methodological innovation. Its causes are: Introduction of a new benchmark using performance ratios for large-scale solar PVs in the NEM.

title: NEM-wide IRPM drops below 15% on Monday 5th September 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide IRPM drops below 15% on Monday 5th September 2022" posted at 05-09-2022 06:40:00 PM is: On Monday, 5th September 2022, the NEM-wide Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) dropped below 15%, reaching a low of 12.17% at the 18:30 dispatch interval, indicating tight supply-demand conditions and resulting in elevated electricity prices across all regions. This situation was compounded by an Actual Lack Of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) condition declared in the QLD region, signifying a shortage in reserve capacity. Historical data suggests that such occurrences may become more frequent, prompting further analysis in upcoming reports. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, leading to elevated prices. Its root_cause is: IRPM drop below 15% indicating supply-demand imbalance and LOR1 condition. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 05-09-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and broader NEM. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers across the NEM. Its cause type is: System-related. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Low reserve margin, tight supply-demand balance, Actual LOR1 condition.

title: Updated monthly statistics for NEM-wide wind production … to the end of August 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Updated monthly statistics for NEM-wide wind production … to the end of August 2022" posted at 05-09-2022 04:19:00 PM is: The article reviews wind production levels across the National Electricity Market (NEM) up to August 2022. The report highlights that August saw extremities with a record maximum instantaneous production from wind reaching 7,305 MW on 4th August and a significant drop to 204 MW on 26th August due to widespread low wind conditions. May through August 2022 showed stronger wind output levels compared to earlier months, particularly in June, July, and August, each reaching above 30% capacity factors, whereas earlier months like February through April had lower outputs. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Long-Term Market trends and forecasts. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term trend observed through 2022. Its root_cause is: Variability of wind conditions and seasonal patterns affecting production. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-09-2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural variability in wind conditions. Its causes are: Extremes in wind conditions resulting in high variability in wind power generation.

title: Widespread cloud cover across QLD dampens solar production on Friday 2nd September 2022 … and broader
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Widespread cloud cover across QLD dampens solar production on Friday 2nd September 2022" posted at 02-09-2022 12:12:00 PM is: The news article discusses the impact of extensive cloud cover across Queensland (QLD) on solar energy production. On 2nd September 2022, the solar production capacity in QLD reached only 700MW, which is significantly lower than the potential maximum capacity of 2,789MW. This reduced output, compared to sunnier days in August, highlights how weather conditions such as cloud cover can influence the effectiveness of solar farms. The dependence of the National Electricity Market (NEM) on weather conditions is emphasized, with cloud cover cited as a major factor. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specifically on the day of the occurrence. Its root_cause is: Widespread cloud cover diminishing solar production capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 02-09-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Solar energy producers and consumers relying on solar-generated electricity. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Extensive cloud cover across Queensland leading to reduced solar power generation.

title: Reliability Panel publishes final report from 2022 Reliability Standard and Settings Review
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reliability Panel publishes final report from 2022 Reliability Standard and Settings Review" posted at 01-09-2022 01:05:00 PM is: The Reliability Panel within the AEMC has released its final report on the 2022 Reliability Standard and Settings Review, which includes recommendations for reliability standards and market price caps for the period 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2028. The report suggests maintaining the current reliability standard form but proposes a progressive increase in the Market Price Cap to $21,500/MWh and the Cumulative Price Threshold to $2,193,000. Additionally, it recommends an increase in the Administered Price Cap from $300/MWh to $500/MWh to minimize reliance on the compensation scheme. The Market Floor Price will remain unchanged. These changes aim to reflect the evolving nature of reliability in the NEM as it transitions to a high VRE power system. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: From 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2028. Its root cause is: The evolving nature of energy reliability as the NEM transitions to a high VRE power system. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-09-2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) region. Its affected key users are: Electricity market stakeholders and consumers within the NEM. Its cause type is: Structural and policy reform. title: Morning volatility in South Australia on Thursday 1st September 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Morning volatility in South Australia on Thursday 1st September 2022" posted at 01-09-2022 07:37:00 AM is: South Australia's electricity market experienced significant volatility on the first day of spring, with low wind generation and constrained electricity imports from Victoria contributing to a spike in prices. During the 06:15 dispatch interval, prices rose above $1,000/MWh, reaching $3,971.59/MWh by 07:35. This contributes to a Cumulative Price exceeding $700,000; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Low wind generation and constrained electricity imports. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 01-09-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Supply constraint. Its causes are: Low wind generation, constrained imports from Victoria.

title: AEMO releases 2022 ESOO on Wednesday 31st August 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases 2022 ESOO on Wednesday 31st August 2022" posted at 31-08-2022 08:30:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) released the 2022 Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO), highlighting urgent electricity reliability concerns in the National Electricity Market (NEM) over the next decade due to impending coal and gas plant closures and insufficient new generation capacity. The report stresses the need for investment in generation, storage, and transmission to maintain reliable electricity supply. Perry Williams and Nick Evans pointed out the expected exit of 8000 megawatts of coal-fired power, while delays in projects like Snowy Hydro 2.0 exacerbate potential supply gaps. The report and accompanying materials are available for download, and a webinar will be hosted on 1st September 2022 to discuss these findings in further detail; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: The next 10 years. Its root cause is: Coal and gas plant closures and insufficient new generation capacity commitments. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 31-08-2022. Its affected region is: Most regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Homes and businesses in NEM regions. Its cause type is: Structural and policy-related issues in energy supply. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Considerable coal and gas plant closures, insufficient new generation capacity commitments, and project delays like Snowy Hydro 2.0.

title: Substantially different than a year ago, the 2022 ESOO points to challenges ahead in the supply/demand balance
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Substantially different than a year ago, the 2022 ESOO points to challenges ahead in the supply/demand balance" posted at 31-08-2022 07:30:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) released the 2022 Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO), highlighting significant changes in the electricity market since the previous year. Key points include updated closure dates for major coal-fired power stations, contributing to the ongoing energy crisis, and concerns about delays in critical infrastructure projects such as Snowy 2.0 and Project Energy Connect. The 2022 ESOO emphasizes the growing roles of summer and winter demand peaks in reliability assessments, noting potential risks in South Australia, Victoria, and New South Wales in the next few years. Uncertainties in demand and supply, including the impact of weather conditions and renewable energy integration challenges, underscore the complexity of future market conditions.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy . Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: The report projects conditions and potential impacts over the next decade, with immediate concerns for the 2022-23 summer and broader concerns leading up to 2024-25 and beyond. Its root_cause is: The evolving electricity market landscape, characterized by coal-fired station closures, infrastructure project delays, and rising renewable integration challenges. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-08-2022. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) regions in Australia, particularly South Australia, Victoria, and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Large industrial customers and general electricity consumers in the NEM regions. Its cause type is: Market forecasting and planning document updates. Its causes are: Uncertainty in coal unit reliability, infrastructure projects facing delays, and the transition to a higher renewable energy mix in the power system.

title: Instantaneous NEM-wide production from wind on Fri 26th Aug 2022 was lowest in eleven months
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Instantaneous NEM-wide production from wind on Fri 26th Aug 2022 was lowest in eleven months" posted at 27-08-2022 05:16:00 PM is: The article discusses the exceptionally low wind power production across the National Electricity Market (NEM) on August 26, 2022. Wind energy contribution was a mere 204MW, just 0.98% of grid demand, marking the lowest level since September 15, 2021. This decline in production is attributed to a combination of low wind conditions and constrained imports, exacerbating volatility in South Australia. Positive prices indicated economic factors were not at play, while an outage on the Liddell to Newcastle line affected some wind farms. The diverse location of wind farms minimally mitigated the impact, with the Mount Emerald Wind Farm contributing significantly.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Low wind conditions and constrained imports. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 26-08-2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions, particularly South Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural and operational constraints. Its causes is: Low wind conditions, constrained imports, and line outage.

title: Morning, afternoon and evening volatility in South Australia on Friday 26th August 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Morning, afternoon and evening volatility in South Australia on Friday 26th August 2022" posted at 26-08-2022 05:31:00 PM is: The electricity market in South Australia experienced significant price volatility on August 26, 2022, due to very low wind production and constraints on imports from Victoria. During the day, the price surged above $10,000/MWh for a sustained period, leading to a cumulative price exceeding $780,000. The region relied heavily on gas-fired generation, supplemented by solar energy and peaking diesel-fired units to meet demand. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (within the day). Its root_cause is: Low wind production and import limitations. title: Australian Energy Council publishes article about the Market Suspension in June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "Australian Energy Council publishes article about the Market Suspension in June 2022" posted at 25-08-2022 12:36:00 PM is: The article addresses the market suspension events in June 2022, particularly in Queensland on June 12 and New South Wales, South Australia, and Victoria on June 13, when the Cumulative Price Cap was hit. It highlights the importance of understanding the National Electricity Market's dynamics during periods of high prices and supply scarcity. The Australian Energy Council has published an article analyzing these events, building on AEMO’s report, to provide a factual and objective perspective on the ongoing energy transition and crisis. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: June 2022 and ongoing. Its root_cause is: Market suspension and high prices due to supply scarcity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: June 12-13, 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia, Victoria. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market-related. Its causes are: High prices, market suspension, cumulative price cap being hit, supply scarcity.

title: Run of price volatility in SA on Thursday morning 25th August 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Run of price volatility in SA on Thursday morning 25th August 2022" posted at 25-08-2022 09:43:00 AM is: The article reports on the substantial price volatility experienced in South Australia's electricity market on the morning of August 25, 2022. Prices surged to as high as $10,000/MWh and remained elevated throughout the morning due to limited wind and solar generation, along with constrained imports from Victoria. Additionally, several Lack of Reserve Market Notices were issued, indicating ongoing supply concerns for the day. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term, potentially extending over several days as indicated by forecast notices. Its root_cause is: Low renewable output and import constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-08-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. title: Diverse generator responses to illusive  FCAS prices on Wednesday 10th August 2022
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Diverse generator responses to illusive FCAS prices on Wednesday 10th August 2022" posted at 22-08-2022 09:02:00 AM is: The article examines the market volatility experienced on 10th August 2022, where the Frequency Control Ancillary Service (FCAS) prices hit the market price cap due to increased demand from 11:35 to 12:35. Diverse generator responses were observed as different fuel types adjusted their outputs in reaction to the price spikes. The event led to high estimated FCAS costs, prompting price revisions by AEMO later that evening, which significantly altered prices back to typical levels for affected intervals. The variations in generator behavior highlight intricate market dynamics faced by different players. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, lasting about 65 minutes on 10th August 2022 before subsequent price revisions on the same day. Its root_cause is: Sudden increases in constraints for FCAS services leading to price spikes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-08-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA), Tasmania (TAS), and Victoria (VIC) with most disturbance in TAS. Its affected key users are: Electricity generators and FCAS service providers. Its cause type is: Market operation and constraint management issue. Its causes are: Sudden increase in FCAS service constraints, AEMO's IT changes requiring rollback, revisions to price levels later the same day.

title: AEMO releases report on Market Suspension during June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases report on Market Suspension during June 2022" posted at 18-08-2022 03:57:00 PM is: AEMO has published a detailed report regarding the market suspension in the National Electricity Market (NEM) from 15th to 24th June 2022, triggered by a complex interplay of factors, including high commodity prices, domestic market price caps, outages of scheduled generating plants, low output from semi-scheduled generation, and increased winter demand. These led to low reserve conditions and the operation of the Queensland – New South Wales Interconnector (QNI) exceeding secure limits, resulting in multiple directions for reliability and an unprecedented market suspension; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Confluence of high commodity prices, market constraints, and generation outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15th to 24th June 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia, and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: System operation and market conditions. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: High commodity prices, domestic market caps, planned and forced outages of scheduled generating plants, low output from semi-scheduled generation, and high winter demand.

title: AEMO released Compensation Update for June 2022 events
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO released Compensation Update for June 2022 events" posted at 18-08-2022 01:28:00 PM is: The article discusses the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) update on compensation following significant market events in June 2022, where the Cumulative Price Cap was hit in Queensland on the 12th and in New South Wales, South Australia, and Victoria on the 13th. This update includes directions given by AEMO during these events and acknowledges ongoing compensatory determinations by the Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) due to the large volume of capacity withdrawn from the National Electricity Market Dispatch Engine (NEMDE) pricing run. There are at least two theories proposed for the capacity withdrawal occurrence.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Since June 2022 with ongoing impacts as compensation decisions are still being determined. Its root_cause is: Withdrawal of large volumes of capacity from the NEMDE pricing run during the specified events. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-08-2022 01:28:00 PM. Its cause type is: Market operational decisions and external economic factors. title: Case Study of Wednesday 10th August 2022 – Part 1 (Response of Wind Farms)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Case Study of Wednesday 10th August 2022 – Part 1 (Response of Wind Farms)" posted at 17-08-2022 08:36:00 AM is: The event described in the article occurred on Wednesday, 10th August 2022, within the National Electricity Market (NEM), where a software upgrade to the NEM Dispatch Engine (NEMDE) caused an unintended spike in the requirement for Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS), resulting in sky-high prices for FCAS commodities and changed dispatch targets. This particularly affected wind farms, which had varied responses ranging from manual rebidding to automated shutdowns. The incident underscores the importance of reliable software updates and highlights the complexities of managing energy markets dynamically under such circumstances; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, primarily on the day of the event. Its root_cause is: A software glitch in the NEMDE system. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-08-2022. Its affected region is: All five regions of the NEM, with specific mentions of Tasmania and mainland regions. Its affected key users are: Semi-Scheduled and other Wind Farm operators. Its cause type is: Technical software failure. Its causes are: A software upgrade error leading to false spikes in FCAS demand and erroneous pricing signals.

title: QLD minimum demand drops lower still on Sunday 14th August 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "QLD minimum demand drops lower still on Sunday 14th August 2022" posted at 14-08-2022 11:30:00 AM is: The article discusses the notable drop in electricity demand in Queensland on August 14, 2022, attributed to optimal weather conditions and increased rooftop PV power generation. Electricity demand hit new lows at multiple points during the day, with the lowest 'Market Demand' recorded at 3,365.23MW at 12:05 PM and the lowest 'Operational Demand' at 3,490MW in the half-hour ending 12:30 PM, according to AEMO. This phenomenon is becoming more common, often triggered by what the article describes as 'Goldilocks' weather conditions, which neither increase heating nor cooling demand. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (immediate impact on the day of the event). Its root_cause is: High photovoltaic generation and moderate weather conditions reducing demand. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates are: 14-08-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity suppliers and grid operators. Its cause type is: Natural and technological. Its causes are: Increased output from rooftop PV installations, moderate weather conditions reducing the need for additional heating or cooling.

title: Load shedding *briefly* forecast for South Australia on Monday evening 15th August 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Load shedding *briefly* forecast for South Australia on Monday evening 15th August 2022" posted at 10-08-2022 02:06:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) initially forecasted a Lack Of Reserve Level 3 (LOR3) condition, suggesting potential load shedding in South Australia on 15th August 2022 due to insufficient electricity reserves. However, this forecast was later canceled after an updated projection showed an increased wind power generation capacity, alleviating the stress on the power supply. This event highlights the sensitivity of the electricity market to weather conditions and renewable energy forecasts. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Temporary, specifically for 15th August 2022. Its root cause is: Initial insufficient power reserves forecast for South Australia, later revised due to increased wind power generation. Its accident scale is: Localized impact forecasted at 5 MW; no actual interruption occurred. Its dates are: 15th August 2022, updated on 10th August 2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecast and weather-dependent energy generation. Its causes are: Initial wind energy forecast underestimation, later corrected to avoid load shedding.

title: Market volatility on Wednesday 10th August 2022 (incl FCAS prices going haywire) – Failure of Market Systems?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Market volatility on Wednesday 10th August 2022 (incl FCAS prices going haywire) – Failure of Market Systems?" posted at 10-08-2022 12:33:00 PM is: On August 10, 2022, a scheduling error in the National Electricity Market (NEM) led to market volatility, particularly affecting contingency Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) prices. Elevated prices were reported, especially in Tasmania, due to a failure affecting market dispatch and pricing systems after an IT change in the NEM dispatch engine. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) reversed the software change, which resolved the issue. AEMO declared a scheduling error and commenced an investigation to assess the impact and provide recommendations for future improvements. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term; primarily affecting trading intervals on August 10, 2022. Its root_cause is: IT-related issue with NEM dispatch engine software. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 10-08-2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (with specific mention of Tasmania). Its affected key users is: All regions in the NEM. Its cause type is: Technical/system failure. Its causes is: 1) IT change in NEM dispatch engine leading to failure affecting market dispatch and pricing, 2) Reversal of the software change, 3) High volumes of FCAS requirements leading to price caps, 4) AEMO's continued investigation into the error.

title: 8th Aug 2022 – EnergyAustralia reported an earnings fall for the first half of calendar 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "8th Aug 2022 – EnergyAustralia reported an earnings fall for the first half of calendar 2022" posted at 08-08-2022 08:00:00 PM is: EnergyAustralia experienced a significant decline in earnings for the first half of 2022 due to lower generation output and the need to purchase high-priced spot market electricity to cover retail client demand. The performance was severely impacted by unforeseen and planned maintenance outages at Mount Piper and Yallourn Power Stations, coupled with fuel supply issues caused by lower-than-expected coal deliveries. This situation unfolded against the wider backdrop of the 2022 global energy crisis, emphasizing the challenges in generation reliability and energy transition. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing through the first half of 2022. Its root cause is: Lower generation output due to maintenance outages and coal supply constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-08-2022. Its affected region is: Unspecified, though related to Mount Piper and Yallourn Power Stations. Its affected key users are: EnergyAustralia's retail customers. Its cause type is: Operational and supply chain. Its causes are: Maintenance outages, coal supply constraints, high spot market prices.

title: New lowest point for QLD ‘Market Demand’ on Sunday 7th August … excluding outages
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New lowest point for QLD ‘Market Demand’ on Sunday 7th August … excluding outages" posted at 07-08-2022 11:30:00 AM is: On a warm and sunny day in Brisbane, the Queensland region marked a new lowest point for 'Market Demand' at 3,506MW, excluding outages, on August 7, 2022. This is a significant drop from the previous low of 3,672MW recorded on October 3, 2021. The trend reflects a continued decline in demand since 2009, under ordinary conditions without extraordinary events like the Callide C4 Catastrophe. The new low for 'Operational Demand' was 3,672MW, representing a significant decrease from prior lows. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Residential satisfaction and residential surveys since the news mainly reflects on changes in demand without major external incidents. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, primarily impacting the day of and possibly similar future periods of low demand. Its root cause is: Favorable weather conditions and possible increased efficiency in energy use or technology improvements. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates are: 07-08-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market-driven reduction in demand. Its causes are: Decline in electricity demand due to warm weather and possibly increased efficiency.

title: All time record for wind production, NEM-wide, ratchets further on Thursday 4th August 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "All time record for wind production, NEM-wide, ratchets further on Thursday 4th August 2022" posted at 05-08-2022 07:50:00 AM is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia reached a new peak in wind power production, hitting 7,305 MW during the 20:50 dispatch interval on Thursday, 4th August 2022. This surpasses the previous record of 7,111 MW set in July 2022 and highlights the significant impact of recent windy conditions across the region. Alongside celebrating this achievement, the report underscores the importance of addressing low production periods and ensuring adequate dispatchable capacity to support the market during wind droughts. This event coincides with the Albanese Government’s new climate legislation passing in the lower house, reflecting the evolving dynamics of energy policy and climate goals. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate but with longer-term implications for capacity planning and energy transition policies. Its root cause is: Windy weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-08-2022. Its affected region is: The National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers relying on the NEM. Its cause type is: Natural phenomena (windy weather). Its causes are: A series of windy weather conditions leading to increased wind energy production, coinciding with legislative developments impacting energy policy.

classified_content: The summary of the news "Today’s presentation to CEC’s Market & Grid Directorate" posted at 01-08-2022 02:43:00 PM is: The article discusses a presentation given to the Clean Energy Council's Market & Grid Directorate about GenInsights21 and a quarterly update for Q2 2022. The presentation analyzed dispatch interval data to inform the Energy Security Board's proposed high-level design of the Capacity Market and reviewed factors contributing to the 2022 Energy Crisis; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). title: AEMO Quarterly Energy Dynamics (QED) released for Q2 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO Quarterly Energy Dynamics (QED) released for Q2 2022" posted at 29-07-2022 07:41:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) released its Quarterly Energy Dynamics (QED) for Q2 2022, highlighting unprecedented challenges in Australia’s energy markets. The report notes a dramatic increase in wholesale electricity spot prices, averaging $264/MWh in the National Electricity Market (NEM), which is significantly higher compared to the previous quarter and the same quarter in the previous year. Factors contributing to this price surge include high international commodity prices, outages in coal-fired generation, an increase in gas-fired generation, fuel supply issues, and an unusually cold start to June across many east coast cities. These challenges underscore the pressing need for a transition to more reliable and cheaper electricity sources. The spot price volatility is also confirmed, with the frequency of spot prices exceeding $100/MWh increasing dramatically. Commentaries across various media sources echo the findings of the report, underscoring the impacts on the energy market and consumers. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: fuel supply issues (coal, gas), turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Q2 2022. Its root_cause is: High international commodity pricing, coal-fired generation outages, and increased gas-fired generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 29-07-2022 07:41:00 AM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants, electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Multiple factors contributing to market instability. Its causes are: High international commodity prices, coal-fired generation outages, increased gas-fired generation, cold weather conditions.

title: AEMO QED highlights (at least) Eight Factors contributing to extreme price outcomes in Q2 2022
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO QED highlights (at least) Eight Factors contributing to extreme price outcomes in Q2 2022" posted at 29-07-2022 01:00:00 AM is: The AEMO's Quarterly Energy Dynamics report for Q2 2022 outlines several factors contributing to extreme electricity price outcomes during the period, notably during the 2022 Energy Crisis and the enforced price cap and market suspension from June 12. Key contributors included high international gas and thermal coal prices, reduced availability of coal-fired generation due to maintenance and outages, and physical fuel supply and hydrological constraints. Other factors affecting prices were early cold weather increasing demand, high fuel prices, periods of low renewable generation, and network constraints affecting inter-regional transfers. The compounding effect of these issues led to significant electricity price volatility and challenges within the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Q2 2022. Its root_cause is: High international fuel prices and supply constraints in coal and gas. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12th June 2022 (for specific events within the quarter). Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia, and Victoria in Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and supply-driven factors. Its causes are: High international fuel prices, reduced coal-fired generation availability, physical fuel and hydrological constraints, early cold weather increasing demand, fuel price rises, low renewable energy generation, and network constraints.

title: Spot price volatility in South Australia Thursday morning 28th July – second morning in a row
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price volatility in South Australia Thursday morning 28th July – second morning in a row" posted at 28-07-2022 07:47:00 AM is: The news discusses significant spot price volatility in South Australia, with prices exceeding $4,000/MWh and peaking above $12,000/MWh during the 07:30 dispatch interval. This price surge is attributed to low renewable generation due to insufficient wind and it being too early for solar, coupled with high market demand and constrained imports, leading the region to rely on gas-fired generation. The situation mirrors a similar occurrence on Wednesday morning.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, affecting spot prices during early morning intervals. Its root_cause is: Low renewable generation combined with high demand and constrained imports. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-07-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Generation and demand imbalance. Its causes are: Low wind generation, reliance on gas-fired power, high market demand, constrained imports.

title: Non-credible contingency event (and ~40MW loss of load) in country NSW early this morning
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Non-credible contingency event (and ~40MW loss of load) in country NSW early this morning" posted at 25-07-2022 07:43:00 AM is: The article describes a significant power supply loss in country New South Wales (NSW) due to an unplanned outage affecting several power lines and locations, as reported by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) in a market notice. Approximately 40 MW of load was lost, impacting areas such as Broken Hill and Balranald. The cause of this non-credible contingency event remains unknown at the time of reporting. AEMO did not instruct load shedding. The event's impact and causes are still under investigation; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, as the event led to an immediate loss of power supply. Its root_cause is: Unknown at the time of reporting. Its accident scale is: Approximately 40 MW load loss. Its dates is: 25/07/2022. Its affected region is: Country NSW, specifically areas like Broken Hill and Balranald. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Non-credible contingency event. Its causes is: Unplanned outage in multiple 220 kV power lines and synchronous condensers, exact cause undetermined.

title: Gas supply constraints spill into the NEM – two Victorian gas-fired generators directed offline
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Gas supply constraints spill into the NEM – two Victorian gas-fired generators directed offline" posted at 20-07-2022 05:37:00 PM is: The AEMO intervened in the Victorian gas market by directing two gas-fired generators offline due to low storage levels at Iona, which posed a threat to system security. This measure is expected to last until the generators can secure adequate gas supplies or the threat is mitigated. The notice indicates heightened gas demand, with usage significantly exceeding levels from the previous year. AEMO issued multiple notices regarding gas supply shortfalls and threats to system security, forecasting potential issues from 11/07/2022 to 30/09/2022. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues (gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 11/07/2022 to 30/09/2022. Its root_cause is: Low gas storage levels at Iona. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-07-2022. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Gas-fired generators, electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Fuel supply disruption. Its causes are: Low storage level at Iona, high demand for gas-fired generation.

title: AEMO’s ‘Threat to System Security’ in the gas market in relation to Iona storage levels
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO’s ‘Threat to System Security’ in the gas market in relation to Iona storage levels" posted at 20-07-2022 09:09:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a 'Threat to System Security' warning due to declining gas storage levels at Iona, which poses a risk of supply shortfalls this winter. As of July 17, Iona's storage inventory had decreased from 11.6 PJ to 10.2 PJ and is projected to reach 6 PJ by August 6, leading to potential supply capacity reductions and increased curtailment risks during peak demand. AEMO has activated the Gas Supply Guarantee mechanism to secure additional gas from Queensland to support the National Electricity Market until 30 September 2022 or until the threat subsides. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Until 30 September 2022 or when gas supplies are deemed sufficient. Its root cause is: Depletion of Iona gas storage levels. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-07-2022, with the situation being monitored since 11-07-2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Gas-powered electricity generators and consumers relying on gas supply. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Low Iona underground gas storage inventory and projected depletion rates leading to a potential supply shortfall.

title: New all-time record for wind output, NEM-wide on Saturday 16th July 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New all-time record for wind output, NEM-wide on Saturday 16th July 2022" posted at 17-07-2022 03:27:00 PM is: On Saturday evening, the National Electricity Market (NEM) achieved a new all-time record for wind power output, surpassing 7,000MW for the first time, with a precise measurement of 7,111MW during the 21:50 dispatch interval. This significant output accounted for just over 27% of the total grid demand, which increased as the grid demand continued to decline overnight. This record surpasses the previous one set on 31st May, which reached 6,853MW; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, predominantly affecting the evening and overnight intervals on 16th July 2022. Its root cause is: High wind generation output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-07-2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural Operational Condition. Its causes are: High wind generation leading to increased wind power output.

title: Burst of extreme prices across the NEM on July 14th 2022
author: Dan Lee

This event contributes to the ongoing analysis of the winter 2022 period in the NEM; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (immediate impact on electricity prices). title: Price volatility on Monday evening 11th July with ‘Market Demand’ near 31,000MW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility on Monday evening 11th July with ‘Market Demand’ near 31,000MW" posted at 11-07-2022 07:54:00 PM is: The news highlights a price volatility event on Monday evening across all mainland regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM) with electricity prices exceeding $1000/MWh. This volatility was attributed to tight supply/demand conditions with the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) dropping to 12%, and at its lowest, 10.77% during the 18:20 dispatch interval. The market demand peaked at 30,823MW at 18:15, and price volatility began at 17:25, continuing into the evening with further alerts about low IRPM. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: Short-term, occurring within a few hours on 11th July 2022. Its root_cause is: Tight supply/demand balance with low reserve margins. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-07-2022. Its affected region is: All mainland regions of the NEM. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers subject to spot market prices. Its cause type is: Market-based supply and demand dynamics. Its causes are: High market demand nearing 31,000MW and reduced reserve margins leading to price volatility.

title: File creation times for P5MIN drop, prior to the end of the Market Suspension
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "File creation times for P5MIN drop, prior to the end of the Market Suspension" posted at 09-07-2022 09:16:00 PM is: The article discusses the increased latency in NEMDE's P5MIN predispatch file creation during the period from June 12 to June 24, 2022, which coincides with the Market Suspension due to exceeding the Cumulative Price Cap (CPT) in the QLD region and subsequent Administered Pricing. The latency drop, seen in both P5MIN and DISPATCHIS files, was a performance concern that was monitored over the last 18 months with various updates from AEMO. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: June 12, 2022, to June 24, 2022. Its root_cause is: Market Suspension and exceeding of CPT affecting predispatch file creation times. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-07-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia, Victoria. Its affected key users are: Data feed clients. Its cause type is: Market operational disruption. title: AEMC published update on Compensation Claims under APC
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC published update on Compensation Claims under APC" posted at 09-07-2022 07:35:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) released an update on July 4, 2022, regarding claims for compensation following the path from the Cumulative Price Threshold being reached in June 2022. The AEMC acknowledged energy market participants for submitting their compensation claims under the Administered Pricing Period and detailed the steps involved in processing these claims, including providing supporting documentation. The compensation framework applies to generators supplying energy during administered price periods, allowing them to claim direct and opportunity costs for any incurred losses. The update highlights two batches of claims filed up to July 1, 2022. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: Reaching the Cumulative Price Threshold due to market conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: Claims received 17 June – 1 July 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia, Victoria. Its affected key users is: Market participants and eligible generators. Its cause type is: Market mechanism response. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Reaching the Cumulative Price Threshold and subsequent implementation of the Administered Price Period.

title: AER published ‘Semi-Scheduled Generator Compliance Bulletin and Checklist’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER published ‘Semi-Scheduled Generator Compliance Bulletin and Checklist’" posted at 09-07-2022 07:13:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) released a new Semi-Scheduled Generator Compliance Bulletin and a Checklist, aiming to guide Semi-Scheduled Generators (SSG) on compliance with National Electricity Rules. These documents outline expectations, particularly highlighting the differences between SSGs and Scheduled Generators due to the reliance on weather conditions. The bulletin encourages best practices for registration, forecasting, dispatch, and data provision. The AER urges market participants to review and update their practices for scalability and sustainability, noting the importance of manual oversight in automated systems to ensure compliance. The guidance is intended to prevent the need for SSGs, such as wind and solar farms, to transition fully to Scheduled status, incurring additional costs; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Compliance guidance release for semi-scheduled generators. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 09-07-2022 07:13:00 PM. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Semi-Scheduled Generators such as wind and solar farms. Its cause type is: Regulatory update. Its causes is: Release of compliance guidelines and checklist for SSGs by AER.

title: Morning volatility in South Australia on Friday 8th July 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Morning volatility in South Australia on Friday 8th July 2022" posted at 08-07-2022 10:01:00 AM is: A notable period of volatility occurred in South Australia's electricity market on the morning of July 8, 2022. The volatility was influenced by a moderate amount of wind production and increased supply from dispatchable plants such as gas, liquid, and batteries. A key factor contributing to this situation was an outage on the Heywood to South East 275kV line, impacting the transfer of power to the Victoria region despite price differences, due to the invocation of the 'I-HYSE' Constraint Set by AEMO. This outage is scheduled to last until Saturday evening and resume for part of Monday, affecting the interconnector's capacity and contributing to price variations.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, scheduled until Saturday evening with further effects part of Monday. Its root_cause is: Outage on Heywood to South East 275kV line causing constraints on interconnection. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-07-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Transmission line outage. Its causes are: Moderate wind production, increased dispatchable plant supply, Heywood 275kV line outage, and 'I-HYSE' Constraint Set impacting regional interconnector flows.

title: AEMO published (final of) High Level Design for IESS
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO published (final of) High Level Design for IESS" posted at 07-07-2022 08:00:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has released the final version of the 'High Level Design' for the Integrated Energy Storage System (IESS) implementation as of July 7, 2022. This follows an earlier draft of the design published in December 2021, with feedback submissions due by February 11, 2022. The document includes key options and classifications relevant to the IESS, notably captured in table 2; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until the rule change implementation in June 2024. Its root_cause is: Development and implementation of IESS policy by AEMO. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: July 7, 2022. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy implementation.
