title: Reviewing price patterns in Q2 2022 … anatomy of an Energy Crisis
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reviewing price patterns in Q2 2022 … anatomy of an Energy Crisis" posted at 06-07-2022 08:41:00 PM is: The article reviews the significant shifts in energy price patterns during Q2 2022, marking an unprecedented peak in the history of the National Electricity Market (NEM). Traditionally, Q2 had lower energy demands, making it suitable for outages. However, Q2 2022, dubbed the epicenter of the '2022 Energy Crisis,' dramatically deviated from this norm with record-setting average prices across various regions such as Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, and Tasmania. Factors contributing to this surge include the five-minute settlement rule and increased price volatility. The analysis observes fewer prices in the normal range, indicating heightened volatility and risk in the market. Additionally, the incidence of high price excursions and negative pricing underscores significant structural shifts in market operations. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather, turbine/generator failures, grid/system instability, fuel supply issues (coal, gas), coal-fired policies, gas pipeline failures. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term within Q2 2022. Root cause is: Market instability and increased volatility due to five-minute settlement adjustments and broader systemic pressures. Accident scale is: Unknown. Dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 06-07-2022 08:41:00 PM. Affected region is: National Electricity Market regions including Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, and Tasmania. Affected key users are: Electricity market stakeholders, including utilities, policymakers, and consumers. Cause type is: Market operational changes and systemic pressures. Causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Transition to five-minute settlement, heightened market volatility, structural changes in price distributions, increase in high and negative price incidences.

title: Alinta Energy submits Rule Change temporarily request to raise the Administered Price Cap (from $300/MWh to $600/MWh)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Alinta Energy submits Rule Change temporarily request to raise the Administered Price Cap (from $300/MWh to $600/MWh)" posted at 06-07-2022 07:28:00 PM is: Alinta Energy submitted a Rule Change request to the Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC), proposing a temporary increase of the Administered Price Cap (APC) from $300/MWh to $600/MWh across all National Electricity Market (NEM) regions. This is intended to reflect the higher short-run marginal costs currently faced by many generators. The request, submitted on 1st July 2022, includes a suggested sunset period of 12 months, though it acknowledges that the AEMC might determine a different suitable period considering ongoing processes like the 2022 Reliability Standard and Settings Review. The request is part of wider discussions on market reform initiatives due to rising fossil fuel costs impacting energy prices. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Temporary impact expected over a 12-month period, subject to AEMC determination. Its root cause is: Rising short-run marginal costs for generators. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-07-2022 (post date); 01-07-2022 (submission date). Its affected region is: All National Electricity Market (NEM) regions in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy generators and possibly consumers affected by energy price adjustments. Its cause type is: Economic/Market adjustment. Its causes are: Proposed increase in APC to match rising generator costs due to fossil fuel price increases.

title: RERT dispatched in QLD – at 17:00 on Tuesday 5th July 2022
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "RERT dispatched in QLD – at 17:00 on Tuesday 5th July 2022" posted at 05-07-2022 02:51:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) dispatched the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) in the Queensland region to ensure the power system remains secure and reliable. This intervention commenced at 17:00 on July 5, 2022, and was projected to last until 21:00, though it concluded earlier at 18:00. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, lasting only during the dispatch period. Its root_cause is: Potential grid/system instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-07-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Preventative measure to ensure stability. Its causes are: Anticipated power system instability managed via RERT dispatch by AEMO.

title: Significant (~1,300MW) drop in Available Generation in QLD on Tuesday morning 5th July
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Significant (~1,300MW) drop in Available Generation in QLD on Tuesday morning 5th July" posted at 05-07-2022 12:56:00 PM is: The article reports a sharp decrease in available generation in Queensland on the morning of July 5, 2022. Between 08:20 and 08:30, available power generation dropped by approximately 1,306MW, equating to a 14% reduction. This unexpected drop, compounded by miscalculations in demand forecasts, resulted in electricity prices spiking to the market price cap of $15,500/MWh. Speculation suggests that the shutdown of the Wivenhoe generator contributed to this decrease, although it does not fully account for the magnitude of the drop. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition, urging an immediate market response. Additionally, there was a constraint violation involving increased power imports from New South Wales to Queensland, which exceeded typical allowable limits. The cumulative price at publication was noted to be slightly below the elevated cumulative price threshold. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, with potential ongoing scrutiny and adjustments. Its root_cause is: Unexpected drop in available generation and incorrect demand forecast. Its accident scale is: Statewide (QLD). Its dates are: 05-07-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Generation shortfall and forecasting inaccuracies. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Shutdown of Wivenhoe generator, incorrect AEMO demand forecasts, increased power imports via QNI interconnector.

title: RERT may be triggered this evening – Tuesday 5th July 2022?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "RERT may be triggered this evening – Tuesday 5th July 2022?" posted at 05-07-2022 07:51:00 AM is: The article discusses potential market interventions by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) due to increasing electricity prices and reserve shortfalls in Queensland. The Cumulative Price has exceeded $1,000,000, and ongoing cold and cloudy weather reduces solar power generation, exacerbating supply issues. A note from AEMO indicates that Reserve Trader options may be implemented to maintain system reliability, with forecast Lack Of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) for Queensland predicted on 5th July 2022 during morning and evening hours. Spot prices are significantly elevated above $5,000/MWh, and the situation may lead to further market responses or interventions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: immediate, as interventions are being considered for the day. Its root cause is: extreme weather and insufficient supply relative to demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-07-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown, but likely to include wholesale electricity market participants and potentially consumers facing higher prices. Its cause type is: Supply-demand imbalance. Its causes are: Cold and cloudy weather conditions, significant strain on solar power production, elevated demand, and high cumulative price levels leading to potential intervention needs by AEMO.

title: Actual LOR1 and $15,500/MWh in the QLD region on a cold/dreary Monday evening 4th July 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Actual LOR1 and $15,500/MWh in the QLD region on a cold/dreary Monday evening 4th July 2022" posted at 04-07-2022 06:30:00 PM is: Electricity prices in the Queensland region of the National Electricity Market (NEM) have surged to the Market Price Cap of $15,500/MWh on 4th July 2022 due to several contributing factors, including constrained electricity imports from New South Wales (NSW), low solar energy production, and an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) situation. The electricity market demand has exceeded historical norms, exacerbated by constrained flows over the Queensland-New South Wales Interconnector (QNI) due to the 'N^^Q_LS_VC_B1' and 'Q>>TRSP_BKSP_MESP' constraint equations, with some impacts expected to persist until 25th August 2022. Additionally, the peak solar capacity factor was significantly low, at around 9%, further compounding the tight supply. This highlights the challenges faced with both renewable energy production variability and system constraints. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to Medium term, potentially persisting until the end of August 2022. Its root_cause is: Constrained electricity imports and low renewable production. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-07-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and retailers in the QLD region. Its cause type is: Multiple, including system constraints and low solar production. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Constrained imports from NSW due to network limitations and low solar generation capacity.

title: Cloud cover and cold temperatures spark high prices in QLD on Monday morning 4th July 2022
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Cloud cover and cold temperatures spark high prices in QLD on Monday morning 4th July 2022" posted at 04-07-2022 01:47:00 PM is: A combination of extensive cloud cover over Queensland's east coast and notably cold temperatures across the state, particularly in the southeast, led to significant spikes in electricity prices. The price reached $9,500 at the 10:40 AM dispatch interval, persisting above $1,000 for the next several hours. The cloud cover resulted in reduced output from utility-scale solar farms, while cold temperatures increased operational demand due to reduced rooftop solar output and increased heating needs. These factors caused Queensland's midday operational demand to rise significantly above recent levels.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, as price spikes occurred on the same day due to the described weather conditions. Its root cause is: Extreme weather conditions, specifically cloud cover and cold temperatures affecting solar output and increasing demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-07-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Weather-induced supply and demand imbalance. Its causes are: Cloud cover reducing solar farm output, cold temperatures increasing demand.

title: Seasons of low wind and solar output will influence the shape of the NEM
author: Geoff Bongers
classified_content: The summary of the news "Seasons of low wind and solar output will influence the shape of the NEM" posted at 30-06-2022 05:50:00 PM is: The article discusses how extended periods of low wind and solar output, referred to as “seasons of low wind and solar,” impact the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. These renewable droughts have been observed mainly during winter months, requiring significant planning involving large storage capacity, renewable overbuild, and thermal backup plants to maintain grid stability. The authors highlight a stress test from May 2010 that demonstrated the need for substantial preparedness and coordination among states to manage such phases efficiently. Ultimately, the solution involves balancing increased renewable resources, enhanced interconnection among states, and robust storage systems to minimize costs while ensuring reliable energy supply.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Prolonged; possibly spanning multiple months, mainly from May to August. Its root_cause is: Periods of low wind and solar renewable energy output. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-06-2022. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Energy generators and grid operators within the NEM. Its cause type is: Natural climate variability. Its causes are: Low wind and solar energy production during specific seasons, particularly winter, necessitating increased storage and backup systems.

title: On the fringes: the outer edges of the NEM remain the most prone to volatile MLFs
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "On the fringes: the outer edges of the NEM remain the most prone to volatile MLFs" posted at 30-06-2022 02:29:00 PM is: The news article discusses the challenges faced by the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia, particularly focusing on Marginal Loss Factors (MLFs), which are crucial for determining electricity prices by accounting for energy lost during transmission. The length and sparsity of the NEM lead to specific issues with electrical losses, affecting pricing signals and revenue calculation for power generators, especially those located on the fringes of the grid. These MLF values, which can be volatile, impact investor confidence and project viability due to changes in effective electricity pricing. The article also highlights regional issues, using examples from Far North Queensland and South-West New South Wales, where generation constraints and MLF degradation have occurred due to increased energy infrastructure. Potential solutions such as Renewable Energy Zones, transmission upgrades, and market reforms are discussed to better manage losses and incentivize appropriate generation siting.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: The inherent design and capacity constraints of the NEM, along with the introduction of new energy generation in remote areas. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-06-2022. Its affected region is: Regions on the fringes of the National Electricity Market, including Far North Queensland and South-West New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Electricity generators and investors operating on the fringes of the NEM. Its cause type is: Systemic and operational. Its causes are: Sparse grid infrastructure, increased remote generation, MLF volatility, and network congestion.

title: AEMO releases 2022 Integrated System Plan (ISP)
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases 2022 Integrated System Plan (ISP)" posted at 30-06-2022 11:25:00 AM is: The AEMO released the 2022 Integrated System Plan, illustrating the urgent need for a significant energy transformation in Australia from fossil fuels to renewables. The ISP outlines potential scenarios for this transition and stresses the importance of accelerating investment in renewable energy projects to ensure reliable electricity supply. Coverage of the ISP release includes analyses and reports from various media outlets emphasizing the rapid change required in Australia’s energy infrastructure. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: The need for an energy transition due to evolving energy policies and market demands. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-06-2022. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy policymakers, infrastructure developers, and renewable energy investors. Its cause type is: Energy policy decision. Its causes are: Release of the 2022 Integrated System Plan highlighting a strategic shift towards renewable energy and faster coal exit.

title: Market volatility on Monday 27th June 2022
author: Paul McArdle

title: Two alternate theories of why capacity was withdrawn
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two alternate theories of why capacity was withdrawn" posted at 25-06-2022 09:45:00 PM is: This article discusses two theories explaining the withdrawal of capacity by a significant number of DUIDs after the Administered Pricing commenced on June 13, 2022, leading to AEMO's decision to suspend the market on June 15, 2022. Theory #1 suggests this was due to compensation arrangements, as the $300/MWh Administered Price Cap might not cover operating costs, prompting participants to seek a more favorable compensation regime through AEMO for directions rather than administered pricing. Theory #2 considers the need to manage limited fuel or water supplies without depleting reserves due to current market conditions, driving generators to withdraw capacity to avoid running out. Both theories highlight crucial aspects of participant behavior in response to market conditions and raise questions about how compensation regimes influence operational decisions. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term, specifically during the period surrounding the market suspension from June 13-15, 2022. Its root_cause is: High operating costs not covered by the administered price cap and the need to manage limited energy resources effectively. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 13-06-2022 and 15-06-2022. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), South Australia (SA), and Victoria (VIC) in Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Economic and resource management. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Insufficient administered price cap to cover short-run marginal costs, 2) Management of limited fuel or energy stocks due to market conditions, 3) Preference for AEMO-directed compensation regime.

title: Quick review of bids – looking back 16 days over APC and Market Suspension
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "Quick review of bids – looking back 16 days over APC and Market Suspension" posted at 25-06-2022 09:34:00 PM is: The article provides a detailed review of the administered pricing and market suspension events that affected the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) due to the '2022 Energy Crisis'. Starting from 12th June, regions like QLD, NSW, SA, and VIC exceeded the Cumulative Price Threshold leading to administered pricing, eventually resulting in AEMO suspending the market on 15th June. The article explores how wind and solar plant contributions dwindled during peak demands and highlights constraints in energy capacity rather than outright shortages. The removal of price caps on 23rd June saw capacity entering the market again, with implications for the ongoing energy transition discussed. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 12th June to 24th June 2022. Its root_cause is: Energy constraints and volatility in the market. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12th June 2022 to 24th June 2022. Its affected region is: QLD, NSW, SA, VIC regions in Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and regulatory. Its causes are: Exceeding Cumulative Price Threshold, administered pricing, and energy supply constraints during market suspension.

title: Eighteen month trend of daily average spot prices highlights extreme volatility in Q2 2022

The volatility is highlighted in the context of the 2022 Energy Crisis, mainly attributed to coal-fired unit outages and high fuel costs. The market analysis indicates the need for a broader consideration of factors influencing the electricity market to ensure a successful energy transition. The article also emphasizes that reviews focusing solely on recent market cap and suspension periods might not be sufficient for long-term solutions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: AEMO estimates RERT payments on Tue 14th, Wed 15th and Fri 17th June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO estimates RERT payments on Tue 14th, Wed 15th and Fri 17th June 2022" posted at 24-06-2022 03:43:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) released estimates of the Reserve Trader (RERT) payments made prior to and during a market suspension that commenced on 15th June 2022 due to a forecast Lack of Reserve (LOR) Condition. On 14th June, RERT was dispatched in NSW for 900 MWh with payments totaling $21.6 million at a unit cost of $24,000/MWh. On 15th June, in response to LOR Conditions 2 and 3, RERT was used in both NSW and QLD, resulting in a combined payment of $34.3 million across 1,724.5 MWh. On the night of 17th June, similar dispatches in NSW cost $29.9 million for 1,416.83 MWh. These costs significantly exceeded the Market Price Cap. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: Forecast Lack of Reserve Conditions leading to a need for additional reserves. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-06-2022, 15-06-2022, 17-06-2022. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational issue. Its causes are: Forecast Lack of Reserve (LOR) Conditions 2 and 3 prompting activation of reserves.

title: Market Suspension to end at 14:00 on Friday 24th June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Market Suspension to end at 14:00 on Friday 24th June 2022" posted at 24-06-2022 07:49:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) announced that the suspension of the National Electricity Market (NEM) in several regions will end at 14:00 on 24 June 2022. This suspension had been in place due to previous instability and monitoring requirements. The resumption of normal market operations signifies that the system is stable enough to handle regular market activities. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Accident scale: No accident reported; this is related to market operations. Dates: 24-06-2022. Affected region: New South Wales, Queensland, South Australia, Tasmania, and Victoria. Affected key users: Electricity market participants and consumers in the affected regions. Cause type: Operational measures in response to instability. Causes (a summarized list of cause events): Market suspension due to instability and monitoring requirements.

title: Voltage disruption (apparently) stemming from Port Augusta Renewable Energy Park Thursday morning 23rd June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Voltage disruption (apparently) stemming from Port Augusta Renewable Energy Park Thursday morning 23rd June 2022" posted at 23-06-2022 05:48:00 PM is: The voltage disruption in South Australia in the early hours of the morning was linked to the Port Augusta Renewable Energy Park. While the exact root cause remains unknown, it resulted in noticeable output reduction from the Park, which had previously been generating consistently high power. Speculation around potential causes includes multiple site-related issues, as highlighted in a news report. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO continue to watch for forecast LOR3 … e.g. MN99072 in preliminary forecast for Thu 30th June in SA" posted at 23-06-2022 02:25:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued Market Notices about a forecast Lack of Reserve Level 3 (LOR3) condition in the South Australian region for Wednesday and Thursday of next week. This forecast indicates potential electricity shortages, but with pricing now back to normal, AEMO expects the predictions to improve as the days near, leveraging tools such as ez2view v9.4.2.62 for enhanced forecast visibility; Its relevance level is: Level 2. title: Administered Pricing ended at 04:00 on Thursday 23rd June 2022 (and an FCAS price glitch)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "Administered Pricing ended at 04:00 on Thursday 23rd June 2022 (and an FCAS price glitch)" posted at 23-06-2022 07:58:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) announced the lifting of the price caps across the National Electricity Market (NEM), which were earlier placed under the $300/MWh Administered Price Cap due to high fuel costs and potential fuel supply issues. The market observed prices bouncing back above this cap, indicating increased energy price levels. Despite this adjustment in administered pricing, AEMO reported an issue of zero values for Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) prices, though energy prices remained unaffected. AEMO is addressing the FCAS pricing anomaly, which persisted briefly from 04:00 to 08:00 on 23 June 2022.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: fuel prices. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and ongoing as price adjustments occur and any further issues with FCAS are addressed. Its root cause is: Heightened short run marginal cost (SRMC) of some fuel sources and potential fuel supply constraints impacting pricing. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-06-2022. Its affected region is: NEM regions including QLD, NSW, SA, and VIC. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the affected NEM regions. Its cause type is: Market adjustment and technical issue. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Lifting of administered price caps due to fuel cost considerations and a technical glitch affecting FCAS pricing.

title: (Somewhat artificially) low NEM-wide IRPM on Wednesday evening 22nd June … looking forward to possible easing of Suspension on Friday morning

classified_content: The summary of the news "(Somewhat artificially) low NEM-wide IRPM on Wednesday evening 22nd June … looking forward to possible easing of Suspension on Friday morning" posted at 22-06-2022 06:55:00 PM is: The article discusses the artificially low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) observed on the evening of June 22nd across the National Electricity Market (NEM), with a notable drop to 7.27%. It also mentions the anticipation surrounding the potential lifting of the market suspension as early as Friday morning, June 24th. This situation arises amid a series of 'Actual LOR2' alerts and restrictions in the market, caused by pricing caps and limited bidding volumes. The article speculates on the impact of these events on cumulative price movements, considering potential price cap lifts and added capacity to NEMDE dispatch. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. title: AEMO MN98805 continues Market Suspension (possibly only till Friday 04:05), but with different prices on a path out
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO MN98805 continues Market Suspension (possibly only till Friday 04:05), but with different prices on a path out" posted at 22-06-2022 10:05:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) announced a staged approach to lifting the suspension of the National Energy Market (NEM), which was originally enacted due to market instability. Starting at 4 AM on June 23, 2022, the pricing regime will shift from Market Suspension Schedule Pricing to Dispatch Pricing across regions including New South Wales, Queensland, South Australia, Tasmania, and Victoria. AEMO aims to allow the market to set prices autonomously, with fewer constraints on the dispatch engine, leading to a possible complete lifting of the suspension. This change follows a seven-day suspension period where 4,000 MW of generation returned to operation. AEMO will monitor conditions to ensure market stability before fully resuming normal operations. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, as the change starts at 4 AM on June 23, 2022, with possible complete lifting by June 24, 2022. Its root_cause is: Market instability and generation outages. The accident scale is: Unknown. The dates are: 22-06-2022 and 23-06-2022 with potential impact till 24-06-2022. The affected region is: New South Wales, Queensland, South Australia, Tasmania, and Victoria. The affected key users are: Power consumers and generators in the affected regions. The cause type is: Operational/systemic. The causes are: Market suspension due to grid/system instability, improved generation conditions with 4,000 MW returning online, and AEMO's phased approach to lifting market suspension.

title: Pricing and dispatch during market suspension
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "Pricing and dispatch during market suspension" posted at 21-06-2022 09:30:00 PM is: The article discusses the suspension of the National Electricity Market (NEM) by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) starting on 15 June 2022 due to high prices and market instability. During the suspension, AEMO employed the Market Suspension Pricing Schedule, applying certain caps and adjustments to settlement prices, including a notable $300/MWh cap. The article further explains the complex measures undertaken for dispatch, noting the roles of thermal and hydro capacity and market direction impacts, especially about wind and solar curtailment. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Beginning 15 June 2022 (ongoing at the time of the article). Its root cause is: High electricity prices and market instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15 June 2022 (start of market suspension). Its affected region is: All regions of the NEM. Its affected key users are: Generators in the electricity market, particularly those dealing with wind and solar energy. Its cause type is: Systemic and operational. Its causes are: Market instability, intervention measures by AEMO, and cap on electricity prices.

title: Climbing out of Market Suspension
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "Climbing out of Market Suspension," posted on 21-06-2022 09:00:00 PM, is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has announced a pathway to end the current suspension of the National Electricity Market (NEM), following extreme challenges in maintaining a stable supply-demand balance during the ongoing 2022 Energy Crisis. This decision is driven by the fact that the stringent spot pricing levels observed beyond seven days ago are subsiding, and cumulative prices across regions are close to the threshold. However, AEMO remains cautious, as underlying supply-demand challenges persist, influencing its staged process and assessment criteria for resuming normal operations. Ultimately, the resumption is contingent on AEMO's confidence in the market's operability without returning to conditions that prompted the suspension, focusing on market stability and reserve monitoring in NEM regions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/System Instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, as resumption is contingent on stability indicators. Its root_cause is: Static administered price cap amid escalating spot prices for coal and gas. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-06-2022. Its affected region is: NEM regions (QLD, NSW, SA, and VIC). Its affected key users are: Energy utilities and industry stakeholders. Its cause type is: Market operation and pricing challenges. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Static administered price cap, escalating spot prices for coal and gas, tight supply-demand balance during the 2022 Energy Crisis.

title: Increased visibility of LOR3 forecasts
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "Increased visibility of LOR3 forecasts" posted at 20-06-2022 08:59:00 PM is: The article discusses the challenges in predicting Lack of Reserve Level 3 (LOR3) forecast conditions amid the 2022 Energy Crisis in Australia, particularly in the regions of New South Wales, South Australia, Victoria, and Queensland during mid-June. The escalation of the situation occurred due to administered pricing mechanisms falling below the Short-Run Marginal Cost (SRMC) of some supply sources, resulting in market suspension and uncertainty. It highlights the measures taken by WattClarity to provide real-time data updates and improve visualization through live-updating widgets and ez2view software enhancements. The Australian Energy Regulator's reminder to generators to fulfill their market obligations underscores fears of possible blackouts due to generator capacity withdrawal. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term impact during and immediately after the crisis period around mid-June 2022. Its root_cause is: Price caps below SRMC causing generators to withdraw capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-06-2022 to 16-06-2022. Its affected region is: New South Wales, South Australia, Victoria, and Queensland. Its affected key users are: Residential consumers and small businesses. Its cause type is: Market and regulatory factors. Its causes are: Price cap issues, generator withdrawal, market suspension, administered pricing mechanisms.

title: ESB releases ‘high-level design paper’ for Capacity Mechanism
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "ESB releases ‘high-level design paper’ for Capacity Mechanism" posted at 20-06-2022 01:19:00 PM is: The Energy Security Board (ESB) has released a 'high-level design' document outlining a proposed Capacity Mechanism to address challenges posed by energy market changes. It focuses on eligibility for participation, forecasting and procurement centralization, obligations for capacity providers, interstate trade, and cost management. The document has drawn varied opinions in media coverage, particularly regarding coal and gas's role. The ESB aims to reconcile issues arising from market shifts towards renewable energy while maintaining reliable power. Submissions are due by 25 July 2022, with further stakeholder discussions anticipated. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: The need to address the changing dynamics of the energy market and ensure reliable power supply. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-06-2022. Its affected region is: Unspecified in the document, but it concerns the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy providers and consumers in the National Electricity Market. Its cause type is: Policy change. Its causes are: The evolving energy mix, sustainability concerns, and the need for a market mechanism that addresses both energy availability and reliability.

title: ACCC’s ‘Inquiry into the National Electricity Market’ (from 23rd May) published (+ an Addendum) Monday 20th June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "ACCC’s ‘Inquiry into the National Electricity Market’ (from 23rd May) published (+ an Addendum) Monday 20th June 2022" posted at 20-06-2022 12:17:00 PM is: The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) has released the ACCC’s seventh report on the National Electricity Market (NEM), dated 23rd May 2022, along with an addendum prepared on 17th June 2022. The report addresses substantial shifts in the Australian energy markets, price impacts, and the ACCC's role in monitoring compliance with electricity pricing laws. The ACCC, together with the Australian Energy Regulator (AER), has communicated with energy retailers to remind them of their pricing obligations, including adherence to the 'Default Market Offer' and enforcing legislation against anti-competitive conduct. The ACCC emphasizes vigilance over market behaviors in upcoming months. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Report on electricity market conditions and regulatory enforcement. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-06-2022. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity retailers and consumers. Its cause type is: Regulatory and market monitoring actions. Its causes are: Significant changes in energy markets, ACCC inquiry findings, and enforcement of compliance and competition laws.

title: File creation times at AEMO for DISPATCHIS and P5MIN increase 50%, coincident [CAVEAT] with Administered Pricing and Market Suspension
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "File creation times at AEMO for DISPATCHIS and P5MIN increase 50%, coincident [CAVEAT] with Administered Pricing and Market Suspension" posted at 18-06-2022 is: The article discusses a significant 50% increase in the latency of file creation times at AEMO for key files related to market dispatch, coinciding with the commencement of Administered Pricing and a market suspension in the National Electricity Market (NEM). The delays, observed over a 30-day trend, have stirred concerns over the system's ability to manage extreme operational stress, particularly during a period marked by extraordinary pressure on personnel and infrastructure. Despite potential correlations, the article underscores that these increases in latency do not necessarily mean causation. These events coincide with a broader energy crisis exacerbating stress on the grid and support systems like NEMDE. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its root_cause is: The increased operational stress in the NEM with market suspension and administered pricing. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-06-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia, and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Wholesale market participants, including generators, retailers, and traders within the NEM. Its cause type is: System/operational stress. Its causes are: Increased operational stress marked by administered pricing, market suspension, and corresponding increases in AEMO file creation times.

title: TransGrid 330kV substation near Tallawarra Power Station on fire [updated ~20:30]
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "TransGrid 330kV substation near Tallawarra Power Station on fire [updated ~20:30]" posted at 18-06-2022 05:55:00 PM is: A fire erupted at the TransGrid 330kV substation near the Tallawarra Power Station in the Illawarra region, resulting in the power station coming offline for around two hours. The fire did not significantly impact electricity supplies, and the plant returned to service later in the evening. Despite initial media confusion, there was no fire at the Tallawarra Power Station itself. Authorities reassured that electricity supplies were stable, although residents in the vicinity were advised to stay indoors due to possible hazards. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, approximately two hours. Its root_cause is: Fire at the substation. Its accident scale is: Potentially significant due to the temporary outage but ultimately minor as supplies were not impacted. Its dates are: 18-06-2022. Its affected region is: Illawarra region, near Tallawarra Power Station. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: External accident (fire). Its causes are: Fire at TransGrid 330kV substation caused a temporary outage of the Tallawarra Power Station.

title: RERT dispatched through Friday night in NSW
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "RERT dispatched through Friday night in NSW" posted at 18-06-2022 09:26:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) activated the Reserve Trader in the NSW region to maintain system reliability over an 8-hour period from 20:00 on 17th June 2022 until 04:10 on 18th June 2022. This extensive dispatch marked the longest contiguous activation of the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) yet recorded. The market intervention aims to ensure a reliable power supply amidst challenges faced by the grid. Energy users might face additional compensation costs due to the activation. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, as the intervention was for a specific 8-hour period. Its root_cause is: System reliability concerns requiring market intervention. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17/06/2022 to 18/06/2022. Its affected region is: NSW. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Market intervention for reliability. Its causes are: Increased demand and potential system instability leading to the need for RERT dispatch.

title: Wafer thin margin Friday evening, NEM-wide [caveat = based on generation bid available for NEMDE pricing/dispatch]
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wafer thin margin Friday evening, NEM-wide [caveat = based on generation bid available for NEMDE pricing/dispatch]" posted at 17-06-2022 11:47:00 PM is: The article highlights a particularly low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) across the National Electricity Market (NEM), which reached only 3% late Friday evening, and a mere 1.18% at one interval, indicating a critical shortage in reserve capacity. This unprecedented low margin was attributed to a significant volume of generation being bid as unavailable in the NEM Dispatch Engine (NEMDE) pricing run, despite technically remaining capable of generating. This decision by generators seems motivated by a desire to preserve scarce onsite energy resources amid the ongoing 2022 Energy Crisis. The situation stirred concerns across the market, prompting hopes for cautious and effective management by control rooms to avoid instability; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (Friday evening with immediate recovery thereafter). Its root_cause is: Low IRPM due to generation bid unavailable in NEMDE and energy resource preservation practices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-06-2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) covering QLD, NSW, SA, and VIC. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational management and market conditions. Its causes are: Low reserve margin, generation bid unavailable in NEMDE, preservation of scarce energy resources.

title: Stepping to suspension
author: Allan O'Neil
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "Stepping to suspension" posted at 17-06-2022 03:40:00 PM is: The automatic capping of NEM spot market prices began on June 12th in Queensland and subsequently in NSW, SA, and VIC, leading to a reduction in generation capacity offered to the market. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) eventually suspended the spot market on June 15th due to risks associated with the declining capacity and increased direction efforts. The suspension was a result of several generators withdrawing significant volumes due to limited fuel or water supplies, forcing remaining generators into a less effective re-pricing mechanism, creating a "rush for the exits." Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Generators withdrawing capacity due to limited fuel or water supplies amidst market price capping. Its accident scale is: Wide-scale market impact. Its dates are: June 12th to June 15th, 2022. Its affected region is: NEM regions including Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia, and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in NEM regions. Its cause type is: Supply-side constraints. Its causes are: Market price cap, limited fuel supplies, and withdrawal of generation capacity.

title: AEMC notice ‘Eligible participants have [until Wednesday 22nd June] to notify the AEMC of their intent to claim compensation’
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC notice ‘Eligible participants have [until Wednesday 22nd June] to notify the AEMC of their intent to claim compensation’" posted at 16-06-2022 09:52:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) has announced that eligible participants affected by the administered pricing period during the 2022 Energy Crisis have until Wednesday 22nd June to notify their intent to claim compensation. Participants must have incurred costs surpassing their total revenue and can claim for direct or opportunity costs. The notification involves specifying the applicable administered price periods and regions, and details of the costs. Following notification, a series of steps for claim assessment are detailed, which include information provision and AEMC's formal assessment. The urgency of the process is emphasized due to the tight deadlines involved; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions . Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Expected short-term, following the claims process and potential compensations due to incurred costs. Its root_cause is: Market suspension and administered pricing period during the 2022 Energy Crisis necessitating compensation claims due to cost-revenue imbalance. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-06-2022; deadline 22-06-2022. Its affected region is: NSW, SA, VIC, and QLD. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants incurring costs during the administered pricing period. Its cause type is: Administrative/Regulatory directive. Its causes are: Imbalance between incurred costs and revenue during administered pricing, necessitating compensation processes.

title: Observation 5/22 from GenInsights21 – the rise of ‘Just in Time’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Observation 5/22 from GenInsights21 – the rise of ‘Just in Time’" posted at 16-06-2022 is: The article discusses the 2022 Energy Crisis, highlighting the increasing reliance on 'Just in Time' supply chains within the electricity sector. The transition towards instantaneous renewable energy supply is presented as a core challenge, demanding serious risk assessments concerning energy storage and supply chain resilience. Historical examples, such as the Texas 2021 energy crisis, Millennium Drought, and Hazelwood closure, are utilized to emphasize the risks of inadequate fuel stockpiling and storage. The need for robust energy storage systems is underscored to prevent future crises.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal, gas). The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: 2022 and beyond. The root cause is: Transition to 'Just in Time' energy supply chains with insufficient backup/storage measures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-06-2022. Its affected region is: Primarily Australia, with references to international examples like Texas, USA. Its affected key users are: Energy producers and consumers relying on stable electricity supplies. Its cause type is: Systemic supply chain and energy storage inadequacies. Its causes are: Over-reliance on 'Just in Time' supply, inadequate energy storage, and insufficient contingency planning in the transition to renewable energy sources.

title: Trended bidding from BESS discharge (NEM-wide) … highlighting APC and Market Suspension
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022 - June - QLD (12th) and NSW, SA and VIC (13th) hit Cumulative Price Cap
classified_content: The summary of the news "Trended bidding from BESS discharge (NEM-wide) … highlighting APC and Market Suspension" posted at 16-06-2022 07:35:00 PM is: The article discusses the response of Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) to administered pricing and market suspension in the National Electricity Market (NEM) amid the 2022 Energy Crisis. Due to limited energy storage, batteries generally bid at high prices to conserve energy. With the imposition of price caps following market suspension, the bidding volume from BESS decreased significantly. The report emphasizes that this behavior is a rational response to energy constraints rather than an instance of 'price gouging.' The article highlights energy constraints faced by various technologies, including hydro, gas, and coal-fired generators, not due to capacity but energy availability. Batteries play a crucial role in providing Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS), which also influences their bidding strategies.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate-term during crisis period. Its root_cause is: Limited energy availability across technologies (battery, hydro, gas, coal) due to market and external factors. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-06-2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions, including NSW, SA, and VIC. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants, energy consumers, regulators. Its cause type is: Energy availability constraints, price cap impositions. Its causes are: Energy constraints across different technologies, administered pricing, market suspension.

This instance is part of a larger issue being progressively analyzed, involving several similar events. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. classified_content: The summary of the news "Steps taken by AEMO, so we could (hopefully!) dodge the LOR3 bullet this evening" posted at 13-06-2022 06:51:00 PM is: AEMO took critical steps to address an electricity supply shortfall in Queensland on 13 June 2022, which was forecasted due to a previously reached cumulative high price threshold. The measures involved directing generators under the National Electricity Rules to relieve the LOR3 condition and maintain system security and reliability. The intervention was prompted by a significantly high electricity price accumulation of $1,359,100, leading to a capped price of $300/MWh and reduced available offers from generators. The actions taken were effective in securing enough generation to meet demand and avoid load shedding. AEMO continues to monitor conditions across the National Electricity Market (NEM) for additional potential reserve shortfalls. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, primarily affecting the day of the notice. Its dates are: 12-06-2022 and 13-06-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, with monitoring across the broader NEM. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Economic and operational. Its causes are: High cumulative electricity prices, administered price cap, reduced generator offers leading to forecasted supply shortfall.

title: Reviewing the 500MW drop in Available Generation in QLD on Friday evening 10th June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reviewing the 500MW drop in Available Generation in QLD on Friday evening 10th June 2022" posted at 11-06-2022 06:04:00 PM is: This article examines the events surrounding a 500MW drop in available generation in Queensland on the evening of 10th June 2022. Significant market volatility occurred as the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued an 'Actual LOR2' warning, indicating a lack of reserve generation in the region. Price spikes were observed, reaching a maximum of $15,100/MWh, due to unforeseen generator availability reductions and strategically delayed rebidding activities by power stations, as seen at Tarong and Braemar Power Stations. The complexity and rapid required response in the dispatch intervals further contributed to market actions. Additionally, changes in bids from various power stations trying to optimize profitability under high forecast prices led to these fluctuations. The rebidding reason cited by operators generally revolved around uneconomic operating conditions or technical issues, resulting in a decrease in available generation and subsequent price increases. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, particularly that evening. Its root cause is: Rapid reduction in generation capacity due to rebidding and technical issues. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10th June 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and operational dynamics. Its causes are: Unexpected generator availability drop, strategic rebidding due to market conditions, and technical issues at specific stations.

Overall, this article highlights the complexities of managing electricity market stability amid high price events. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. title: A closer look at coal-fired generation availability
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "A closer look at coal-fired generation availability" posted at 10-06-2022 03:45:00 PM is: The article discusses the challenges faced by coal-fired generators in Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM), highlighting a disconnect between projected and actual availability. At the start of the quarter, only 64% of practical maximum capacity was available, with significant outages at key plants, exacerbated by forced outages and extended maintenance. A persistent shortfall of 2-4 GW was noted due to unplanned outages, longer maintenance, derating, and economic withdrawal, potentially linked to fuel supply issues. The situation illustrates ongoing performance issues within coal-fired fleets and the need for improved reliability as severe weather could strain supply.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). The severe frost and temperatures dropping below 5C across the southeast contributed to a peak in electricity demand, hitting over 32,000 MW on June 7th, the highest for June in over a decade. The AEMO also noted a new winter record for operational demand in Queensland on June 9th, 2022. The demand is, however, forecasted to decrease into the following week. It highlights the challenges in determining when interconnectors reach their limits due to complex constraint equations and numerical discrepancies in data reporting. The constraints on interconnectors like Heywood and QNI can result in price spikes or drops due to regional energy flows being restricted. The article underscores the importance of correctly interpreting data on interconnector constraints to make informed market decisions. Improvements are underway in data visualization tools like ez2view to assist users better in understanding interconnector constraints. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Symphony in 3 Movements" posted at 08-06-2022 06:57:00 PM is: The article discusses the fluctuations in electricity spot prices in Queensland on June 8, 2022, amid the ongoing 2022 Energy Crisis. It describes the pattern of prices throughout the day, highlighting a morning high due to increased market demand and limited solar PV contribution, a midday price crash as solar PV ramped up, and an evening surge when solar PV contribution ceased. The article further explores broader challenges in energy transition, touching on discussions about a capacity market and diverging opinions on coal usage, as well as the need for solutions to address the pricing volatility and energy supply challenges, exacerbated by the crisis. title: What (I believe) is the real cause of our energy crisis – and what should we do?
author: Tristan Edis
classified_content: The summary of the news "What (I believe) is the real cause of our energy crisis – and what should we do?" posted at 08-06-2022 11:02:00 AM is: The east coast energy market is grappling with extraordinarily high gas and electricity prices, which are primarily attributed to high international market prices set by gas and coal producers. Contrary to some claims, these high prices are not due to shortfalls in wind and solar generation. Coal output has declined in 2022 compared to 2021, partly due to unplanned outages of coal power plants and planned maintenance. Gas prices have risen because of increased demand for gas-powered generation to compensate for coal shortfalls. The article argues that solutions like extending coal and gas generation life might deter investment in more sustainable alternatives like batteries. Instead, speeding up grid connection processes for ready wind and solar plants and enhancing energy efficiency in rental properties could offer more effective relief and reduce reliance on gas. A short-term levy on gas windfall profits could fund immediate consumer relief and long-term efficiency improvements. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Fuel prices. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Throughout 2022. Its root_cause is: High international market prices for gas and coal and coal power plant outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-06-2022 11:02:00 AM. Its affected region is: East coast of Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Economic and operational. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: High international prices for gas and coal, coal power plant outages, increased gas demand for power generation.

title: La Nina is the main cause of high electricity and gas prices
author: David Leitch
classified_content: The summary of the news "La Nina is the main cause of high electricity and gas prices" posted at 06-06-2022 01:31:00 PM is: The article discusses how the La Niña weather pattern has significantly disrupted coal production across Australia's east coast, leading to reduced coal sales, notably in New South Wales. This disruption has been exacerbated by floods affecting mines and rail routes, contributing to soaring electricity and gas prices. Domestic coal supplies have decreased as producers prioritize international markets due to higher prices, stressing domestic coal generators. The shortfall in coal production has increased demand for gas generation by about 15%, constrained further by limited pipeline capacity. However, a recovery in coal production is anticipated within the next six months as weather conditions stabilize, potentially alleviating some of the pressure on electricity and gas prices. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Next six months. Its root_cause is: La Niña weather pattern causing significant rainfall and floods, disrupting coal production. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-06-2022 01:31:00 PM. Its affected region is: East coast of Australia, particularly New South Wales and Queensland. Its affected key users are: Domestic coal generators and electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Natural and market constraints. Its causes are: La Niña weather causing flooding and coal production disruptions, reduced coal supply prioritizing international markets, increased demand for gas generation exceeding pipeline capacities.
