title: Quick review of a price spike on Sunday evening 5th June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Quick review of a price spike on Sunday evening 5th June 2022" posted at 05-06-2022 08:33:00 PM is: The article provides an analysis of a sudden price spike in South Australia's electricity market, reaching the Market Price Cap of $15,100/MWh at 18:05 NEM time, primarily resulting from a rapid and unexpected ramp-down of the Pelican Point power station possibly due to a technical issue. The situation was compounded by constraints on the Heywood interconnector, ongoing maintenance outages, and a lack of reserve condition reported by AEMO. This led to increased volatility in a day characterized by strong wind production and reduced gas-fired generation. In addition to Pelican Point, other generators and batteries adjusted their output significantly, while a wind farm's output dropped unexpectedly; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (on the day of the event). Its root_cause is: Rapid ramp-down of Pelican Point power station possibly due to a technical issue. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 05-06-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Energy consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Technical issue and grid constraint. Its causes is: - Rapid ramp down of Pelican Point power station - Constraint on Heywood interconnector - Lack of Reserve condition.

title: AEMO announces a ‘Protected Event’ for South Australia from 19:15 on Saturday 4th June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO announces a ‘Protected Event’ for South Australia from 19:15 on Saturday 4th June 2022" posted at 04-06-2022 07:54:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a market notice declaring a 'Protected Event' in South Australia commencing from 19:15 on June 4th, 2022. This declaration is in response to forecasted damaging winds by the Bureau of Meteorology that could lead to the loss of multiple transmission elements and disconnection of generation in the region. As a preventative measure, AEMO will manage the power system's stability by restricting flows on the Heywood interconnector, reminiscent of actions stemming from past incidents like the SA System Black event. This constraint is expected to cause low electricity prices in South Australia due to high winds and limited export market access. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until further notice. Its root_cause is: Forecasted damaging winds causing potential grid instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 04-06-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Forecasted damaging winds, potential loss of multiple transmission elements, and subsequent generation disconnection.

title: Updated long-range trend of monthly wind statistics (maximum, average and minimum) for the end of May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Updated long-range trend of monthly wind statistics (maximum, average and minimum) for the end of May 2022" posted at 04-06-2022 07:28:00 PM is: The article reports on new records in wind energy production, noting a significant increase in maximum aggregate wind production with new peaks set on 31st May 2022. The production rose to 6,637 MW in the morning and further to 6,853 MW later that day, surpassing the previous record from July 2021. While wind production saw these highs, it also experienced lows, with output dropping to 258 MW just days earlier, illustrating significant short-term variability. The article further notes oscillations in the monthly capacity factor and its stabilization around the 30% long-run average, facilitated by increased installed capacity and geographical diversity of wind farms. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term variability within May 2022. Its root_cause is: Natural variability in wind conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 04-06-2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) region, primarily southern areas. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural factors. Its causes is: Increased wind capacity, geographical diversity, natural wind variability, and extreme weather conditions anticipated.

title: Tasmania sees its own spell of volatility on Saturday evening 4th June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tasmania sees its own spell of volatility on Saturday evening 4th June 2022" posted at 04-06-2022 06:21:00 PM is: On June 4, 2022, Tasmania experienced unusual market volatility, reaching the Market Price Cap with a spot price of $15,100/MWh. The market demand rose to 1,466MW, nearing its all-time maximum of 1,781MW. The cumulative price in Tasmania was $779,209, approaching levels seen in NSW and QLD, while the Basslink was unable to import power into Tasmania. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: High electricity demand and constraints in inter-regional power imports. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-06-2022. Its affected region is: Tasmania. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and infrastructure constraints. Its causes are: High spot prices, increased electricity demand, inter-regional transfer limitations.

title: Anatomy of an Energy Crisis, Australian style – part 1
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Anatomy of an Energy Crisis, Australian style – part 1" posted at 03-06-2022 07:36:00 PM is: The article outlines the significant events contributing to what is being referred to as Australia's worst energy crisis in 50 years. These pivotal events range from the 'Millennium Drought' from 2007-2008, which affected hydro facilities and coal generators, the 'Carbon Tax' period between July 2012 and June 2014 under the Gillard-led Federal Labor Government, the closure of the Hazelwood power station in March 2017, which had an outsized and long-lived impact on the market, to the Callide C4 Catastrophe of May 25, 2021, which caused a major disruption and marked the beginning of the 2022 Energy Crisis. The crisis intensified significantly post the Callide C4 explosion, with unusually high energy prices. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability, turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Starting from May 25, 2021, with escalation into 2022. Its root_cause is: A combination of historical environmental, regulatory, and technical events culminating in a severe supply-demand imbalance. Its accident scale is: Significant, with widespread implications on the national electricity market (NEM). Its dates are: Ranges from 2007 through key events in 2012-2014, March 2017, May 2021, and notably post-April 2022. Its affected region is: Australia, specifically involving regions within the NEM. Its affected key users are: National electricity distributors, major industrial users, and residential consumers. Its cause type is: Multiple interrelated factors including environmental, regulatory, and mechanical failures. Its causes are: The Millennium Drought, carbon tax implementation, Hazelwood closure, Callide C4 explosion, and a significant rise in energy demand coupled with supply constraints.

title: Volatile prices on Friday evening 3rd June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Volatile prices on Friday evening 3rd June 2022," posted at 03-06-2022 05:43:00 PM, is: The article highlights an evening of volatile energy prices across the National Electricity Market (NEM) on June 3, 2022. Prices surged above $1,000/MWh in the regions of QLD, NSW, and VIC, while South Australia's prices were significantly lower due to constraints on the Tailem Bend to South-East 275kV line. This constraint is expected to affect the market until June 7, 2022. The high demand, notably in NSW, and the return of wind production affected price variances across regions, with AEMO issuing multiple LOR1 notices. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Until at least June 7, 2022, when the constraint is expected to be resolved. Its root cause is: Transmission constraint and high demand relative to supply resources. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-06-2022 to 07-06-2022. Its affected region is: NEM, including QLD, NSW, VIC, TAS, and SA. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and market participants in NEM. Its cause type is: Technical constraint and demand dynamics. Its causes are: High market demand, transmission line constraint, varying wind production levels, and market dynamics due to constraint resolutions.

title: AEMO continues to monitor gas supplies – with respect to high demand
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO continues to monitor gas supplies – with respect to high demand" posted at 02-06-2022 06:17:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) is closely monitoring gas supply conditions due to a tight supply-demand balance in the National Electricity Market (NEM) following a potential gas supply shortfall event. Gas producers in Queensland are responding to shortages in southern states with near-capacity gas flows to New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia. High gas demand is driven by colder weather and power generation needs. Price caps remain in place in Sydney and Brisbane, and Victoria following recent high price thresholds. Despite the tight conditions, electricity supply is deemed sufficient to meet forecast demand. AEMO is collaborating with market participants and governments to manage the situation. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, until at least 7 June 2022. Its root_cause is: High gas demand driven by colder weather and power generation needs. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date): 02-06-2022. Its affected region is: Southern states of Australia, including NSW, VIC, SA. Its affected key users are: Gas and electricity consumers in affected regions. Its cause type is: Operational and demand-related. Its causes are: Activation of the Gas Supply Guarantee mechanism, high gas demand for heating and power generation, administered price caps due to previous high price thresholds.

title: Very tight supply-demand (NEM wide!) on Thursday 2nd June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Very tight supply-demand (NEM wide!) on Thursday 2nd June 2022" posted at 02-06-2022 05:43:00 PM is: The article discusses a tight supply-demand balance across the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia on the evening of June 2, 2022. Despite the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) cancelling earlier warnings for a low reserve condition (LOR2), electricity prices surged above $4,000/MWh in all regions at the 17:45 dispatch interval. The market demand climbed due to typical evening factors such as lighting and heating, alongside unavailable rooftop PV, with demand reaching 30,412MW nationwide. The supply was constrained by reduced wind production, with reliance on hydro, gas, liquid, and a limited number of batteries to meet the demand. The Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) fell to 10%, indicating a narrow reserve capacity. Rescheduling significant transmission outages mitigated the risk of more severe conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, with potential ongoing effects. Its root_cause is: Increased market demand and constrained supply sources. Its accident scale is: NEM-wide. Its dates are: 02-06-2022. Its affected region is: NEM, Australia. Its affected key users are: NEM electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Operational. Its causes are: Tight supply-demand balance, high market prices, increased evening demand, low wind production, limited generation resources.

title: First evening price spike above $1,000/MWh – at 16:40 on Thursday evening 2nd June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "First evening price spike above $1,000/MWh – at 16:40 on Thursday evening 2nd June 2022" posted at 02-06-2022 04:45:00 PM is: The article reports a significant price spike in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on the afternoon of June 2, 2022, with electricity prices exceeding $1,000/MWh in New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia. Queensland and Tasmania saw prices nearly reach this level as well. This occurred despite a NEM-wide market demand of 27,808 MW and available generation of 34,053 MW. Previously, Tasmania's electricity prices spiked earlier in the day at 11:55 and 15:20; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Market reactions to demand and supply conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 02-06-2022. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, Tasmania, and Queensland. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in the affected regions. Its cause type is: Economic/market-driven. Its causes is: Market demand exceeding available generation leading to price spikes.

title: Morning price volatility on Thursday 2nd June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Morning price volatility on Thursday 2nd June 2022" posted at 02-06-2022 07:56:00 AM is: The article reports on the morning price volatility observed across all regions in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on June 2, 2022, driven by widespread cool weather that increased market demand, and compounded by coal unit outages that reduced available capacity. Specific details highlight a significant unavailability ratio of coal units in Queensland and New South Wales, and a drop in wind output, which added pressure on the fuel mix. The situation posed challenges to the grid, despite having more than 20% spare capacity, due to steep ramp rate requirements amidst growing energy transition dynamics. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term, over the course of the day. Its root_cause is: Increased market demand due to cool weather and significant coal unit outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 02-06-2022. Its affected region is: Southern regions of the NEM, primarily Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: External (weather) and internal (coal unit outages). Its causes are: Cool weather increasing demand, high coal unit unavailability, and reduced wind output.

title: Explaining dispatch (and price setting) regarding Eraring Unit 2 on 30th May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Explaining dispatch (and price setting) regarding Eraring Unit 2 on 30th May 2022" posted at 01-06-2022 08:35:00 PM is: The article discusses the complexities of price setting in the National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly focusing on the dispatch and pricing of Eraring Unit 2 on May 30, 2022. It delves into how simplistic models of bid stacking fail to capture the intricate nuances involved in price determination. A rebid from Origin Energy regarding an 'ash hopper issue' led to changes in bidding capacity, causing a reduction in Eraring's output and affecting the price setting process, with TUMUT3 unit becoming instrumental in this regard. This resulted in a detailed examination of various factors, including bid bands and pricing across different regions. Tools like ez2view were highlighted for their role in helping stakeholders understand these complex situations. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and short-term. Its root cause is: Operational adjustments due to a technical issue ('ash hopper issue') at Eraring Unit 2. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30th May 2022. Its affected region is: NSW, with implications in QLD, VIC, TAS, and SA. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and stakeholders. Its cause type is: Operational/Technical issue. Its causes are: Ash hopper issue, rebidding by Origin Energy, changes in dispatch requirements and price settings.

title: Update to the precarious situation forecast for tomorrow – Thursday 2nd June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Update to the precarious situation forecast for tomorrow – Thursday 2nd June 2022" posted at 01-06-2022 06:09:00 PM is: The report highlights a potential gas supply shortfall alert issued by AEMO for the VIC, SA, and TAS regions due to cold weather expected on 2nd June 2022. This shortfall raises concerns about demand for gas in domestic heating and gas-fired electricity generation during the winter months. While initial warnings of low reserve capacity conditions (LOR2) were issued for QLD and NSW for the evening of 2nd June, they were later canceled, though concerns remain regarding electricity supply tightness, especially in response to increased heating demands. This situation emphasizes challenges in the energy market during winter and future energy transitions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: fuel supply issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, impacting the evening of 02/06/2022. Its root cause is: Cold weather increasing demand for gas and electricity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 02-06-2022. Its affected region is: VIC, SA, TAS, QLD, NSW. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Extreme weather and fuel supply. Its causes are: Notification of a potential gas supply shortfall in VIC, SA, TAS due to cold weather; Initial LOR2 forecast for QLD and NSW due to low reserve capacity, later canceled.

title: NEM-wide IRPM drops below 15% on Wednesday 1st June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM-wide IRPM drops below 15% on Wednesday 1st June 2022" posted at 01-06-2022 06:00:00 PM is: The article reports on a rare occasion where the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) across the National Electricity Market (NEM) fell below the 15% yellow alert trigger level. This occurred despite the market demand being below 31,000MW. This situation highlights a significant concern regarding reserve plant margin levels within the NEM; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term; Its root_cause is: Low reserve plant margin levels; Its accident scale is: Unknown; Its dates are: 01-06-2022; Its affected region is: NEM-wide; Its affected key users are: Unknown; Its cause type is: System operational challenge; Its causes are: IRPM dropping below the threshold due to system conditions.

title: Tight supply-demand balance forecast for QLD and NSW on Thursday 2nd June 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tight supply-demand balance forecast for QLD and NSW on Thursday 2nd June 2022" posted at 31-05-2022 08:34:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued market notices forecasting a tight supply-demand balance for Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) on Thursday, 2 June 2022, due to a Lack Of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition. The notices highlight potential capacity shortages from 1700 to 2100 hours in NSW and from 1730 to 2000 hours in QLD on the same date. Additional warnings were given for NSW on 6 June and both regions on 7 June. A substantial drop in wind power forecasts for 2 June is cited as a contributing factor. AEMO is seeking market responses to mitigate the situation.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, around 2 June 2022, with potential impacts on 6 and 7 June 2022. Its root_cause is: High demand and reduced wind power generation capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2 June 2022, with additional events on 6 and 7 June 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in QLD and NSW, possibly including large industrial users. Its cause type is: Demand-supply imbalance and reduced renewable energy input. Its causes are: High electricity demand exceeding 30,000 MW across the National Electricity Market (NEM) and a significant decrease in wind power.

title: Tight supply-demand, so price volatility, in NSW and QLD on Tuesday 31st May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tight supply-demand, so price volatility, in NSW and QLD on Tuesday 31st May 2022" posted at 31-05-2022 05:56:00 PM is: The electricity markets in New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD) experienced price volatility on May 31, 2022, due to a tight supply-demand balance. During the 17:30 dispatch interval, electricity prices rose above $1,000/MWh in these regions, driven by a climb in NSW demand and a constrained fuel mix involving wind, solar, hydro, and gas peakers. Transmission constraints further complicated supply, with critical constraint equations invoked during an outage involving a key power line. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) for NSW, indicating reserve shortfalls until 8 PM. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, immediate impact on May 31, 2022. Its root_cause is: Tight supply-demand balance and transmission constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31/05/2022. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Queensland. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and generators in NSW and QLD. Its cause type is: Market and operational constraints. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: High electricity demand in NSW, reduced solar output, transmission constraints due to an outage, and reserve shortfalls leading to LOR1 declaration.

title: New record production from wind on Tuesday 31st May 2022 (larger still in the afternoon!)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New record production from wind on Tuesday 31st May 2022 (larger still in the afternoon!)" posted at 31-05-2022 02:51:00 PM is: The article highlights a record-breaking wind production milestone in the National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia on May 31, 2022, due to strong winds induced by an 'Antarctic blast' weather pattern. The peak production was reached twice on the same day, first in the early morning at 6,637MW and later in the afternoon at 6,853MW, with significant contributions from the Victorian region. This high level of wind generation accounted for approximately 32% of the Grid Demand during the early morning hours. The event also resulted in localized power outages in northern New South Wales. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: Strong winds from an ‘Antarctic blast’ weather pattern. Its accident scale is: Localized power outages in northern NSW. Its dates are: 31-05-2022. Its affected region is: Northern NSW, National Electricity Market areas (VIC, SA, NSW, QLD, TAS). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Strong winds leading to increased wind energy production and subsequent power outages in some areas.

title: What would happen if…?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What would happen if…?" posted at 30-05-2022 06:36:00 PM is: The article raises concerns about the potential impact of the Cumulative Price Threshold (CPT) being exceeded in the Victorian Declared Wholesale Gas Market (DWGM) and the electricity market. It discusses the possibility of reaching the Administered Price Cap (APC) level of $300/MWh in the electricity market due to recent volatility in prices. The article questions what would happen if the APC is hit, particularly since $300 may not cover the Short-Run Marginal Cost (SRMC) of coal and gas, leading generators to bid at the Value of Lost Load (VoLL), but clearing at $300 may not be economical. Readers are invited to offer insights on this issue. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Extreme market volatility affecting price caps. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-05-2022. Its affected region is: Victoria. Its affected key users are: Generators and electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Economic/Market condition. Its causes are: Potential exceedance of the Cumulative Price Threshold and volatility in electricity prices.

title: Tight supply-demand balance on Monday evening 30th May 2022 drives prices high and IRPM low
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Tight supply-demand balance on Monday evening 30th May 2022 drives prices high and IRPM low" posted at 30-05-2022 06:25:00 PM is: On the evening of 30th May 2022, electricity prices soared, driven by a tight supply-demand balance in the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia, particularly impacting Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). Prices spiked above $9,000/MWh in a significant dispatch interval and were exacerbated by an early sunset coupled with cooler temperatures, reducing solar generation and increasing demand for artificial lighting. Reserve plant margins fell below critical thresholds, triggering alerts, partially due to limitations on the VIC-NSW interconnector. This situation resulted from various factors including low solar output due to weather conditions and constraints on power flow between regions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, particularly on the evening of 30th May 2022, with potential ongoing implications. Its root_cause is: High demand due to earlier sunset and cooler temperatures combined with supply constraints and interconnector limitations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-05-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in QLD and NSW, particularly those reliant on lower-cost electricity supply. Its cause type is: Systemic factors and infrastructure constraints. Its causes are: Early sunset, cooler temperatures, limited solar output, VIC-NSW interconnector constraints, increased demand for artificial lighting, and generation limitations.

title: AEMO notice about ‘Upcoming cold weather and gas market operation from 30/05/2022 to 06/06/2022’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO notice about ‘Upcoming cold weather and gas market operation from 30/05/2022 to 06/06/2022’" posted at 30-05-2022 05:42:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a notice regarding the forecast of cold weather across several National Electricity Market (NEM) regions from 30th May to 6th June 2022. This has prompted AEMO to highlight an administered market state in certain gas markets, urging market generators to review local weather forecasts and update their capacity and energy availability in response. The notice mentions temperature forecasts for several regions, including Adelaide, Melbourne, Bankstown, and Hobart, which are expected to be lower than usual. AEMO's advisory suggests potential implications for electricity and gas markets in response to the weather conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 30/05/2022 to 06/06/2022. Its root_cause is: Cold weather forecast. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30/05/2022 to 06/06/2022. Its affected region is: South Australia, Victoria, New South Wales, Tasmania. Its affected key users are: Market Generators. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: Cold weather forecast leading to market operation adjustments.

title: AEMO suspects possible LOR3 Lack of Reserve conditions in forecast for QLD next Monday 6th June 2022 … but it was data input error!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO suspects possible LOR3 Lack of Reserve conditions in forecast for QLD next Monday 6th June 2022 … but it was data input error!" posted at 30-05-2022 04:15:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) initially forecasted potential Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) and Level 3 (LOR3) conditions for Queensland on 6th June 2022, raising concerns about possible load shedding during specific periods. However, this warning was later deemed invalid due to data input errors, averting any anticipated power supply issues. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Initially for 6th June 2022, but the issue was resolved on 30th May 2022 with no expected impact beyond this date. Its root_cause is: Data input error. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-05-2022 and 06-06-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Human error. Its causes are: Input errors in forecasting reserve conditions for Queensland.

title: Victorian wholesale gas prices hit Cumulative Price Threshold
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Victorian wholesale gas prices hit Cumulative Price Threshold" posted at 30-05-2022 10:45:00 AM is: The Victorian wholesale gas market reached the Cumulative Price Threshold, triggering Administered Pricing measures as reported by Josh Stabler on LinkedIn. Readers are advised to follow the LinkedIn thread for additional comments and developments; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Fuel prices. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: High cumulative gas prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-05-2022. Its affected region is: Victoria. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market reaction. Its causes are: Surpassing of the Cumulative Price Threshold in the gas market.

title: A (temporary?) reprieve from the incessant high prices on Sunday 29th May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A (temporary?) reprieve from the incessant high prices on Sunday 29th May 2022" posted at 29-05-2022 12:58:00 PM is: On May 29, 2022, electricity spot prices in Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia dropped to $0/MWh or below, marking a significant departure from the high prices observed in recent months. The price decline began at 10:15 AM in Queensland and was followed by NSW, VIC, and SA, with Market Demand in these regions colored down in the 'blue zone.' This drop in demand was notably influenced by mild temperatures and increased rooftop solar energy injection, particularly in Queensland, resulting in a low market demand level. It is uncertain how long this reduction in electricity prices will persist. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, possibly several hours to days. Its root_cause is: Mild weather conditions and increased rooftop solar generation reducing grid demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 29-05-2022 12:58:00 PM. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Demand reduction due to external influences. Its causes are: Mild temperatures influencing demand, increased rooftop solar energy generation.

title: Short-term wind lull on Friday 27th May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Short-term wind lull on Friday 27th May 2022" posted at 28-05-2022 10:21:00 AM is: The article discusses a short-term lull in wind production across Australia on May 27, 2022, which coincided with the end of solar production for the day. This situation resulted in elevated electricity prices, reaching around $1,000/MWh in all regions during the evening. The article highlights concerns about such events becoming more frequent and prolonged in the future, especially during periods of low solar production, known as 'Dunkelflaute'. The event serves as a reminder of the challenges in maintaining electricity price stability and supply security as Australia increases its reliance on renewable energy sources. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term. Its root_cause is: low wind conditions leading to reduced wind energy generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27-05-2022. Its affected region is: all regions of Australia in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: electricity consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: natural cause. Its causes are: short-term lull in wind production, end of solar production for the day.

title: Blast off!   Q3 base-load futures contract prices jump $30/MWh to $40/MWh on Wednesday 25th May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Blast off! Q3 base-load futures contract prices jump $30/MWh to $40/MWh on Wednesday 25th May 2022" posted at 26-05-2022 03:17:00 PM is: The article discusses significant increases in Q3 base-load futures prices across various Australian regions, notably Queensland, New South Wales, and Victoria, on May 25, 2022, driven by market conditions and events like contract options expiry causing trading volume surges. title: AER publishes final determination for the 2022-23 Default Market Offer
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER publishes final determination for the 2022-23 Default Market Offer" posted at 26-05-2022 12:22:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) has released its Default Market Offer (DMO) for the 2022-23 financial year, detailing that electricity prices in New South Wales, south-east Queensland, and South Australia will rise for both households and small businesses from July 1, 2022. This increase is attributed to a 41.4% rise in wholesale costs in New South Wales, 49.5% in Queensland, and 11.8% in South Australia over the past year, driven by unplanned thermal generation outages, higher coal and gas input costs, slowed investment in new capacity, and more volatile peak demand. The AER has noted four main factors contributing to the price hike, including transmission congestion. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Starting from 1 July 2022 for the 2022-23 financial year. Its root_cause is: Significant rises in wholesale electricity costs due to various factors including unplanned outages and higher fuel costs. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 26-05-2022. Its affected region is: New South Wales, south-east Queensland, South Australia. Its affected key users are: Households and small businesses. Its cause type is: Economic/technical factors. Its causes are: Thermal generation outages, higher input fuel costs, slowed investment in capacity, increasingly peaky demand, and transmission congestion.

title: Who gets to run when everyone bids the same? A crash course in disorderly bidding and tie-breaking
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "Who gets to run when everyone bids the same? A crash course in disorderly bidding and tie-breaking" posted at 25-05-2022 11:41:00 AM is: The article discusses the tie-breaking principle in the National Electricity Market Dispatch Engine (NEMDE), which is applied when multiple electricity generation units offer the same price, leading to a “disorderly bidding” scenario. This situation often occurs when generators bid their entire volume at the market floor price to maximize their chances of being dispatched, amidst constraints posed by limited transmission capacity or system strength issues. It explains how the tie-breaking is done on a pro-rata basis based on generator capacity, ensuring fair dispatch allocation among generators with similar bids, as demonstrated with the South Australian system strength constraint example. The article provides insights into economic dispatch and the operational intricacies within the NEM during constraints, highlighting challenges in maintaining system reliability and equity among competing generators.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term, affecting dispatch outcomes within the day or over a few days during constraint conditions. Its root_cause is: Generator competition due to transmission and system strength constraints, leading to disorderly bidding at the market floor price. Its accident scale is: Not applicable. Its dates are: 25-05-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity generators operating in the National Electricity Market, particularly in regions with system strength constraints. Its cause type is: Market operational challenge. Its causes are: Disorderly bidding caused by system strength constraints and transmission limitations leading to tie-breaking requirements in dispatch processes.

title: Victoria’s Essential Services Commission increases Electricity Default Offer for Victorian consumers
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Victoria’s Essential Services Commission increases Electricity Default Offer for Victorian consumers" posted at 24-05-2022 08:00:00 PM is: The Essential Services Commission in Victoria announced an increase in the Electricity Default Offer, affecting about seven percent of households and sixteen percent of small businesses, with an average annual bill increase of $61 for households and $270 for small businesses. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Regulatory decision to adjust electricity pricing. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24-05-2022. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Households and small businesses on the Default Offer. Its cause type is: Policy decision. Its causes are: Adjustment of the Default Market Offer by the Essential Services Commission following regulatory analysis and deliberation.

title: Exploring some things that could affect a Large-Scale Solar Farm’s value proposition
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Exploring some things that could affect a Large-Scale Solar Farm’s value proposition" posted at 22-05-2022 08:49:00 PM is: The news article discusses discussions at the CEC’s Large-Scale Solar Forum, highlighting challenges affecting the value proposition of large-scale solar projects in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Key points include the need for flexibility in solar projects to maximize asset value, potential impacts of generation curtailment, the role of storage, and the introduction of a congestion management mechanism. The article emphasizes the importance of considering various performance metrics beyond revenue, suggesting broader value indicators for solar farms. It reflects on the statistical insights provided by the GSD2021 and GenInsights21 reports. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, as it pertains to future project evaluations and potential policy developments. Its root_cause is: The evolving dynamics and requirements of the NEM for solar projects. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19th May 2022 and 22-05-2022. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Large-scale solar farm operators. Its cause type is: Policy and operational factors. Its causes are: The need for solar farms to adapt to changes in the NEM, potential generation curtailment, the evolving storage role, and possible congestion management adjustments.

title: Australian domestic weekly gas prices exceed $30 per GJ for the first time
author: Josh Stabler
classified_content: The summary of the news "Australian domestic weekly gas prices exceed $30 per GJ for the first time" posted at 17-05-2022 03:49:00 PM is: The Australian domestic weekly gas prices have reached a record-breaking level, exceeding AUD$30 per GJ for the first time during the period from 9th to 15th May 2022, with recorded prices of $33.25/GJ in Brisbane STTM, $34.88/GJ in Sydney STTM, $36.06/GJ in Adelaide STTM, and $34.75/GJ in Melbourne DWGM. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Fuel prices. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: Increased demand and supply constraints in the gas market. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 9th to 15th May 2022. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Gas consumers and industries dependent on gas. Its cause type is: Market factors. Its causes are: Rising demand for gas and supply limitations leading to increased prices.

title: Mainland hydros running hard, as a result of the recent volatility
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mainland hydros running hard, as a result of the recent volatility" posted at 17-05-2022 12:28:00 PM is: The article discusses the increased output of mainland hydroelectric units in recent weeks, particularly in May 2022, as a response to market volatility. This surge in hydro generation marks the highest level of utilisation since July 2021, excluding short-lived spikes such as the one on 31st January 2022. The analysis excludes Tasmanian hydro units due to their unique operational factors. The continuous high output in May 2022 surpasses that of the same period in 2021.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: May 2022. Its root_cause is: Recent market volatility. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-05-2022. Its affected region is: Mainland (excluding Tasmania). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market condition. Its causes are: Increased hydroelectric output due to market volatility.

title: Valley Power peaker runs hard during Thursday 12th May and Friday 13th May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Valley Power peaker runs hard during Thursday 12th May and Friday 13th May 2022" posted at 17-05-2022 11:04:00 AM is: The news discusses the operational activity of Snowy Hydro's Valley Power peaking facility in the Victorian region during the period from early morning Thursday 12th May until Friday evening 13th May 2022. During this period, the facility operated at maximum availability and pushed its bids down to –$1,000/MWh, effectively acting as a price taker. The VIC spot price was noted to rarely drop below the $300/MWh ASX cap trigger price, indicating significant price fluctuations in the market. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, specifically 12th-13th May 2022. Its root cause is: Market dynamics driven by operational strategies of the power facility and prevailing spot prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. The dates are: 12-05-2022 to 13-05-2022. The affected region is: Victorian region, NEM. The affected key users are: Energy traders, market analysts, and potentially consumers in the Victorian market. The cause type is: Operational and market-driven. The causes are: High operational availability of the Valley Power peaking facility and its strategic bidding behavior.

title: Morning price volatility in South Australia on Tuesday 17th May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Morning price volatility in South Australia on Tuesday 17th May 2022" posted at 17-05-2022 07:47:00 AM is: This news reports on significant price volatility in the South Australian electricity market on the morning of May 17th, 2022. Price spikes were observed with the spot price reaching $15,010.80/MWh at 07:15 AM and prices exceeding $10,000/MWh prior. Contributing factors include scheduled maintenance during off-peak periods, constrained support from Victoria's interconnectors, a declining contribution from wind energy, and minimal solar input, necessitating increased reliance on gas and liquid-fueled peaking generation. Although a Low Reserve Condition Notice was forecasted, the LOR1 warning was canceled the previous day, while LOR2 warnings remain for the following days.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, observed on the same day, with ongoing implications expected throughout the week due to constraints and generation dynamics. Its root_cause is: Constrained interconnector support, scheduled maintenance, and changing fuel mix dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 17-05-2022 and affecting days include 18th and 19th May. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: System and market operations. Its causes are: Constraints on interconnectors, declining wind and solar contributions, reliance on peaking generation, and maintenance scheduling.

title: Market Volatility (and low IRPM) on Monday evening 16th May 2022 in QLD region
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Market Volatility (and low IRPM) on Monday evening 16th May 2022 in QLD region" posted at 16-05-2022 06:13:00 PM is: The article discusses a significant market volatility event in the Queensland (QLD) electricity market on the evening of May 16, 2022, where electricity prices exceeded $1,000/MWh multiple times, reaching up to $1,201/MWh during the 17:40 dispatch interval. The elevated prices occurred amidst a modest local demand of 7,647 MW and severe constraints on the QNI Export Limiter, which restricted northward energy flows. These constraints were attributed to the ‘N^^Q_LS_VC_B1’ constraint equation. As a result, the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) in the QLD-only region was at a low level of 12%, indicative of a 'red alert' status. Additionally, six coal units were offline, further limiting supply, while solar and wind generation contributed minimally. This tight supply situation was anticipated by earlier AEMO market notices indicating low reserve conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, during and possibly shortly after the reported event. Its root_cause is: Multiple generator outages and transmission constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 16-05-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the QLD region. Its cause type is: Operational and generator outage. Its causes are: Generator outages, transmission constraints, low IRPM, solar and wind generation deficits.

title: Run of price volatility in the QLD Region on Sunday evening 15th May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Run of price volatility in the QLD Region on Sunday evening 15th May 2022" posted at 15-05-2022 06:42:00 PM is: The article reports on a significant price volatility event in the Queensland region of the National Electricity Market (NEM) on May 15, 2022, where electricity prices spiked above $1,000/MWh during the 16:50 dispatch interval due to a forecasted Low Reserve Condition (LOR1). The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) had issued a notice forecasting this tightness in supply due to a lower-than-required reserve margin, with a minimum reserve capacity of 698 MW against the requirement of 730 MW. This price volatility is attributed to the tight reserve conditions which are not isolated to Queensland, but also predicted for South Australia in the following days. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to several days following the event. Its root_cause is: Forecasted low reserve conditions and tight supply margins. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 15-05-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in the QLD region. Its cause type is: System operation and reserve forecasting. Its causes are: Forecast LOR1 condition, limited reserve capacity, and subsequent price spikes in the electricity market.

title: Josh Stabler discusses the ‘timeline of energy scarcity’ at the EUAA conference this morning
author: Josh Stabler
classified_content: The summary of the news "Josh Stabler discusses the ‘timeline of energy scarcity’ at the EUAA conference this morning" posted at 11-05-2022 11:49:00 AM is: Josh Stabler from EnergyEdge presented at the EUAA conference, discussing the timeline of energy scarcity in the National Electricity Market (NEM). He highlighted rising spot and futures prices, with Queensland's spot price averaging $255/MWh from 1st April to 9th May 2022, and forward curves exceeding $100/MWh for the next eight quarters. Other mainland regions of the NEM, including New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia, also experience elevated spot prices. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Medium term. Its root cause is: Rising electricity spot and futures prices in the NEM. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 1 April 2022 - 9 May 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Economic. Its causes are: Rising spot prices, elevated forward curves, and energy market volatility.

title: Morning volatility in South Australia on Wednesday 11th May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Morning volatility in South Australia on Wednesday 11th May 2022" posted at 11-05-2022 08:01:00 AM is: The news highlights a significant surge in spot prices within the South Australian electricity market reaching the Market Price Cap of $15,100/MWh during the 07:50 dispatch interval. This volatility is occurring amidst tightly balanced supply and demand conditions a day before potential LOR3 warnings from AEMO. This situation underscores the ongoing fluctuations within the National Electricity Market (NEM) during Autumn 2022, possibly influenced by wider market instability events. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Tight supply-demand conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 11-05-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market Instability. Its causes is: Tight supply-demand balance and forecasted LOR3 warnings.

title: High prices being served up again in SA on Tuesday evening
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "High prices being served up again in SA on Tuesday evening" posted at 10-05-2022 06:28:00 PM is: On the evening of October 5, 2022, South Australia (SA) experienced severe spot market volatility, with electricity prices soaring into quadruple digits, reaching a peak of $9,941.06 during the 18:00 dispatch interval. This early evening price surge was particularly acute in SA compared to less severe spikes in other regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM) such as Victoria, New South Wales, Queensland, and Tasmania. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-Term. Its root_cause is: Spot market volatility leading to extreme price spikes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-05-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA) within the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes are: Spot market volatility, high demand during early evening hours.

title: Spot market volatility across all 4 mainland regions of the NEM – but particularly South Australia
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot market volatility across all 4 mainland regions of the NEM – but particularly South Australia" posted at 09-05-2022 07:51:00 PM is: This article outlines significant market volatility in the National Electricity Market (NEM) across all four mainland regions, particularly in South Australia, due to skyrocketing spot prices above $1,000/MWh on the evening of May 9, 2022. This volatility appears to be linked to broader challenges in the energy transition and discrete issues in each region, such as temporary forecast load shedding and low solar and wind output in South Australia, widespread rain impacting solar output in Queensland, and reduced wind output at the Coopers Gap Wind Farm. The discussion highlights the multifaceted nature of the electricity market's challenges, urging further exploration into the discrete issues affecting each region. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate impact on May 9, 2022, with ongoing implications throughout the week. Its root_cause is: Energy transition challenges and discrete regional factors affecting electricity supply and demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-05-2022. Its affected region is: All four mainland regions of the NEM, with particular emphasis on South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Multiple contributing factors. Its causes are: Low solar and wind output, forecast load shedding, and specific regional challenges like widespread rain in Queensland and decreased output at Coopers Gap Wind Farm.

title: Transmission outage on ‘Ravine to Yass (2) 330kV line’ still ongoing
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Transmission outage on ‘Ravine to Yass (2) 330kV line’ still ongoing" posted at 09-05-2022 04:28:00 PM is: An ongoing transmission outage on the Ravine to Yass 330kV line has significantly impacted market volatility in Queensland and New South Wales by restricting electricity flows from southern NSW and Victoria to Sydney. Initially scheduled to end by May 7th, the outage's completion has been delayed to May 13th, causing issues with radial constraints and limited flow capacities, consequently affecting electricity prices and flow patterns. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: until at least May 13, 2022. Its root_cause is: an outage on the Ravine to Yass 330kV line. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: May 2, 2022, to May 13, 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: electricity consumers and providers relying on the Sydney RRN. Its cause type is: infrastructure failure. Its causes are: the network outage and its impact on flow capacity and market behavior.

title: Consistently elevated prices as a segue into EUAA National Conference this week
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Consistently elevated prices as a segue into EUAA National Conference this week" posted at 09-05-2022 01:17:00 PM is: The article discusses the persistently elevated electricity spot prices across all regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM) during the 13:05 dispatch interval, attributed to factors such as coal and other unit outages and high spot gas prices. It notes that these price patterns are similar to those observed in recent weeks and are expected to be a key topic at the Energy Users Association of Australia (EUAA) National Conference 2022 in Melbourne. This conference marks the first in-person EUAA event since 2019, with discussions likely focusing on these challenges related to the energy transition; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures, fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Persistent over recent weeks. Its root_cause is: Unit outages and high gas prices. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-05-2022. Its affected region is: All regions of the NEM. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Infrastructure and market conditions. Its causes are: Coal unit outages and high spot gas prices.

title: Drop in forecast wind conditions for Thursday 12th May 2022 briefly triggers forecasts of Load Shedding (LOR3) in South Australia
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Drop in forecast wind conditions for Thursday 12th May 2022 briefly triggers forecasts of Load Shedding (LOR3) in South Australia" posted at 09-05-2022 10:47:00 AM is: The article discusses the brief forecast of a Level 3 Low Reserve Condition (LOR3), indicating potential load shedding in the South Australian region for Thursday 12th May 2022, due to a significant drop in forecasted wind generation. Despite initial forecasts showing a maximum interruption of 113 MW in the morning and 7 MW in the evening, the forecast was canceled later due to improved generation capacity predictions. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) had issued multiple notices indicating fluctuations in predicted renewable energy availability, with none requiring intervention. Increased forecast generation capacity later mitigated the risk of load shedding.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, limited to the forecast period of May 12th, 2022. Its root_cause is: Drop in forecasted wind generation capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-05-2022 to 12-05-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Renewable energy forecast issues. Its causes are: Significant decrease in forecasted wind and solar generation capability.

title: Evening volatility again in QLD and NSW on Thursday 5th May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility again in QLD and NSW on Thursday 5th May 2022" posted at 05-05-2022 05:12:00 PM is: For the fourth consecutive evening, electricity price volatility has affected the Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) regions, with elevated prices also noted in Victoria (VIC) and South Australia (SA). A specific constraint set 'N-RVYS_2', which restricts power flow from VIC to NSW, has been extended until 7th May, contributing to this instability; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, affecting evening prices from 3rd to 7th May 2022. Its root cause is: Extension and modification of the 'N-RVYS_2' Constraint Set affecting electricity flow. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 5th May 2022 - 7th May 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Constraint-induced market volatility. Its causes are: Extension of 'N-RVYS_2' Constraint Set, limited VIC-to-NSW electricity flow.

title: A quick look at the (tight) QLD+NSW supply stack, on Tuesday evening 3rd May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at the (tight) QLD+NSW supply stack, on Tuesday evening 3rd May 2022," posted on 04-05-2022 08:50:00 PM is: The article details the significant electricity price volatility occurring on the evening of 3rd May 2022 in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW), with prices peaking over $10,000/MWh. The volatility stemmed from limited supply in the regions, despite modest demand. Constraint equations and auto-bidding were also highlighted as contributing factors to the price spikes. Additionally, the necessity to consider Frequency Control Ancillary Services (FCAS) amidst energy price surges was emphasized, presenting both a revenue opportunity and potential cost burden. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Supply stack constraints and auto-bidding volatility in QLD and NSW. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: Same date, 03-05-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and suppliers in QLD and NSW. Its cause type is: Market bidding behavior and supply constraints. Its causes are: High electricity prices due to tight supply stack, constraint equations, and changing bid behaviors.

title: Volatility continues in QLD and NSW for Wednesday evening, 4th May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Volatility continues in QLD and NSW for Wednesday evening, 4th May 2022" posted at 04-05-2022 06:18:00 PM is: The article discusses continued volatility in the electricity market in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) on the evening of May 4th, 2022. Prices in these regions neared $10,000/MWh during the 17:40 dispatch interval and exceeded $1,000/MWh earlier in QLD. The lack of discharge from the Wandoan Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) during the evening peak, unlike the previous day, is highlighted as a contributing factor, although further exploration of the causes is not provided in the article; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Price volatility in the evening due to demand and supply conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 04-05-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market-related. Its causes is: High demand, insufficient discharge from Wandoan BESS during peak times.

title: Extended outage of Loy Yang A2 from 15th April 2022
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2022-04 long term outage Loy Yang A2
classified_content: The summary of the news "Extended outage of Loy Yang A2 from 15th April 2022" posted at 04-05-2022 02:26:00 PM is: The Loy Yang A2 unit in Victoria suffered an extended outage beginning on 15th April 2022 due to an electrical fault with the generator, particularly an 'excitation fault' resulting in its offline status since Good Friday. Investigations and repairs are underway, with an estimated return to service by 1st August 2022. The outage costs AGL Energy an estimated $73 million pre-tax, with significant impacts on its financial guidance and market operations. This situation echoes a similar outage in 2019 which resulted in significant financial costs and operational delays. The current outage could lead to an earnings hit and contribute to rising electricity prices in the region due to reduced supply. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/Generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing until at least 1st August 2022. Its root_cause is: An electrical fault with the generator's rotor insulation. Its accident scale is: Significant with potential widespread market impact. Its dates are: 15th April 2022 (outage began) to 1st August 2022 (expected return to service). Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: AGL Energy, electricity consumers in Victoria, market stakeholders. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: Generator 'excitation fault', electrical fault with rotor insulation.

title: April market prices surge
author: Carl Daley
classified_content: The summary of the news "April market prices surge" posted at 04-05-2022 02:07:00 PM is: The article reports an unprecedented surge in market prices across all mainland States of the National Electricity Market (NEM), attributed to a combination of factors, including increased baseload outages, realignment of gas and coal spot market offers with global prices, and reduced solar generation due to cloud cover. This surge has resulted in record April spot prices and has pushed forward prices for FY-22/23 above $100/MWh, with NSW and QLD nearing $150/MWh. The situation pressures upward revisions of draft Default Market Offers and Victorian Default Offers, with public awareness potentially delayed due to election timing. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability, fuel supply issues (coal, gas). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and potentially extending into the next financial year (FY-22/23). Its root_cause is: Higher baseload outages, spot market repricing by gas and coal sectors, reduced solar generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 04-05-2022. Its affected region is: All mainland States of the NEM, particularly NSW, QLD, VIC, and TAS. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: A combination of operational and market factors. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Increased baseload outages, spot market repricing by gas and coal sectors, reduced solar generation due to cloud cover.

title: Trucking coal from Callide to Millmerran, as a result of the La Nina rain event
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Trucking coal from Callide to Millmerran, as a result of the La Nina rain event" posted at 03-05-2022 06:53:00 PM is: Heavy vehicle traffic has increased around Biloela due to coal being trucked from Callide Mine to Millmerran Power Station following supply shortages caused by recent La Nina-induced weather events in Southern Queensland and Northern New South Wales. The transportation is anticipated for a four-week period starting from 13 April 2022. This coal shortage has led to adjustments in operations at Millmerran, with bidding strategies observed to conserve coal supplies. There are also noted quality issues with coal, indicated by repeated rebidding due to 'Mill/CV*/Feeder limitation'. The handling of bidding and regulatory recommendations, such as times in rebid columns, are also highlighted. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues (coal). Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short term, specifically covering and anticipated four weeks from 13 April 2022. Its root cause is: La Nina rain event causing disruptions. Its accident scale: Unknown. Its dates are: 13 April 2022 - approximately mid-May 2022. Its affected region is: Millmerran, Southern Queensland, and Northern New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Millmerran Power Station. Its cause type is: Natural event leading to coal supply adjustments. Its causes are: Heavy rainfall from La Nina leading to coal supply shortages requiring increased transportation logistics.

title: Price volatility in QLD and NSW on Tuesday 3rd May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility in QLD and NSW on Tuesday 3rd May 2022" posted at 03-05-2022 06:43:00 PM is: The article highlights significant price volatility in Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW) in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on May 3, 2022, with prices reaching up towards the Market Price Cap since the 15:55 dispatch interval. The volatility is linked to a tight supply-demand balance, despite moderate market demand, due to factors like constrained flow on the VIC-NSW interconnector leading to a yellow level alert for Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM). Contributing factors include planned and forced outages of coal units, gas units ramping up, and limited solar generation. The VIC to NSW interconnector was not operational due to specific constraint equations, exacerbating the issue. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, with relevance on the specific day mentioned. Its root_cause is: Tight supply-demand balance worsened by interconnector constraints and generator outages. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-05-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and generators in the affected regions. Its cause type is: Market and operational constraints. Its causes are: Constrained VIC-NSW interconnector, generator outages, weather conditions affecting coal quality, and limited renewable generation.

title: Evening volatility in QLD (and NSW), and a mysterious/significant drop in Available Generation, on Monday 2nd May 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility in QLD (and NSW), and a mysterious/significant drop in Available Generation, on Monday 2nd May 2022" posted at 02-05-2022 07:45:00 PM is: On 2nd May 2022, there was a significant price volatility in the electricity markets of Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW), exacerbated by an unplanned outage of the Ravine-Yass No. 2 330 kV Line, which limited the export capability from Victoria (VIC) to NSW. The market cap price soared to $15,100/MWh in QLD while NSW prices were similarly elevated. Despite modest electricity demand, available generation in QLD surprisingly dropped by 743MW within minutes, reducing the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) to 7%. The cause of this drop remains unclear as no unit trip alerts were triggered, although there were indications of some coal units not being operational. The situation stabilized by 19:05, although the underlying reasons for the generation drop are still unknown. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short term (hours to a day). Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage and unknown drop in available generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 02-05-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD) and New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in QLD and NSW. Its cause type is: Unplanned outage and unknown generation issue. Its causes are: Unplanned outage of Ravine-Yass No. 2 330 kV Line, unknown reduction in generation capacity in QLD.

title: The slow arrival of the Wandoan Battery
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The slow arrival of the Wandoan Battery" posted at 21-04-2022 07:04:00 PM is: The article discusses the delayed operational progress of the Wandoan Battery in Queensland, which, despite being registered in early July 2021, has not moved to full commercial operations. Limited by a charge and discharge capacity of 100MW, the battery showed intermittent activity and apparent data aberrations, raising questions about its operational challenges. Significant spikes in daily average prices, influenced by external factors such as the Callide C4 Catastrophe and regional heatwaves, further complicate the Wandoan Battery's integration into the market. The interest centers around understanding these delays and the absence of expected performance in the NEM. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. title: What Demand-Side Responses can we see in QLD’s heatwave of Tue 1st February and Wed 2nd February 2022?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What Demand-Side Responses can we see in QLD’s heatwave of Tue 1st February and Wed 2nd February 2022?" posted at 18-04-2022 05:41:00 PM is: The article discusses the demand-side responses and market dynamics during Queensland's heatwave on 1st and 2nd February 2022. The period saw high spot prices, the dispatch of the Reserve Trader (RERT), and potential load shedding forecasts due to tight supply-demand conditions exacerbated by outages and low renewable output. Energy users, particularly large ones, were affected, leading to some demand response measures though not widespread negawatt registration in QLD. Despite this, the article notes a visible reduction in energy consumption, particularly in the Surat zone, contributing to managed demand and price stabilization. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term impact. Its root_cause is: Queensland's heatwave and unexpected generator outages. Its accident scale is: Significant impact due to high prices and tight supply but no widespread catastrophic failure. Its dates are: 1st and 2nd February 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Large energy users and possibly residential sectors. Its cause type is: Environmental and operational factors. Its causes are: Heatwave exacerbating electricity demand, generator outages, low renewable energy output, and the implementation of demand response measures.

title: AEMO releases update to 2021 ESOO following earlier closure announcement for Eraring Power Station
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases update to 2021 ESOO following earlier closure announcement for Eraring Power Station" posted at 14-04-2022 03:03:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has updated its 2021 Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO) to reflect recent announcements regarding early closures of coal-fired power stations, particularly the Eraring Power Station slated for as early as 2025. The update incorporates new modeling to assess the impact on reliability forecasts and the integration of new generation and transmission projects. These changes are based on scenarios developed with stakeholders, suggesting that if anticipated investments proceed on schedule, reliability standards will be met for the 2025-26 period. The update underscores ongoing concerns about potential electricity shortages and increased blackout risks, demanding rapid infrastructure developments to ensure stability.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The closure plans are expected to impact the market by 2025-26. Its root_cause is: The announcement of potential early closures of coal-fired power stations, particularly Eraring Power Station. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: The main date is 14-04-2022; Eraring's closure announcement was made on 17-02-2022. Its affected region is: Primarily NSW, with concerns also for Victoria and Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy decision and market reaction. Its causes are: Early closure plans for Eraring and Bayswater Power Stations and the need for new generation and transmission projects to meet reliability standards.

title: Observation 8/22 from GenInsights21–what is the purpose of the ESOO (Electricity Statement of Opportunities)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Observation 8/22 from GenInsights21–what is the purpose of the ESOO (Electricity Statement of Opportunities)?" posted at 12-04-2022 02:33:00 PM is: The article discusses the evolving role of the AEMO's Electricity Statement of Opportunities (ESOO) in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM). Originally, the ESOO served as a reliable source of truth, outlining operational expectations in increasingly complex market conditions. The report now attempts to fulfill three distinct purposes: determining if AEMO should intervene in the market, acting as a statement of commercial opportunities, and analyzing the resilience of the NEM against high-impact, low-probability events. The author suggests that each objective requires its own dedicated report to improve effectiveness and prevent confusion. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: The evolving complexity in the National Electricity Market and its diverse challenges. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-04-2022 02:33:00 PM. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy sector participants, spectators, and commentators. Its cause type is: Market dynamics and policy evolution. Its causes are: Changes in energy market dynamics, policy considerations, and the need for enhanced market resilience reports due to increased complexity.
