title: Ten hours of prices above $100/MWh overnight, across *all* regions
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Ten hours of prices above $100/MWh overnight, across *all* regions" posted at 05-04-2022 07:41:00 AM is: The article reports unusually high electricity prices across all regions in the NEM, sustained over a ten-hour period, starting from the previous evening and carrying through to the early morning. Prices were notably around $300/MWh, whereas historically low prices were expected overnight. Contributing factors include consistently low wind production dropping below 1,000MW overnight. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, potentially affecting market prices for the short term. title: Price Volatility continues in QLD, on Monday 4th April 2022 … and spreads to all regions
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price Volatility continues in QLD, on Monday 4th April 2022 … and spreads to all regions" posted at 04-04-2022 05:54:00 PM is: The electricity prices in Queensland have seen extreme volatility with dispatch prices spiking to $9,500/MWh and earlier to $13,200/MWh on April 4, 2022, due to generator outages. This price instability spreads to all regions with elevated spot prices. The outages include significant coal unit outages at Callide and Gladstone and gas unit outages at Swanbank E and a short one at Darling Downs CCGT, leading to a Lack of Reserve (LOR1) condition forecast in Queensland for the afternoons of April 4 and 5. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, particularly affecting April 4 and 5, 2022. Its root cause is: Generator outages and reserve shortfall. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-04-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia and to an extent, all NEM regions. title: Updated monthly statistics for NEM-wide wind production
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Updated monthly statistics for NEM-wide wind production" posted at 01-04-2022 04:05:00 PM is: The article discusses updates on wind production statistics in the National Electricity Market (NEM) as of April 1, 2022. It highlights an increase in installed wind capacity, with new installations at Murra Warra Stage 2 and Port Augusta Wind Farms pushing the capacity close to 10,000 MW. Although the maximum output level has remained static since July 2021, there has been growth in monthly peak and average outputs from March 2021 to March 2022. The minimum levels have also shown a slight increase due to the growth in installed capacity. Generally, the monthly capacity factor is stabilizing around 30% across the NEM.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. title: 1st April 2022 is the last hurrah for Liddell unit 3
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "1st April 2022 is the last hurrah for Liddell unit 3" posted at 01-04-2022 08:46:00 AM is: The article highlights the closure of Liddell unit 3, the first of the four units at the Liddell power station, marking a significant step in the shift away from coal-fired energy in Australia. AGL Energy, who operates the station, decided in May 2021 to retire the more problematic unit 3, while extending the operation of unit 4 for another year. The closure was registered by AEMO, albeit with minor data issues in some monitoring tools. The event underscores broader changes in energy policy as more coal units prepare to shut down in the coming years. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: Simultaneous trip of Gladstone units 3 and 4 on Thursday 31st March 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Simultaneous trip of Gladstone units 3 and 4 on Thursday 31st March 2022" posted at 31-03-2022 05:00:00 PM is: On Thursday, 31st March 2022, Gladstone Power Station units 3 and 4 tripped simultaneously, impacting the 132kV line connected to the Boyne Smelter. This incident caused a brief period of price volatility in the electricity market, with prices spiking to $15,100/MWh, then dropping to $100.02 and subsequently rising to $1,000/MWh later in the evening. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued Market Notice 95668 regarding this non-credible contingency event but has not identified the root cause at this time. However, no load shedding was instructed, and no bulk electrical load disconnection was reported. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability; Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 31-03-2022 05:00:00 PM. Its affected region is: Queensland region. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Non-credible contingency event. Its causes is: Simultaneous trip of Gladstone units 3 and 4 and Calliope River – Gladstone PS 132 kV feeder 7375.

title: AEMO Gas Statement of Opportunities (GSOO) flags possible short-term tightness, and longer-term challenges
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO Gas Statement of Opportunities (GSOO) flags possible short-term tightness, and longer-term challenges" posted at 29-03-2022 11:42:00 AM is: The AEMO's annual Gas Statement of Opportunities (GSOO) for 2022 highlights the uncertainty and potential challenges facing Australia's gas supply, emphasizing an increase in "peakiness" of gas demand and the need for flexible solutions. The implications include possible gas shortages by winter 2023, as media reports indicate concerns about a supply squeeze in southeastern Australia. These challenges relate to the variability and complexity within the National Electricity Market (NEM) prompted by external factors such as weather conditions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Fuel supply issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term tightness in the coming year, with longer-term challenges anticipated after 2023. Its root_cause is: Increasing demand variability and uncertainty of gas requirements, partly influenced by weather conditions and the transition in energy supply dynamics. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 29-03-2022, with potential impact noted for winter 2023. Its affected region is: Southeast Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market-related. title: The Generator Statistical Digest 2021 (GSD2021) is released
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Generator Statistical Digest 2021 (GSD2021) is released" posted at 28-03-2022 07:07:00 AM is: The Generator Statistical Digest 2021 (GSD2021) has been released, providing an update to previous statistical reports and offering detailed insights into the operation of the electricity market throughout 2021. Produced by a collaboration between Greenview Strategic Consulting and Global-Roam Pty Ltd, the GSD2021 is designed to complement the comprehensive analytics of GenInsights21. The digest features comprehensive data for all DUIDs in the ENERGY market, utilizing a dual-page format for detailed year-over-year and annual review, as well as offering a bound hard copy and electronic PDF formats for users. Clients and interested parties are encouraged to place their orders for access; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. title: Swanbank E out until September 2022 with damaged Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Swanbank E out until September 2022 with damaged Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR)" posted at 26-03-2022 08:20:00 PM is: Swanbank E Power Station is expected to be out of service until September 2022 due to a damaged Automatic Voltage Regulator (AVR), causing delays in power generation in Queensland. CleanCo, the operator of the power station, has begun investigating the cause of this event and is assessing options to replace the AVR. The situation underscores concerns over grid stability, as past outages contributed to significant market volatility. The exact return-to-service date remains uncertain and contingent on infrastructure repairs. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. title: “Rise of the Machines” – the increasing role of auto-bidding and self-forecasting in the modern-day NEM
author: Jonathon Dyson
classified_content: The summary of the news "“Rise of the Machines” – the increasing role of auto-bidding and self-forecasting in the modern-day NEM" posted at 25-03-2022 07:47:00 AM is: The article discusses the growing integration and implications of auto-bidding and self-forecasting technologies in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM). It highlights the benefits of these technologies in optimizing operations and addressing market challenges but also raises concerns about deliberate deviations in self-forecasting to manage the Causer Pays Factors. The importance of human oversight alongside automation is emphasized to ensure compliance and effective market participation.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). title: AEMO published Addendum on the draft 2022 ISP
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO published Addendum on the draft 2022 ISP" posted at 20-03-2022 03:44:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) released a 19-page addendum to the Draft 2022 Integrated System Plan (ISP), addressing concerns highlighted by the Australian Energy Regulator (AER). The addendum covers four key topics: the timing of thermal coal plant retirements, the decision rules for the Victoria-New South Wales Interconnector West and HumeLink, the timing of the Marinus Link, and the application of low gas price sensitivity across scenarios. The addendum clarifies that the assumed rate of coal plant closures relies on an anticipated carbon constraint, acknowledges potential changes impacting interconnector projects, and addresses the AER's queries about scenario assumptions. Submissions regarding this addendum are due by March 28, 2022. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium to long-term. Its root_cause is: AER's Transparency Review. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 20-03-2022. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy regulators and stakeholders in the Australian electricity market. Its cause type is: Policy and regulatory review. Its causes are: Concerns raised by the AER about assumptions and methods in the Draft 2022 ISP.

author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases preliminary report into TAS Market Suspension on 1st March 2022" posted at 20-03-2022 01:15:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a preliminary report on the suspension of the Tasmanian electricity market on 1st March 2022 following the loss of SCADA systems due to damage to Telstra fibre cables linking Tasmania to the mainland. The market was suspended from 12:35 PM to 7:15 PM, though the power system remained secure with no loss of generation or load. The prices were set using market suspension schedule pricing during this period. Further analysis and a final report by AEMO are forthcoming. Its relevance level is: Level 1. This event occurred after Electranet experienced a significant loss of system visibility, with only 20% visibility reported. Despite this, the power system managed to remain secure, and no generation or load was lost. AEMO indicated that further analysis would be conducted, and a comprehensive final report would be delivered subsequently; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). title: AEMO publishes report on RERT contracted on 1st and 2nd February 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO publishes report on RERT contracted on 1st and 2nd February 2022" posted at 20-03-2022 12:49:00 PM is: The article discusses the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) report on the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) contracted for 1st and 2nd February 2022 during a summer heatwave in Queensland. On 1st February 2022, 331MW of RERT was mobilized in Queensland, amounting to 1,448MWh with a total cost nearing $40 million, equating to an average cost of $27,450/MWh, surpassing the current Market Price Cap. Additionally, a minor RERT of 8MW was contracted on 2nd February 2022 in Queensland but was not dispatched. Furthermore, a substantial 256MW was contracted in New South Wales on 1st February 2022, which was also not dispatched. These interventions were a response to anticipated high electricity demand due to the heatwave. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root cause is: Summer heatwave leading to high demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 1st and 2nd February 2022. Its affected region is: Queensland and New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: Anticipated high electricity demand due to a summer heatwave.

title: AEMC Rule Determination on ‘Enhancing operational resilience in relation to indistinct events’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC Rule Determination on ‘Enhancing operational resilience in relation to indistinct events’" posted on 20-03-2022 12:23:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) has made a rule determination to enhance operational resilience against indistinct events such as major storms, flooding, fires, and cyberattacks. The new rule aims to improve the operational tools available to the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) for managing threats to power system security. While some parts of the rule have already commenced, others will take effect in the coming years, reflecting the urgency and complexity involved. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Beginning 9 March 2023, with further phases in 2024. Its root cause is: The need to address increasing threats from indistinct events to power system security. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10 March 2022, 9 March 2023, and 3 June 2024. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (Australia). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market reform/policy adaptation. Its causes are: Growing threat from unpredictable and uncertain events like storms, floods, fires, and cyberattacks.

title: Prices spike in South Australia on Saturday evening, 19th March 2022 (State Election Day)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Prices spike in South Australia on Saturday evening, 19th March 2022 (State Election Day)" posted at 19-03-2022 06:32:00 PM is: The South Australian electricity market experienced price peaks exceeding $9,900/MWh on State Election Day, notably during the 18:15 dispatch interval. Contributing factors included high demand above 2,000 MW, fading solar energy in the evening, and minimal wind power, which led to reliance on gas-fired, liquid-fueled plants, and batteries. High imports from Victoria reached their capacity limit, and while the IRPM was healthy at 36%, insufficient market return likely intensified the price surge. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) forecasted a Low Reserve Condition at the LOR1 level and directed additional plant operations. This spike occurred as vote counting was ongoing in Adelaide, underscoring the significance of energy policy. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, primarily affecting the evening of March 19, 2022. Its root_cause is: High electricity demand and limited renewable energy supply. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-03-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Market dynamics and energy supply constraints. Its causes are: High demand, reduced renewable generation, maximum import capacity from Victoria, and energy plant directives by AEMO.

title: First big price spike in QLD at 18:35 on Wednesday 9th March 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "First big price spike in QLD at 18:35 on Wednesday 9th March 2022" posted at 09-03-2022 07:05:00 PM is: This news highlights a significant price spike in the Queensland (QLD) region, where electricity prices soared to $8,800/MWh during the 18:35 dispatch interval on March 9, 2022. The spike was primarily due to a 92MW increase in market demand at a time when solar and wind generation were minimal, and imports from New South Wales were constrained due to network issues. Additionally, outages at key power stations like Callide, Swanbank, Gladstone, and Mt Stuart contributed to the scarcity of supply. A constraint set relating to an outage on the Lismore SVC further restricted imports, exacerbating the situation.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, during the 18:35 dispatch interval. Its root_cause is: A combination of increased market demand and supply constraints. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 09-03-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Multiple outages and import constraints. Its causes are: 1) Outage at Callide C4 unit, 2) Outage at Swanbank E unit, 3) Non-operation of two units at Gladstone, 4) Non-operation of one unit at Mt Stuart, 5) Low solar and wind production, 6) Limited imports from New South Wales due to constraint equations related to the Lismore SVC outage.

title: Queensland is back for round 2 – Wednesday 9th March 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland is back for round 2 – Wednesday 9th March 2022" posted at 09-03-2022 11:18:00 AM is: The article reports a forecasted electricity reserve shortfall for Queensland on 9th March 2022, following a record electricity demand and pricing surge the previous day. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued several notices indicating a possible Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) condition due to forecast errors and variable weather conditions affecting supply and demand, especially with potential afternoon storms influencing air conditioning load and solar power generation. The predicted electricity demand is expected to strain available generation capacity as solar power decreases at sunset, leading to anticipated high electricity prices in the evening. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term (same day, 9th March 2022). Its root_cause is: Unanticipated high electricity demand coupled with weather uncertainties affecting supply and forecast inaccuracies. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-03-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Queensland electricity consumers, particularly during peak evening hours. Its cause type is: Operational challenges and weather-dependent forecasting difficulties. Its causes are: High actual electricity demand vs. forecast, uncertainties in weather affecting load and solar output, and inadequate generation capacity during peak demand times.

title: Basslink goes offline, again
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Basslink goes offline, again" posted at 09-03-2022 10:41:00 AM is: Basslink, the DC submarine cable interconnector between Tasmania (TAS) and Victoria (VIC), went offline shortly after 7:30 am on March 8, 2022, resulting in significant price differences between the two regions. Despite this, the Tasmanian Minister for Energy and Emissions Reduction, Guy Barnett, assured that there was no threat to the state's energy security due to acceptable storage levels. The cable was expected to resume operations by Friday. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, until the cable is back online. Its root_cause is: A fault in the submarine cable. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-03-2022 (event occurrence date). Its affected region is: Tasmania (TAS) and Victoria (VIC). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: Fault in the Basslink DC submarine cable causing the interconnector to go offline.

title: Queensland scrapes through on Tuesday 8th March 2022 … with only 210MW spare capacity at the lowest point!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland scrapes through on Tuesday 8th March 2022 … with only 210MW spare capacity at the lowest point!" posted at 09-03-2022 12:55:00 AM is: The article discusses the critical situation in Queensland on March 8, 2022, where electricity demand reached near-record levels, resulting in an extremely tight supply-demand balance. The Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) fell to a critically low 2.1% during peak demand times, leading the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) to issue an Actual Lack Of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) alert. Contributing factors included the absence of several power generation units, notably Callide C4, Swanbank E, two Gladstone units, and a Mt Stuart unit. The constraints on the QNI interconnector limited the ability to import electricity from New South Wales, further exacerbating the situation. This event highlights ongoing challenges in managing the balance of supply and demand in the transition to renewable energy sources, especially given the complete unavailability of large solar and wind power during the timeframe of high demand. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term. Its root_cause is: High electricity demand coinciding with limited generation capacity and import constraints. Its accident scale is: Regional (Queensland). Its dates are: 08-03-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: General electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Combination of high demand and generation limitations. Its causes are: Near-record high electricity demand, absence of key power units, interconnector constraints, and lack of renewable energy contribution.

title: An almost three hour long run of extreme prices in QLD on Tuesday evening, 8th March 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "An almost three hour long run of extreme prices in QLD on Tuesday evening, 8th March 2022" posted at 08-03-2022 11:08:00 PM is: The article reports on a prolonged period of extreme electricity prices in Queensland on 8th March 2022, with prices remaining above $1,000/MWh for nearly three hours. This surge followed a new all-time record for electricity demand in Queensland. The price run began at 17:30 at $1,190.63/MWh and peaked at $8,679.33/MWh at 20:15. During this period, prices intermittently reached the Market Price Cap of $15,100/MWh. Consequently, the Cumulative Price for the region climbed to over $564,000, approaching the new Cumulative Price Threshold set post Five Minute Settlement.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: High electricity demand leading to price surges. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-03-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and providers in QLD. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes are: Record electricity demand, prolonged extreme pricing period, market price cap occurrences.

title: New all-time record demand for QLD region on Tuesday 8th March 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New all-time record demand for QLD region on Tuesday 8th March 2022" posted at 08-03-2022 10:38:00 PM is: The Queensland region experienced a new all-time high demand of 10,119MW on March 8th, 2022, surpassing the previous record of 10,052MW set in February 2019. This significant surge was observed during the evening, driven by increased 'Market Demand', which plays a crucial role in electricity price settings and generator dispatching. The substantial demand led to price volatility and a tight supply-demand balance, with the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin dropping to 8% for the region. This event underscores the challenges of meeting high electricity demands, particularly during peak times, and highlights the importance of accurate demand forecasting. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: High electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-03-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Demand-driven. Its causes are: Record-breaking electricity demand, price volatility, tight supply-demand balance.

title: Queensland Market Demand hits 10,000MW (and is rising fast) late Tuesday afternoon, 8th March 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland Market Demand hits 10,000MW (and is rising fast) late Tuesday afternoon, 8th March 2022" posted at 08-03-2022 05:07:00 PM is: On March 8, 2022, the electricity market in Queensland saw demand soar to 10,000MW, nearing the all-time high previously recorded. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a market notice forecasting a Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) condition due to a tight supply-demand balance heightened by high demand and constrained import capabilities, particularly through the QNI interconnector from New South Wales. The demand levels for 17:00 were notably higher than anticipated in earlier forecasts, increased by consistent high temperatures and declining solar output into the evening. The QLD's economic island's reserve margins were critically low, signifying the region's precarious electricity stability; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: High electricity demand amidst limited reserve capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 08-03-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: System-induced. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: High market demand, constrained imports from NSW, low reserve margins, and high temperatures reducing solar output.

title: How rare was this rain and flooding event?
author: Ben Domensino
classified_content: The summary of the news "How rare was this rain and flooding event?" posted at 08-03-2022 01:14:00 PM is: The news article details an extreme rain and flooding event that occurred in eastern Australia during late February and early March 2022. The event was marked by prolonged and intense rainfall resulting in unprecedented flooding across southeast Queensland and northern New South Wales. The historical rainfall and subsequent floods were driven by a persistent atmospheric river and several low-pressure systems, breaking numerous meteorological and hydrological records, and were statistically rare occurrences. As a result, significant flooding affected many regions, setting new benchmarks for flood levels and rainfall. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short- to medium-term impact due to potential infrastructure damage and supply chain disruptions. Its root cause is: Relentless atmospheric river feeding moisture into a slow-moving low-pressure system. Its accident scale is: Widespread, affecting approximately 1000 km of the eastern Australian seaboard. Its dates are: February 22 to early March, 2022. Its affected region is: Southeast Queensland and Northern New South Wales. Its affected key users are: Unknown, but potentially includes residential areas, businesses, and infrastructure services. Its cause type is: Natural causes. Its causes are: Persistent atmospheric river, slow-moving upper-level low pressure system, surface-based low pressure troughs.

title: Early media coverage of GenInsights21
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Early media coverage of GenInsights21" posted at 08-03-2022 01:01:00 PM is: The article discusses the release and media coverage of the GenInsights21 report, published by Global-Roam Pty Ltd and Greenview Strategic Consulting in December 2021. The 622-page report analyzes over two decades of generation data within the National Electricity Market (NEM) and highlights the challenges and complexities associated with Australia's energy transition. Various industry experts and publications have referenced the report, acknowledging its insights into the increasing reliance on forecasting due to variability and uncertainty, and the impact of renewable energy on market dynamics. The GenInsights21 report aims to provide an in-depth understanding of the current energy landscape and potential future challenges.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: The complexities and challenges linked to Australia's transition to cleaner energy sources. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 08-03-2022. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Energy market stakeholders and policymakers. Its cause type is: Report/Analysis release. Its causes is: Examination of historical generation data and forecasting future energy market challenges due to energy transition dynamics.

title: Rooftop PV output during QLD’s heatwave week 2022 – Part B
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Rooftop PV output during QLD’s heatwave week 2022 – Part B" posted at 07-03-2022 10:45:00 AM is: The article provides an in-depth analysis of the impact of clouds on rooftop photovoltaic (PV) output during the heatwave on 1st and 2nd February 2022 in Queensland. It shows that cloud coverage, particularly in postcode regions 40XX, 41XX, 42XX, 43XX, and 45XX, significantly reduced rooftop PV output on both days, with more substantial declines observed on the 2nd due to stratified clouds. The assessment also involves examining the variability in solar farm outputs and individual rooftop PV sites, highlighting the impact of clouds and the 'Russ Christ effect', where PV output declines precede a decrease in temperature. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, particularly on 1st and 2nd February 2022. Its root_cause is: Cloud coverage reducing solar PV output during the heatwave. Its accident scale is: Regional, affecting specific postcode areas in Queensland. Its dates are: 01-02-2022, 02-02-2022, with the main analysis conducted on these dates. Its affected region is: South-east Queensland, particularly postcode regions 40XX, 41XX, 42XX, 43XX, and 45XX. Its affected key users are: Residential and commercial users relying on rooftop PV for electricity supply. Its cause type is: Natural causes - weather-related. Its causes are: High cloud coverage during the heatwave, impacting solar PV output and creating a lag between PV output reduction and temperature drop.

title: AEMO forecasts LOR2 Lack of Reserve level for QLD on Tuesday 8th March 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO forecasts LOR2 Lack of Reserve level for QLD on Tuesday 8th March 2022" posted at 06-03-2022 05:53:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued a forecast for a Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition for the Queensland (QLD) region on 8th March 2022. This condition is expected between 18:30 and 19:00, where the forecast capacity reserve requirement is 668 MW, but only 655 MW will be available. This situation has arisen partially due to decreased solar production and potentially other factors not yet verified. AEMO has issued these forecasts to encourage market responses to address the reserve shortfall, with the possibility of intervention if necessary. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to short-term (on the day of the event). Its root cause is: Low reserve capacity driven by lower solar production. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 06-03-2022 (forecast issued), 08-03-2022 (event expected). Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecast reserve shortfall. Its causes are: Decreased solar production during peak demand; potential additional factors.

title: Southeast Queensland flooding, 2022 – electricity demand observations
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Southeast Queensland flooding, 2022 – electricity demand observations" posted at 04-03-2022 04:10:00 PM is: The extreme weather in southeast Queensland, characterized by record-breaking rainfall and flooding during the week ending on February 27, 2022, led to widespread power outages. Despite these outages, state-level maximum electricity demand in Queensland did not decline significantly. However, notable fluctuations in regional demand, particularly in the Gold Coast and Moreton zones, were observed. These fluctuations included drops and recoveries in demand at various times, likely reflecting the ongoing reconnection of sites and potentially influenced by local weather and operational activities. The reasons for these demand variations remain partly unexplored, inviting further analysis and input from the community. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: February 28, 2022, to March 3, 2022. Its root_cause is: Record-breaking rainfall leading to flooding. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: February 28, 2022, to March 3, 2022. Its affected region is: Southeast Queensland, including Gold Coast and Moreton zones. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Record-breaking rainfall, flooding, power outages, operational fluctuations in demand.

title: Market suspended in Tasmania on March 1st 2022
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Market suspended in Tasmania on March 1st 2022" posted at 01-03-2022 05:29:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) announced a market suspension for the Tasmania region on March 1st, 2022, due to the failure of SCADA systems. This suspension was triggered by the accidental severing of two undersea fibre cables, impacting internet and mobile coverage on the island. The Basslink cable remained operational during this outage. As of 5 PM AEST on the same day, the market suspension had not been lifted, and efforts were underway to restore the cables. Market suspensions in the National Electricity Market (NEM) are rare, with a similar suspension occurring in South Australia two weeks prior for different reasons.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Failure of SCADA systems due to severed undersea fibre cables. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 01-03-2022. Its affected region is: Tasmania, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes is: Severed undersea fibre cables leading to failure of SCADA systems.

title: Updated (and extended) latency statistics for P5MIN file creation at the AEMO
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Updated (and extended) latency statistics for P5MIN file creation at the AEMO" posted at 25-02-2022 03:32:00 PM is: The article highlights ongoing concerns with increased latency in file creation times for P5MIN and DISPATCHIS files at the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO), affecting auto-bidding processes for clients. A noticeable spike in latency was observed on February 20, 2022, followed by a slight improvement later that evening, possibly due to automated or manual adjustments by AEMO. The issue has been persistent since July 2021, with increased client queries related to slower data feeds impacting operations. Historical data comparison indicates that this slow file creation has been the worst since December 2018; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate to ongoing. Its root cause is: Increased latency in file creation times reported at AEMO. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: February 20, 2022 (specific incident mentioned in the article) to February 25, 2022 (article date). Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Clients using auto-bidders reliant on P5MIN data. Its cause type is: Operational and technical delays. Its causes are: Spikes in P5MIN latency, adjustments at AEMO, increased client demand for timely data feeds, historical latency issues since July 2021.

title: Price spike in South Australia on Wednesday 23rd February 2022 – (perhaps) because of sudden drop in output at Bungala Solar Farm?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price spike in South Australia on Wednesday 23rd February 2022 – (perhaps) because of sudden drop in output at Bungala Solar Farm?" posted at 23-02-2022 04:31:00 PM is: On February 23, 2022, South Australia experienced a significant electricity price spike with prices reaching $4,031.78/MWh. This spike was attributed to multiple factors including constraints on the Heywood and Murraylink interconnectors, very low wind output, and a sudden drop in output at Bungala 1 and Bungala 2 Solar Farms. Although wind farms were not constrained, low wind conditions resulted in an aggregate output of only 100MW. The Bungala Solar Farms experienced a reduction of around 50% in output within a 5-minute period preceding the price spike. These events highlight the increasingly vulnerable state of the National Electricity Market (NEM), where any disruption can lead to substantial price volatility.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate impact on February 23, 2022. Its root cause is: Sudden drop in output at Bungala Solar Farms, low wind conditions, and interconnector constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 23-02-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Multiple causes including grid constraints, low renewable output, and sudden generator output drop. Its causes are: Constraints on Heywood and Murraylink interconnectors, very low wind harvest, sudden drop in output at Bungala Solar Farms.

title: Price spike in NSW and QLD on Monday 21st February 2022 following trip of Eraring unit 2
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price spike in NSW and QLD on Monday 21st February 2022 following trip of Eraring unit 2" posted at 21-02-2022 02:49:00 PM is: On February 21, 2022, a trip at Eraring Unit 2 led to a significant price spike in the New South Wales (NSW) electricity market, with prices hitting $15,100/MWh for the 14:05 dispatch interval. This event highlighted the vulnerabilities of the electricity grid during the transition away from coal, as the loss of 649 MW from Eraring necessitated increased output from other generation units like the Tumut units and some coal plants. Additionally, contributing factors included constraint equations that limited output of renewable units and restricted the VIC-NSW interconnector. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate. Its root_cause is: Trip of Eraring Unit 2. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 21-02-2022. Its affected region is: NSW and QLD. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical. Its causes is: Trip of Eraring Unit 2, constraints on renewable units, VIC-NSW interconnector limitations.

title: How are South Australia’s non-scheduled wind farms participating in the market now?
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "How are South Australia's non-scheduled wind farms participating in the market now?" posted at 21-02-2022 12:42:00 PM is: The article discusses the recent changes imposed by the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) on six of South Australia's large, early non-scheduled wind farms, which have now acquired additional dispatch obligations and are participating as semi-scheduled generators in AEMO's market systems. This transition is aimed at enhancing system operation and maintaining power system security. The wind farms, originally non-scheduled, have been categorized under semi-scheduled to ensure their participation in central dispatch, thereby exposing them to system constraints and economic curtailment. Consequently, technical upgrades were undertaken to meet AEMO's requirements, impacting their revenue as they now face both emergency and system-normal constraints. Strategically, their bidding behavior has shifted, with some actively managing offers and bids to mitigate negative price periods. These changes, proposed in response to AER negotiations initiated by the Energy Ministers, reflect a significant regulatory shift to incorporate non-scheduled generators into a more controlled market framework.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Change in market regulations by AEMO. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 21-02-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Regulatory. Its causes are: Imposition of additional dispatch obligations, Transition from non-scheduled to semi-scheduled status.

title: Market Suspension in South Australia on Friday 18th February 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Market Suspension in South Australia on Friday 18th February 2022" posted at 18-02-2022 04:09:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared a suspension of the electricity spot market in South Australia on 18th February 2022 due to a SCADA outage. The market was suspended at 14:25 hrs and resumed at 16:10 hrs the same day. During this period, dispatch prices were subject to manual review for accuracy. The suspension highlights potential complexities and disruptions caused by SCADA outages, as previously seen in January 2021. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, lasting for approximately 1 hour and 45 minutes. Its root cause is: SCADA loss/outage. Its accident scale is: Localized to the South Australian region. Its dates are: 18-02-2022 for both suspension and resumption. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and operators in South Australia. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: SCADA system outage leading to market disruption and manual price review during suspension.

title: Rooftop PV output during QLD’s heatwave week – Part A – Regional overview
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Rooftop PV output during QLD’s heatwave week – Part A – Regional overview" posted at 18-02-2022 03:08:00 PM is: The article details how high electricity demand during a heatwave in Queensland on February 1-2, 2022, was impacted by rooftop PV output. Despite high temperatures and humidity, over 2GW of coal-fired capacity was unavailable, causing electricity supply pressures. Thunderstorms on February 1 caused extensive damage, leading to outages for about 24,000 customers, and reserve contracts were activated to reduce demand. On February 2, PV output declined sharply due to cloud cover, leading to a spike in grid demand. The article highlights how weather conditions and renewable energy sources like PV impact electricity demand and supply dynamics. Its relevance level is Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is February 1-2, 2022. Its root cause is high demand due to extreme weather and reduced coal-fired capacity. Accident scale is extensive as it affected 24,000 customers. Dates are: February 1-2, 2022. Its affected region is Queensland, Australia. Affected key users are residential users. Cause type is natural (extreme weather) and technical (energy supply). Causes are extreme heat pushing demand higher, unavailable coal-fired capacity, thunderstorms causing infrastructure damage, and reduced rooftop PV output due to cloud cover.

title: Some real data for Eraring Power Station (from soon-to-be-released GSD2021)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some real data for Eraring Power Station (from soon-to-be-released GSD2021)" posted at 18-02-2022 01:56:00 PM is: The article discusses the announcement by Origin Energy regarding the potential early closure of Eraring Power Station, a significant event in the Australian National Electricity Market that has generated considerable commentary. The closure, planned potentially around 2025, is influenced by the shifting operational flexibility and performance of Eraring's units over the past few years as detailed in the upcoming Generator Statistical Digest 2021. Origin intends to work with its workforce and community on transitioning plans which could involve re-skilling and employment support. The precise closure timeline remains tentative, underscoring the uncertainty in the broader context of energy transitions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: coal-fired policies. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term (up to 2025). Its root_cause is: Closure announcement due to seeking operational flexibility and changes in market demands. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-02-2022. Its affected region is: Australia, specifically the National Electricity Market. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy and market-driven changes. Its causes are: Early closure decision by Origin, evolving operational and market requirements, and energy transition pressures.

title: NEMDE under strain? Escalation in File Creation times for P5MIN

title: What does the future hold for Wind and Solar in the NEM – seen via GenInsights21?
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "What does the future hold for Wind and Solar in the NEM – seen via GenInsights21?" posted at 11-02-2022 07:02:00 AM is: The article discusses insights from the Generator Insights 2021 report, focusing on the increasing penetration of large-scale wind and solar generation in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Key points include the shift in generation revenues across different times of the day, with solar's midday value decreasing and evening peaks becoming more lucrative for wind farms, highlighting the market's evolving dynamics. It underscores the growing importance of accurate day-ahead and five-minute-ahead wind and solar forecasting due to increasing weather-dependent variability. The article also touches on challenges and strategies towards achieving high levels of renewable penetration, including the roles of self-forecasting and auto-bidding by renewable plants. Additional strategic consideration is given to reaching zero-thermal scenarios and addressing periods of 'wind drought.' Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Initially, prices remained below $382/MWh but spiked to almost $4,000/MWh at 17:30, sustaining over $10,000/MWh until 18:05. Two registered WDR units in Victoria reached their maximum capacity of 10 MW during this period, benefiting from lucrative prices but faced sudden drops after constraints were lifted. The event highlights the unpredictability of demand response opportunities in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. title: A quick look at the 14:30 price spike today (Wed 2nd Feb 2022)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at the 14:30 price spike today (Wed 2nd Feb 2022)" posted at 02-02-2022 04:03:00 PM is: The article discusses a notable electricity price spike in Queensland (QLD), Australia, at 14:30 on February 2, 2022, when prices surged to $9,898.98/MWh. This spike was primarily caused by a rapid increase in market demand, which was unexpectedly 154MW higher than forecasted and grew by 63MW within 5 minutes, as well as a simultaneous reduction in supply due to a 42MW output decrease at the Millmerran units. The supply challenges were accentuated by constraints in the 'central to south' electricity distribution pathways. Other contributing factors possibly include shifts in bidding strategies and changes in imports and exports, though not explicitly analyzed in the report.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its causes are: Unforeseen market demand increases, reduced supply from Millmerran units, potential shifts in bidding, and existing infrastructure constraints.

title: QLD ‘Market Demand’ at 9,869MW at 14:50 … are we going to reach an all-time record?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "QLD ‘Market Demand’ at 9,869MW at 14:50 … are we going to reach an all-time record?" posted at 02-02-2022 03:10:00 PM is: On February 2, 2022, Queensland's electricity demand approached a record high, reaching 9,869MW at 14:50 and hitting 9,924MW at 14:45, just below the all-time maximum of 10,052MW. The demand spike followed a period of extreme heat and faced potential offsets from afternoon storms in South-East Queensland. AEMO declared a Forecast Low Reserve Condition (LOR2) from 16:00 to 19:00, indicating tight supply conditions. The market responded to this scenario with price spikes, reaching $9898.98/MWh, as AEMO warned of reserve shortages and requested market reaction. Storms were anticipated to have conflicting effects—either cooling demand via environmental cooling or increasing demand by reducing solar PV injections. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate, with short-term effects on the same day. Demand peaked at 9,470MW but remained high at 8,060MW by late evening, leading to AEMO's activation of reserve contracts until 21:30. Although the Cumulative Price soared to $911,437, AEMO ceased interventions as reserve margins improved, reducing prices to $109.73/MWh. Looking ahead to February 2, forecasts indicate the demand might reach 10,000MW, with potential price spikes to the Market Price Cap of $15,100/MWh, amid declining generation capacity, prompting AEMO's potential negotiations for additional reserves. author: Paul McArdle

Both wind farms produced modest to very low outputs during peak pricing periods, influenced by the Q^^NIL_CS constraint equation. Coopers Gap, despite a negative factor, should have been 'constrained up' but had modest output, while Mt Emerald was 'constrained down' and bid its volume at the market floor. classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland ‘Q^^NIL_CS’ central-south constraint also a factor, on Tuesday 1st February 2022," posted at 01-02-2022 07:45:00 PM is: The article discusses a constraint equation known as ‘Q^^NIL_CS’ that was active in Queensland, impacting market dynamics by affecting the dispatch of various DUIDs during a summer heatwave. At the time, the QLD and NSW regions faced a very low Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) of 10%, indicating a tight supply-demand balance. AEMO sought market response to mitigate intervention risks while weather forecasts predicted Queensland's demand could exceed 10,000 MW, near record highs. The forecasted decline in available generation exacerbated the potential need for load shedding. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (within a day). Its root_cause is: Extreme weather forecast leading to high electricity demand and generation constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-02-2022. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Weather-induced demand and supply imbalance. Its causes are: Extreme heat forecast, potential high demand exceeding supply, fluctuating load-shedding forecasts, and insufficient available generation.

title: AEMO to commence RERT (i.e. Reserve Trader) negotiations in QLD for Tuesday 1st February 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO to commence RERT (i.e. Reserve Trader) negotiations in QLD for Tuesday 1st February 2022" posted at 31-01-2022 09:03:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has released a market notice indicating their intention to initiate Reserve Trader (RERT) negotiations for Queensland on 1st February 2022. This action is due to anticipated high electricity demand because of extreme heat, which could lead to LOR3 conditions necessitating load shedding. AEMO's approach aims to secure additional power reserves to prevent a situation where load shedding becomes unavoidable. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: 1st February 2022, between 16:00 to 22:00 hrs. title: Price volatility (and low IRPM) on Monday 31st January 2022 – in QLD, VIC, SA  (a first initial review)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility (and low IRPM) on Monday 31st January 2022 – in QLD, VIC, SA (a first initial review)" posted at 31-01-2022 07:15:00 PM is: On January 31, 2022, significant spot price volatility was observed in the Queensland, Victoria, and South Australia regions due to extreme demand and constrained interconnector flows, with prices soaring above $12,000/MWh. The event was driven by a combination of high market demand exceeding 32,000MW, a drop in the Instantaneous Reserve Plant Margin (IRPM) below 15%, and limited gas and hydro capacity in Queensland resulting from the transition from solar to other sources at sunset. Future price fluctuations are anticipated as the supply-demand balance remains tight. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and short term. Root cause is: High demand, low supply reserves, and dependency on fuel mix transition. Accident scale is: Regional impact in QLD, VIC, and SA. Dates is: 31-01-2022. Affected region is: Queensland, Victoria, South Australia. Affected key users is: Regional electricity consumers. Cause type is: System operation and market dynamics. Causes is: High electricity demand, low IRPM, constrained interconnector flows, transition from solar to gas-fired generation.

title: Price volatility in the QLD region on Sunday evening, 30th January 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price volatility in the QLD region on Sunday evening, 30th January 2022" posted at 30-01-2022 06:53:00 PM is: The QLD region experienced significant price volatility on Sunday evening, with electricity prices reaching the market price cap of $15,100/MWh due to a combination of declining market demand, a sudden 60MW spike in demand, constrained imports from NSW, and reduced solar production. title: Forecast ‘Market Demand’ in Queensland on Wednesday 2nd February now up at 9,929MW at 17:30
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast ‘Market Demand’ in Queensland on Wednesday 2nd February now up at 9,929MW at 17:30" posted at 30-01-2022 02:11:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has forecasted a significant increase in market demand for electricity in Queensland, approaching 9,929 MW on Wednesday, 2nd February 2022, driven by a heatwave with temperatures reaching 35°C. title: South Australian price spike on Sunday 30th January 2022 with declining wind, and following (apparent) trips of Bungala 1 and 2 Solar Farms
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "South Australian price spike on Sunday 30th January 2022 with declining wind, and following (apparent) trips of Bungala 1 and 2 Solar Farms" posted at 30-01-2022 01:18:00 PM is: A significant price spike in the South Australian electricity market occurred on January 30, 2022, primarily due to a sudden drop in large solar output from Bungala 1 and 2 Solar Farms. The price spiked to $9,950.27/MWh at 12:35 PM, exacerbated by constrained flows between South Australia and Victoria, creating an 'Economic Island' effect. The trips of the Bungala Solar Farms and declining wind production were notable contributors, although the exact cause of the solar farms' trips was unclear at the time of reporting. The situation underscored the vulnerability of the electricity market to supply disruptions. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (same day). Its root_cause is: Apparent trips of Bungala 1 and 2 Solar Farms. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 30-01-2022. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Market participants in South Australia. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: Sudden trips of solar farms and declining wind production.

title: Forecast tight supply/demand balance in Queensland on Monday 31st January, Tuesday 1st and Wednesday 2nd February 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast tight supply/demand balance in Queensland on Monday 31st January, Tuesday 1st and Wednesday 2nd February 2022" posted at 27-01-2022 04:49:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has issued several market notices forecasting possible LOR3 Low Reserve Condition on 2nd February 2022, indicating a controlled load-shedding scenario due to a tight supply-demand balance in Queensland. The demand is projected to reach 9,505MW, close to the record high, as a heatwave hits the region. The uncertainty in weather forecasts and discrepancies between various forecast runs are contributing to changes in demand predictions. On the supply side, a ramp-up in return-to-service generation might alleviate pressure, but there's uncertainty surrounding the exact timing of this return. AEMO's cautious approach includes sending out generator recall information requests. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 31st January to 2nd February 2022. Its root_cause is: High demand due to a heatwave and potential inadequacies in generation capacity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 27 January 2022 (forecast and notices issued), 31 January 2022 (generator recall information deadline), 1st and 2nd February 2022 (forecasted high-demand period). Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Environmental and operational. Its causes are: Heatwave leading to increased electricity demand, potential inadequacy in generation capacity and delayed return to service of generators.

title: Midday price volatility in South Australia on Monday 10th January 2022
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Midday price volatility in South Australia on Monday 10th January 2022" posted at 10-01-2022 04:44:00 PM is: The article highlights significant price volatility in South Australia's electricity market on January 10, 2022, where prices spiked to $10,247.41/MWh around midday. This volatility occurred despite moderate demand and temperatures and was driven by a rapid change in 'Market Demand' and a decrease in wind farm output. The analysis points to the ongoing challenge of managing electricity supply amidst technology transitions and unpredictable weather conditions, exacerbated by interconnector and market dynamics. AEMO issued high-temperature warnings for South Australia and Victoria, advising market participants to adjust capacities accordingly. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term impact. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Supply and demand imbalance. Its causes are: Decrease in wind farm generation, high demand ramping, and interconnector limits.

title: AER asks AEMO for more details on Assumptions about Coal Unit Closure in Draft 2022 ISP
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AER asks AEMO for more details on Assumptions about Coal Unit Closure in Draft 2022 ISP" posted at 10-01-2022 11:43:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Regulator (AER) has requested the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) to provide more detailed explanations regarding its assumptions in the Draft 2022 Integrated System Plan related to the accelerated closure of coal power plants. The AER has raised concerns that AEMO has not sufficiently detailed how it derived predictions of large financial losses for these plants or the thresholds for determining their retirement. The AER is encouraging AEMO to consider whether the flexibility of these plants could extend their operational lifespan. Meanwhile, the discussion around this topic has gained traction, prompting further debate on the future of coal power in Australia. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Until 2043, according to projections. Its root_cause is: Predictions in AEMO's Draft 2022 ISP about rapid coal plant closures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-01-2022 11:43:00 AM. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Assumptions in energy modeling. Its causes are: Rapid retirement projections of coal plants in AEMO's draft plan, lack of detailed explanations about profit assumptions, and the potential need for flexible operation of coal plants.
